CATNON (EORTC): Concurrent vs. Adj TMZ

Van der Bent et al., Lancet Oncol 2021

2007-2015: 751 patients with 1p/19q non-co-deleted anaplastic gliomas randomized

 RT alone � 59.4 Gy/33 Fx
 RT + concurrent TMZ 75 mg/m2 per day
 RT + adjuvant TMZ 150�200 mg/m2 days 1�5 q4 wks x12 cycles
 RT + concurrent & adjuvant TMZ

2nd interim analysis � declared futility of concurrent TMZ

 Median OS 66.9 mo with concurrent TMZ vs. 60.4 mo without (p=0.76)
 Median OS 82.3 mo with adjuvant TMZ vs. 46.9 mo without (p<0.0001)

 Found that OS benefit was seen in IDH mutant tumors, but NOT in IDH wildtype

Primary toxicity was hematologic in patients receiving TMZ (9% RT alone vs. 15% adj TMZ)

 CATNON: Adjuvant TMZ provides OS benefit for 1p/19q non-codeleted anaplastic gliomas. Benefit is
 specifically seen in IDH mutant tumors. Concurrent TMZ does not provide a benefit, by itself or with

 adjuvant TMZ.

Stupp Trial (EORTC): RT + Conc & Adj TMZ

Stupp et al., NEJM 2005

573 patients with new histologic dx GBM randomized
within 6 weeks of surgery

 RT 60 Gy/30 Fx alone (GTV + 2-3 cm margin)
 RT 60 Gy/30 Fx + concurrent & adjuvant TMZ

 Concurrent: 75 mg/m2 daily with radiation (7 d/wk) --> 4-wk break
 Adjuvant: 150-200 mg/m2 (escalate as tolerated) x5 days (d1-5) q28 days x6c
 Given with PCP ppx (oral pentamidine or TMP-SMX)

Results RT + concurrent and adjuvant TMZ improves OS &
 PFS by ~2 months. Established TMZ as standard of
 Addition of TMZ improved OS & PFS care regardless of MGMT status

 OS improved by 2.5 months (14.6 TMZ vs. 12.1 RT alone, p < 0.001)
 2-year OS 26% TMZ vs. 10% RT alone; 5-year OS 10% vs. 2%
 PFS 6.9 mo TMZ vs. 5.0 mo RT alone, p < 0.001

 7% in TMZ arm had grade 3-4 hematologic toxicity

 Neutropenia & thrombocytopenia

 Unplanned subset analysis

Tumor Treating Fields

Novocure Inc. (OPTUNE)

Antimitotic therapy � disrupts cell division
by interfering with spindle formation

Low-intensity intermediate frequency (200
kHz) alternating electric fields

4 transducer arrays with 9 insulated
electrodes on shaved scalp, connected to
portable device

 Transducer array layouts determined using
 TTFields mapping software (NovoTAL)

 Replace transducer arrays every 2 weeks

Wear for average 18 hours per day
uninterrupted

Only works for supratentorial tumors
Tumor Treating Fields + Maintenance TMZ

Stupp et al., JAMA 2017

2009-2014: 695 patients with supratentorial glioblastoma s/p resection and chemoRT randomized 2:1

 Maintenance temozolomide alone (150-200 mg/m2/day x5 days q28 days for 6 cycles)
 Maintenance TMZ + TTFields (low-intensity 200 kHz frequency alternating electric fields 18 hours/day)

 MRIs performed q2 months & TTFields worn until SECOND radiographic progression or for 2 years

75% wore 18 hours/day, median TTF duration 8 months, 50% continued after 1st progression

TTFields significantly improved OS & PFS when added to maintenance TMZ

 Median PFS 6.7 months with TTFields vs. 4.0 months TMZ alone (p < 0.001)
 Median OS 20.9 months with TTFields vs. 16 months TMZ alone (p < 0.001)
 In post hoc subgroup analysis, TTFields showed benefit across all subgroups regardless of age, sex, KPS, MGMT

 promoter methylation status, geographic region, or extent of resection

No significant difference in grade 3-4 toxicity; TTFields skin redness, itchiness, irritation

 Tumor treating fields (alternating electric fields) added to maintenance TMZ improve PFS & OS!

Does dose escalation help?

Phase II Trial of 18F-DOPA-PET-Guided Dose Escalation (Laack et al., IJROBP 2021)

 Used tumor to normal brain tissue SUV ratio cutoffs to delineate 51, 60, and 76 Gy volumes
 Treated 51-60-76 Gy/30 Fx as a SIB and compared to historical controls
 2014-2018: 85 patients enrolled (DE-Un & DE-Mth vs. HC-Un & HC-Mth)
 Median PFS improved for DE-Un compared to historical controls (median 8.7 vs. 6.6 mo, p=0.017)
 For DE-Mth, PFS not significant but OS was improved (35.5 mo vs. 23.3 mo, p=0.049)

NRG-BN001 � phase II dose intensification (Gondi et al., abstract ASTRO 2020)

 GBM patients postoperatively randomized to 60 Gy/30 Fx vs. 75 Gy/30 Fx using primarily IMRT

 75 Gy arm receives 50 Gy/30 Fx with SIB to 75 Gy/30 Fx
 Proton arm (Group 2) continues to accrue

 IMRT completed accrual (229 patients total for IMRT dose intensification vs. standard dose analysis)
 Dose-intensified IMRT did NOT show PFS (~6.5 mo) or OS (~17 mo) benefit vs. standard dose RT

 NRG-BN001 initial results did NOT show benefit to dose-intensification, but manuscript pending.
 Currently no dose-intensification used outside of clinical trials.

Recurrent GBM
Identifying recurrence

~80% of recurrences occur within 2 cm of primary
Follow-up after completing RT: brain MRI at 2-8 weeks, q2-4 mo x3 years, q3-6 mo indefinitely
Recurrent GBM vs. pseudoprogression on MRI can be very challenging to discern

 Rates of pseudoprogression may be 30-40% after RT

Introduction
What are gliomas?

Gliomas are primary tumors of the CNS

 Most common cause of primary CNS malignancy
 Arise from glial cells (supportive cells of nervous system)

 Astrocytes
 Oligodendrocytes
 Microglial cells

 Typically do not spread outside of the CNS

Unlike most cancers, they do not have a "stage"

 Instead, they have a "grade" on WHO grading system

 Grades I � IV
 Lower grades tend to be less aggressive
 Higher grades tend to be more aggressive
Glioblastoma (GBM)
= WHO Grade IV

Most common primary CNS malignancy in
adults (80%)

 Aggressive, fast-growing tumor
 Poor overall survival (median ~14 months)

Most often, grade IV primary CNS tumors are
GBM
Low-Grade vs. Anaplastic Gliomas

Grade III: Anaplastic Gliomas High-grade gliomas are
 most common primary
 ~25% of high-grade gliomas CNS tumor in adults.
 Majority are anaplastic astrocytomas Most common primary
 Oligodendrogliomas more common in pediatric patients CNS tumor in adults is
 GLIOBLASTOMA (GBM) �
Grade I-II: Low-Grade Gliomas
 Grade IV.
 Uncommon, heterogenous group of primary brain tumors
 More common in younger adults and children
Classification of Gliomas
Classification of gliomas has evolved

Traditionally classification was based on histology alone
2016 WHO update incorporated molecular features (4th edition)
Most recent WHO classification system published in 2021 (5th edition)

Key updates in 2021 WHO classification (5th edition):

 Adult & pediatric-type gliomas are classified separately
 Adult-type diffuse gliomas consolidated based on IDH mutation status

 Glioblastomas are IDH wild-type
 IDH-mutant are either oligodendrogliomas (1p/19q codeletion) or astrocytomas

 Astrocytomas, IDH mutant, grade 4 � these are no longer considered glioblastomas
 Mitotic cell Giant cell (atypia) Histologic

Other Molecular Features

Important updates from WHO 2021 classification:

IDH-wildtype Grade 2 or 3 on histological Dx with One (or more) of the following 3
genetic patterns is considered sufficient to assign WHO Grade IV:

 EGFR amplification
 Combined whole chromosome 7 gain and whole chromosome 10 loss
 TERT promoter mutation

IDH mutant Grade 2-3 Gliomas on histological Dx

 Presence of CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion results in classification of these as WHO Grade IV

 Classified as astrocytoma, IDH-mutant, WHO grade IV
Workup & Management
Workup

Presentation: neurological symptoms (headaches, focal deficits, seizures, confusion, etc.)

Obtain full history & physical with focus on neurological exam

 Fundoscopic exam if suspect increased intracranial pressure

Labs: CBC, CMP

Imaging:

 CT head without contrast to rule out bleeding
 MRI brain with and without gadolinium (including T1c FSPGR & T2 FLAIR sequences)

Other considerations:

 EEG if seizures
 Neurocognitive testing to establish baseline

 � Biopsy alone if not amenable to any degree surgery

Management � GBM: 60 Gy/30 Fx (or 40 Gy/15 Fx or 25 Gy/5 Fx for poor PS)
Overview � Anaplastic gliomas: 59.4 Gy/33 Fx

 Radiation � Low grade gliomas: 54 Gy/30 Fx

 Chemo � GBM: Temozolomide (TMZ) concurrently with RT, then adjuvant x6
 mo + TTF
 � RT alone vs. Chemo alone if very poor PS

 � Anaplastic gliomas: Adjuvant PCV (oligo) or TMZ (astro)
 � Low grade gliomas: PCV vs. TMZ
Maximal Safe Resection

Maximal safe resection with preservation of neurological function
Gross total resection (GTR) is associated with improved functional status & prolonged survival

 At least 95% resection of the cavity as assessed by neurosurgeon & postop imaging

Deep-seated tumors, tumors in eloquent areas, or multifocal tumors not amenable to GTR

 Subtotal resection (STR) or debulking
 Stereotactic biopsy

To evaluate extent of resection, obtain MRI brain with and without gadolinium 24-48 hours
post-operatively (72 hours max)

 Maturation of blood products can lead to enhancement within and bordering the surgical cavity
 This can be confounding and make it difficult to evaluate extent of resection
Radiation

Depending on histology, daily radiation M-F for 5.5-6.5 weeks
Begins ideally ~2 weeks postoperatively, or obtain repeat MRI with gadolinium
Simulation with 3-point thermoplastic mask, fuse both pre and postop MRI to planning CT

 Acute Toxicities Late Toxicities

Fatigue Somnolence syndrome (subacute)
Hair loss (starts to grow back ~3 months) Neurocognitive deficits/short term memory loss
Radiation dermatitis Radiation necrosis
Headache/nausea/vomiting Leukencephalopathy
Exacerbation of presenting neurological deficits Myelopathy
Cytopenias (from TMZ, esp. Thrombocytopenia)
Temozolomide

Temozolomide
 Oral alkylating agent
 Deposits methyl (CH3) groups on DNA guanine bases
 Arrests cell at G2/M phase
 Small and lipophilic, so crosses blood-brain barrier

O-6 Methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase (MGMT) gene
 Encodes a gene that repairs O6-alkylguanine lesions
 (removes the methyl group)
 High MGMT expression results in resistance to TMZ
 Certain tumors have "MGMT methylation" -- MGMT
 promoter is methylated & expression is silenced
 MGMT methylation is a strong positive prognostic factor
Temozolomide

Oral chemotherapy agent

Used for GBM (concurrent & adjuvant) and anaplastic astrocytoma (adjuvant)

Dosing (GBM)

 During radiation: 75 mg/m2/day every day (including weekends)
 After radiation: 150-200 mg/m2/day x5 days, given q28 days x6 cycles

 Start at 150 and dose escalate as tolerate up to 200

Notable adverse effects

 Cytopenias, notably thrombocytopenia
 Give with PCP prophylaxis � oral pentamidine or TMP-SMX
PCV Regimen

Procarbazine Mechanism Dose
Lomustine (CCNU) Alkylating agent
Vincristine Alkylating agent cross-linking 60 mg/m2 PO daily on days 8 through 21 of each cycle
 Antimicrotubule agent 110 mg/m2 PO on day 1 of each cycle
 1.4 mg/m2 [max 2 g] IV on days 8 and 29 of each cycle

Given for 6 cycles adjuvantly after RT
Used in management of IDH mutant 1p/19q codeleted oligodendrogliomas
Thank you!

Shaw et al., JCO 2002; Breen et al., Neuro Oncol 2020

1986-1994: 203 patients with supratentorial LGG randomized

included astro/oligo/mixed

Intergroup long-term results: no significant difference in OS or
PFS with high-dose RT

EORTC 22845: "Non-Believers' Trial"

Van der Bent et al., Lancet Oncol 2005

311 patients with low-grade glioma (grade 1-2) s/p surgery randomized

Patient characteristics

 Astrocytoma (50%), oligodendroglioma (13%), mixed (13%)

Primary endpoints PFS & OS: PFS improved with immediate RT, but OS did NOT

Better 1-year seizure control in RT group

75% in observation arm eventually received RT

Brain Metastases.SRS for Brain Mets:
Stereotactic
Radiosurgery for Brain
Metastases

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Brain Stereotactic
Radiosurgery (SRS)

Precisely focuses photon beams on one or more targets in the brain
 Delivery of much higher doses of radiation compared to conventional EBRT
 while sparing normal brain parenchyma and other organs at risk
 Involves neurosurgeon and radiation oncologist
 Minimally invasive � no actual surgery involved!
 Radiation is delivered in 1 to 5 fractions

Used to treat both benign and malignant entities
 Brain metastases (most common)
 Meningiomas
 Vestibular schwannomas (acoustic neuroma)
 Pituitary adenomas
 Arteriovenous malformations
 Trigeminal Neuralgias
 Among others!

Our focus = brain metastases
Objectives Treatment Planning

Review Simulation
 Target Volumes
 Management of brain mets Prescription dose
 Role of SRS Plan Evaluation
 Dose constraints
History of SRS
 Toxicity & Follow-up
Basic Principles of SRS
 Follow-up MRIs
 Gamma Knife Radiation necrosis grading & management
 LINAC-based SRS

Evidence for SRS

 SRS +/- WBRT
 Multiple brain mets
 SRS +/- surgery
Review from WBRT Talk
WBRT Recap

 Over 50% of brain tumors in adults are due to brain metastases

 Brain mets occur by hematogenous spread, usually located at gray-white matter junction

 Brain mets treatment

 Supportive care first (steroids, antiepileptics)
 Then some combination of surgery, SRS, & WBRT based on prognostication (depends highly on patient's age, performance status,

 & extent of disease)
 RTOG Recursive Partitioning Analysis (RPA): age, performance status, presence of extracranial disease
 Graded Prognostic Assessment (GPA): added number of intracranial metastases

 WBRT is an option, either alone or as an adjunct � local control benefit & delays neurological decline

 WBRT alone if too many or too large volume of metastases, or if presence of leptomeningeal disease
 WBRT as an adjunct to SRS and/or surgery

 The problems with WBRT

 Cognitive decline at 4-6 months, in particular delayed recall (short-term memory)
 30 Gy in 10 Fx is standard treatment � daily treatment M-F for 2 weeks
Treatment Surgery
options for
brain � Large tumor with significant edema & mass effect
metastases � Uncertainty in histological diagnosis

 Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS)

 � Pre- or post-operative SRS to improve local control
 � SRS alone for small tumors or multiple tumors not

 surgically accessible (e.g., eloquent areas)

 Whole brain radiation therapy (WBRT)

 � Multiple tumors (e.g., >10) or high volume of disease
 � Leptomeningeal disease
 � Prophylaxis of brain metastases in SCLC

 Or some combination of these
Role of SRS in Brain Metastases

Pre- or post-operatively

 Helps increase local control

Patients with expected life expectancy >4-6 months with reasonable number, size, and total
volume of brain metastases

 Helps avoid or delay toxicity of WBRT

 Although WBRT is superior for local control, SRS is less toxic and can be repeated

 For treatment to be delivered safely, need reasonable number, size, and total volume of brain
 metastases

 Number: Typically <15-20 but institution-dependent
 Size: Typically <2-2.5 cm diameter for single fraction, <5 cm diameter for 3 fractions
 Total volume: depends on size & number, but need to safely meet whole brain constraints

 Single fraction: V12 Gy < 5 cc (10% risk of symptomatic radionecrosis), 10% (15% risk)
 3-fraction: V20 Gy < 20 cc (10% risk of necrosis or edema)
 5-fraction: V24 Gy < 20 cc (10% risk of necrosis or edema)
History of SRS
History of SRS

1908: Horsley (neurosurgeon) & Clarke (mathematician)

 Devised an apparatus that enabled insertion of a needle electrode and
 electrocoagulation of an intracranial target

 Used on monkey model, never applied to humans

 1947: Spiegel (neurologist) & Wycis (neurosurgeon)

 Stereoencephalotomy using similar stereotactic device for human
 application

 Used to section extrapyramidal system for treatment of Parkinson's disease
History of SRS

1951: Lars Leskell (neurosurgeon) described concept of
stereotactic radiosurgery (Sweden)

 Collimated gamma ray beam that could move along a semicircular
 arch of stereotactic apparatus to strike an intracranial target

 1968: first gamma unit installed in Stockholm, Sweden

 179 sources of cobalt-60 distributed with collimators to create a sharply
 circumscribed disc-shaped lesion

 Intended for use in functional neurosurgery

 Updated version designed for SRS to produce a more spherical radiation field
 Plain film radiographs and air encephalography used to identify the target
 Used to treat AVMs and benign tumors, including vestibular schwannomas
History of SRS

1987: Lunsford (neurosurgeon) & Flickinger (radiation
oncologist) lead installation of the first Gamma Knife Unit in
the USA (University of Pittsburgh, PA)

 Sophisticated treatment planning software
 Automated positioning system (APS) for more conformal dose

 plans and less radiation exposure to personnel

 1983: first report of LINAC-based SRS technique

 1997: Adler (neurosurgeon) introduced the Cyberknife
 (Stanford University, CA)

 Frameless stereotactic radiotherapy system that mounts
 lightweight 6mV linear accelerator on highly robotic arm
Basic Principles of SRS
Treatment Delivery

SRS delivers high dose per fraction compared
to conventional RT

 Generally stereotactic RT (SRS or SBRT) is defined
 as 1-5 fractions

 Examples of SRS doses for brain metastases

 Single fraction: 15-24 Gy/1 Fx
 3 fractions: 21-30 Gy/3 Fx

Techniques for delivering brain SRS

 Gamma Knife (GK)
 Linear accelerator (LINAC)-based SRS

 Cyberknife, Truebeam
Gamma Knife

192 cobalt sources divided into 8 sectors

 24 cobalt sources per sector

 Each sector has 3 options for collimator size &
 option to block completely

 4 mm, 8 mm, 16 mm openings

 Can place one more "shots" within the tumor and
 adjust collimators for each of the 8 sectors to
 shape isodose lines along the tumor

 Dose is generally prescribed to the 50% isodose
 line in GK
LINAC-based SRS

Linear accelerator

 Electrons accelerated to a tungsten target
 Produces X-rays (photons) used to deliver treatment
 Convergent beams or arcs used to target a well-defined lesion
 Steep dose gradients at the margin of tumor and normal tissue

 Ensures low dose outside the target

Techniques of LINAC-based SRS

 Static IMRT � multiple static intensity-modulated beams
 VMAT � one or more conformal arcs, constantly modulated

recurrence <4 cm after prior brain RT (30-60 Gy)

Patients received single fraction SRS

 Dose dependent on size of the tumor

 Aimed to keep rate of G3+ toxicity <20%

Takeaways from
the Evidence Compared to either modality alone

 SRS alone/peri-op worse local control, but improved
 cognitive outcomes at 4 months

 Compared to WBRT
 Favor SRS over WBRT in patients with good PS and expected survival >4

 months

 Volume occupied by brain metastases is more important than
 number of brain metastases for OS & toxicity

 HYTEC data shows that for lesions 2 cm, doses ~18 Gy offer >85%
 one-year local control

 Larger lesions >2 cm, consider fractionated SRS

 SRS can be used alone, with surgery (preop or postop), and/or
 with WBRT

 Patients need multidisciplinary consultation, with treatment approach tailored
 to their clinical needs

Serial Brain MRIs

Follow-up patients after SRS to brain mets with serial brain MRIs q2-3 months

 Can space out (e.g., q4 months, then q6 months) after 2 years

Feared toxicity = radiation necrosis

 Vascular injury: free radicals inflammation and production of VEGF vessel narrowing and fibrinoid
 necrosis of small vessels ischemia & cell death

 Glial cell theory: hypoxia caused by endothelial cell damage liberation of hypoxia inducible factor 1-
 alpha & VEGF neoangiogenesis, but leaky capillaries edema, contrast extravasation

Some risk factors for radiation necrosis

 Dose-volume (& PTV margin) fractionate large tumors
 Prior radiation exposure
 Concurrent systemic therapy

Kano et al., NSGY 2010

Tools to help differentiate radiation necrosis from tumor recurrence/progression:

 T1/T2 matched changes

 Rapid time to imaging progression (<3 months)

 Serial MRIs

radiation necrosis tumor

(WBRT)

Our focus = brain metastases

Brain Metastases
Brain metastases

Most common intracranial tumor in adults

 Account for >50% brain tumors
 20-40% of all cancer patients will develop intracranial

 metastases

 Increasing incidence due to better detection (MRI) & improved cancer
 therapies (better survival)

 80% of patients have multiple brain metastases

What are the most common primary tumors
responsible for brain metastases?

 Adults � Lung, Breast, Kidney, Colorectal, Melanoma
 Children � Sarcomas, Neuroblastoma, Germ cell tumors
Pathogenesis

Most common mechanism of metastasis to the
brain is via hematogenous spread

 Metastases usually located at the gray-white matter
 junction

 Blood vessels decrease in diameter and trap clumps of tumor
 cells

 Also more common in the terminal "watershed areas" of arterial
 circulation

Distribution of metastases roughly follows relative
weight & blood flow in each area

 80% cerebral hemispheres
 15% cerebellum
 5% brainstem
Presentation

Symptoms caused by gradually expanding tumor
mass & associated edema

 Headache � 40-50%
 Focal neurologic dysfunction � 20-40% (e.g.,

 hemiparesis)
 Cognitive dysfunction � 30-50% (e.g., mood or

 personality changes)
 Seizures � 10-20%
 Stroke � 5-10%

Less common

 Intratumoral hemorrhage
 Obstructive hydrocephalus
 Tumor embolization
Diagnosis

Full history and physical with focus on neurologic exam

Imaging

 CT head without contrast � rule out brain bleed
 MRI with & without contrast � gold standard

 T1 pre-contrast: iso to hypointense (may have intrinsic high signal if hemorrhagic)
 T1 post-contrast (T1c): uniform, punctate, or ring-enhancing, but usually hyperintense
 T2 & T2 FLAIR: typically hyperintense � highlights peritumoral edema

Pathology

 Always need a tissue-diagnosis of cancer

 Example 1: known history of metastatic lung cancer with new-onset seizure
 Imaging confirms brain metastasis

 Example 2: no history of malignancy with new onset hemiplegia and word-finding difficulty
 CT CAP, brain imaging, pursue biopsy of another metastatic site vs. maximal safe resection of brain tumor
Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis

Cancer invades the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) &
may attach to meninges

Presenting symptoms

 Pain, seizures, nausea/vomiting,
 lightheadedness, weakness, ataxia, incontinence,
 sensory abnormalities, cranial nerve deficits

Diagnosis

 MRI � nodular meningeal tumor, meningeal
 thickening, strong contrast enhancement

 CSF � cytology; increased opening pressure,
 leukocytes, elevated protein
What are treatment
options for brain mets?
Initial management

Supportive care

 Anti-seizure medication, pain medication, etc.
 Steroids (dexamethasone) shown to improve symptoms in 75% of patients within 3 days

 Can prescribe IV 10 mg loading dose then 4 mg q6h and initiate taper after tumor-directed treatment is initiated
 With steroids, always give PPI for GI ppx & manage hyperglycemia, counsel on side effects of steroids
Treatment Surgery
options for
brain � Large tumor with significant edema & mass effect
metastases � Uncertainty in histological diagnosis

 Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS)

 � Pre- or post-operative SRS to improve local control
 � SRS alone for small tumors or multiple tumors not

 surgically accessible (e.g., eloquent areas)

 Whole brain radiation therapy (WBRT)

 � Multiple tumors (e.g., >10) or high volume of disease
 � Leptomeningeal disease

 Or some combination of these
How do we decide on a
treatment?
Tools for
Prognostication

 RTOG Recursive Partitioning Analysis (RPA) � 1997
 Graded Prognostic Assessment (GPA) � 2008
 Diagnosis Specific GPA � 2020

Need to know patient's performance status

 Karnofsky � scale 0-100%
 ECOG � scale 0-4

 Remember: ECOG 2 = KPS 70%, where patient is able to care
 for self and perform ADLs, but cannot work
RTOG Recursive Partitioning Analysis
(RPA) � 1997

RPA Class Description Median Survival
I 7.1 months
 KPS 70 & Age <65
II Controlled primary, no extracranial metastases 4.2 months

III KPS 70 & Age >65 or 2.3 months
 Uncontrolled primary, presence of extracranial metastases

 KPS <70

 What 3 factors predicted survival in RTOG RPA classification for
 patients with brain metastases?

 1. KPS
 2. Age
 3. Extent of primary disease
Graded Prognostic Assessment (GPA) �
2008

Score 0 0.5 1
Age >60 50-59 <50
KPS <70 70-80 90-100
Number of CNS mets >3 2-3 1
Extracranial mets Present -- None

Score Median survival How did GPA differ from RPA in prognostication
3.5-4 11 months of brain metastases?
3 6.9 months
1.5-2.5 3.8 months Added number of CNS metastases (in
0-1 2.6 months addition to age, KPS, extracranial disease)
Diagnosis Specific GPA
(2020)

Multivariate analysis of multi-institutional database of 6,984
patients newly diagnosed brain metastases

 Patients treated between 2006-2018
 Excludes recurrent brain metastasis and/or leptomeningeal

 disease

www.brainmetgpa.com

 Updated disease-specific prognostic tool to estimate survival,
 individualize treatment, and stratify clinical trials
Evidence for WBRT
Role of WBRT in Brain Metastasis

WBRT alone

 Patients who are not eligible for SRS or surgery

 Either due to poor performance status, or metastases that are too many/too large in volume

 Leptomeningeal disease

WBRT as an adjunct

 After surgery or SRS for brain metastases
Radiation Therapy for Brain Metastases �
Cairncross et al., MSKCC, 1980

183 patients treated with steroids Most patients with brain metastases benefit from RT
+ WBRT 1977-1978 Systemic disease, not CNS disease, limits length of life

 See image for tx details

74% had neurological
improvement

 20% no change
 6% deterioration

Of those that improved

 33% relapsed � 33% of these
 improved with additional steroids
 +/- RT

 60% of deaths were due to
 advancing systemic disease in s/o
 stable/improved neurological
 function
Patchell I � Surgery added
to WBRT, 1990

48 patients with single brain metastasis randomized 1:1

 Surgical resection of brain tumor WBRT (36 Gy/12 Fx)
 Needle biopsy WBRT (36 Gy/12 Fx)

Results � improved outcomes with surgery

 Significant improvement in rate of recurrence (20% vs. 52%)
 Significant improvement in overall survival (40 weeks vs. 15

 weeks)
 Significant improvement in maintenance of KPS (38 weeks vs. 8

 weeks)

 Surgery + WBRT for solitary metastasis
 improves OS, reduces LR, improves QoL/PS
Patchell II � Postop WBRT
added to surgery, 1998

97 patients with single brain metastasis s/p surgical resection randomized

 Postoperative WBRT (50.4 Gy/28 Fx prescribed to cranial midline)
 No further treatment

 Steroids tapered in both groups after completing therapy

Results � WBRT reduced recurrence but not OS

 Postop RT significantly prevented recurrence

 At site of original metastasis (10% vs. 46%) (SS)
 At all other sites of the brain (14% vs. 37%) (SS)

 WBRT group less likely to die of neurologic causes (14% vs. 44%) (SS)
 No significant difference in overall survival or time functionally dependent

 48 weeks WBRT vs. 43 weeks observation

 Postop WBRT improves LC and neurologic
 death, but not OS
QUARTZ Study � WBRT vs. Best
Supportive Care, Lancet 2016

Quality of Life after Treatment for Brain Metastases (QUARTZ) study

 Non-inferiority phase 3 RCT in UK and Australia
 538 NSCLC patients with 1 or more brain mets unsuitable for surgery or SRS
 1:1 randomization

 Dexamethasone + WBRT (20 Gy/5 Fx), or
 Supportive care alone (included dexamthasone)

Results

 Median age 66 years, 25% 5 mets, 38% KPS <70, balanced groups
 56% RPA class II, 37% RPA class III
 No significant difference in overall survival or QALY between groups

 Median OS ~2 months
 Subset analysis � OS benefit for 5 mets, but not powered

 Data supports hospice if limited life expectancy
WBRT Dose Trials

RTOG 6901 and RTOG 7361 established 30 Gy/10 Fx as most common treatment regimen

 Other common regimens � 20 Gy/5 Fx; as effective in palliation as longer schedules

RTOG 9104 compared 30 Gy/10 Fx over 10 days vs. accelerated hyperfractionation (AH)

 AH arm = 32 Gy/20 Fx followed by boost to total 54. 4 Gy/34 Fx BID over 17 days
 No survival difference or change in toxicities (AH had 1 G4 ototoxicity, & 1 G5 cerebral edema)

 30 Gy/10 Fx is the standard WBRT dose
Concerns with WBRT
Toxicities of WBRT

 Acute Late

� Fatigue � Neurocognitive deficits
� Alopecia, scalp irritation � Short-term memory loss
� Ototoxicity (otitis, pressure-like sensation)
� Nausea/vomiting � Leukoencephalopathy & brain atrophy
� Headaches � Neurocognitive deterioration, dementia
� Dry eye, vision changes
� Xerostomia � Radiation necrosis
� Worsening cerebral edema exacerbates � Higher risk with >3 Gy/Fx

 existing neuro deficits � Cataracts

Neurocognitive function is affected at 4-6 months
If patients are expected to live this long, we try to spare neurocognitive function
� Avoid WBRT in favor of surgery/SRS
� If they cannot get surgery/SRS and avoid WBRT, then WBRT with hippocampal avoidance +/- memantine
RTOG 0614 � Memantine + WBRT, 2013

Memantine = NMDA receptor antagonist Memantine titration:
 Excessive NMDA stimulation excitotoxicity � Week 1: 5 mg daily (morning)
 Memantine neuroprotective in preclinical models (vascular dementia) � Week 2: 5 mg bid (morning & evening)
 � Week 3: 10 mg morning + 5 mg evening
RTOG 0614 randomized patients undergoing WBRT (37.5 Gy/15 Fx) to � Week 4: 10 mg bid (morning & evening)
memantine vs. placebo
 Note: stop at 5 mg BID if Cr Cl <30 mL/min (hold
 Memantine dose regimen � titrate up to 10 mg bid & continue x24 memantine if <5 mL/min)
 weeks

Results � less decline in delayed recall with memantine, but did not reach
significance

 Primary endpoint was delayed recall (not significant)
 Memantine arm had significantly longer time to cognitive decline
 Memantine arm superior for executive function at 8 and 16 weeks,

 processing speed at 24 weeks, and delayed recognition at 24 weeks
 No difference in PFS & OS
 No increase in toxicity related to memantine
RTOG 0933 � WBRT + HA, 2014

Phase II multi-institutional trial of hippocampal avoidance (HA-WBRT) for brain metastases

Included 113 patients with brain metastasis >5 mm from hippocampus

IMRT planning to 30 Gy/10 Fx with the following hippocampal dose constraints:

 D100% < 9 Gy
 Dmax < 16 Gy
 Mean < 10 Gy

Results

 Mean relative decline in delayed recall at 4 months was 7% (vs. 30% historical control, SS)

 Age 60, neurologic sx at baseline, higher D100% associated with decline in delayed recall

 Median survival 6.8 months, median PFS 5.9 months (3/67 patients who progressed had progression in
 hippocampal avoidance area)
NRG CC001 � HA-WBRT +
Memantine, 2020

Multi-center phase III RCT of 518 patients

 HA-WBRT + Memantine, vs.
 WBRT + Memantine

 Both groups received 30 Gy/10 Fx
 Memantine dose as per RTOG 0614

Risk of cognitive failure significantly lower in HA-WBRT +
memantine

 No difference in cognitive failure at 2 months
 HA-WBRT less likely to have deterioration in executive function at 4

 months, and total recall & delayed recognition at 6 months

 HA-WBRT + Memantine improves cognitive
 outcomes at 4-6 months
WBRT Technique &
Hippocampal Avoidance
Traditional "German Helmet Field"

Simulation: supine, head in neutral position, 3-point
thermoplastic mask

Fields: portal films with gantry at 90 & 270 degrees

 Collimator rotated to allow inferior border to parallel the
 base of skull

 Field borders should go beyond skull by 2 cm anteriorly,
 superiorly, and posteriorly

 Inferior border from bony canthus to C1-C2 intervertebral
 space; should cover base of skull with 1 cm margin

Technique: two parallel opposed lateral fields

 Shield lens and upper airway
 Light field � lateral canthus to ear lobe
 Energy: 4-6 MV
CT-based
planning

Prescribe treatment to a volume
(whole brain)

 Opposed lateral fields but may add
 wedges or field-in-field to improve
 homogeneity

 Can use MLCs to spare scalp, better
 shield lenses and facial structures
HA-WBRT with IMRT Planning

Hippocampal Avoidance Hippocampal 1)
 Tail
 We don't contour the entire hippocampus
 Focus on the subgranular zone 2) Body
 3) Head
 This is where adult neurogenesis occurs
 Red: Hippocampus
Imaging Green: Hippocampal Avoidance Zone

 T1-weighted MRI

Contouring the hippocampus

 Hypointense signal (dark) medial to temporal horn
 Start inferiorly (hypointense grey matter medial to CSF

 intensity)
 More superiorly, quadrigeminal cistern is a good medial

 landmark
 Superiorly, end near splenium of corpus callosum, when T1

 hypointense signal no longer visible

Add a 5 mm margin

 This is the hippocampal avoidance zone
Temporal Horn
 Quadrageminal cistern

 Splenium
WBRT Plan Evaluation

Coverage

 Look for 95% of brain to receive 95% of dose (V95 = 95%)
 Pay attention to hot spots � aim for <110% hot spot (Dmax <110%)

Dose constraints

 Lens: Dmax < 6-8 Gy
 Parotids: V20 Gy < 47%
 Hippocampus: D100% < 9 Gy, Dmax < 16 Gy, Mean < 10 Gy

 NRG-CC001 dose constraints for HA-WBRT
 Whole brain: D2% 37.5 Gy, D98% 25 Gy, V30 Gy 95%
 Optic nerves & chiasm: Dmax 30 Gy
Conclusion

 Over 50% of brain tumors in adults are due to brain metastases

 Brain mets occur by hematogenous spread, usually located at gray-white matter junction

 Brain mets treatment

 Supportive care first (steroids, antiepileptics)
 Then some combination of surgery, SRS, & WBRT based on prognostication (depends highly on patient's age, performance status,

 & extent of disease)

 Patchell trials showed that surgery added to WBRT improves OS, but WBRT added to surgery improves
 only local control and neurologic death

 Most common WBRT regimen = 30 Gy/10 Fx
 Quartz trial (metastatic NSCLC) supports hospice with best supportive care if limited life expectancy
 Can improve neurologic outcomes (e.g., delayed recall) with hippocampal avoidance and/or memantine

Breast Cancer Overview

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
 Distribution by subtype

Breast Cancer

Most common malignancy among
females in the US

 Other than non-melanoma skin
 cancer

 1 in 8 lifetime risk

2nd most common cause of cancer
death in females

 1st is lung cancer

Classified based on hormone
expression (HR+/-) and HER2
oncogene expression (HER2+/-)
Objectives Staging

Presentation & Workup TNM staging
 Group staging
 Clinical presentation

 Breast anatomy

 Systemic Therapy
Breast Imaging

 MRI Immunotherapy

Pathology

 Techniques
 Morphology
 Pathology report
Presentation & Workup
Clinical Presentation

~90% detected on screening mammogram Group Screening Guideline for Average Risk

~10% detected via self or clinical breast exam American Cancer 40-44: option to start mammograms
 Society (ACS) 45-54: annual screening mammogram
 Usually painless mass 55: biennial screening mammogram
 Sometimes present with pain
 Nipple discharge (though usually this is benign) United States 40-74: biennial screening mammogram
 Nipple retraction Preventative 75: no screening recommended
 Axillary lymphadenopathy with occult primary Services Task
 Force (USPTF)

Differential diagnosis: Fibroadenoma, cysts, infection, Mondor's

cord (thrombophlebitis), intraductal papilloma (bloody discharge),
sclerosing adenosis, lactocele
Risk Factors Lifestyle/exposure

Estrogen exposure High-fat diet
 Postmenopausal obesity
 Female gender Sedentary lifestyle
 Older age
 Early menarche Genetic predisposition Risk/Association
 Nulliparity BRCA1 mutation (AD, 17q21) 30-50% (more likely TNBC)
 Older age at first birth (>30 years) BRCA2 mutation (AD, 13q12) 50-60%
 Lack of breastfeeding Li Fraumenti (p53, 17p) Sarcoma, leukemia, brain,
 Late menopause (>55 years) adrenocortical carcinoma
 Hormone replacement therapy Cowden (AD, PTEN, 10q23) Hamartomas
 Ataxia-telangiectasia (AR, ATM, 11q22)
Family history Peutz-Jeghers

Personal history of breast cancer

 Prior breast cancer
 DCIS, LCIS
 Atypical ductal hyperplasia
 Dense breast tissue
 History of RT during youth
Breast Anatomy

Breast is divided into 4 quadrants:

 Upper outer quadrant (UOQ): most volume, most common location for cancer
 Lower inner quadrant (LIQ): least common location for cancer

Tumor position indicated by "o'clock" position

 Clock superimposed on breast in anatomical position

 12 12

9 3 9 3

 6 6
Lymphatic Drainage

Primarily to axilla (levels I-III)
Medial tumors (esp. LIQ) may drain to internal mammary nodes

Supraclavicular nodes Axillary level I Inf: pec major inserts on 4th rib
 Sup: axillary vessels exit lateral to pec minor
 Axillary level II
 Posterior to pec major where ax vessels cross
 Axillary level III (Rotter's nodes: space b/w pec muscles)
 (Infraclav)
 Supraclavicular Inf: axillary vessels medial to pec minor
 Sup: pec minor inserts on coracoid process
 Internal mammary
 nodes Bottom of clavicular head to cricoid
 Between SCM and scalene muscles

 First 3 intercostal spaces lateral to sternum
 (follow internal mammary artery)
Workup

Full H&P with breast and lymph node exam (axilla & SCV)

Imaging

 Mammography
 Ultrasound
 MRI if indicated

Pathology

 Core biopsy is strongly preferred

 Needle aspirate cannot differentiate ductal carcinoma in situ vs. invasive ductal carcinoma

Additional workup (clinical stage IIIA and higher � T3N1, T4, or N2 � need systemic staging)

 CT chest abdomen pelvis
 Bone scan or PET CT
 Consider brain MRI
 Echocardiogram if considering anthracycline-based chemotherapy
Breast Imaging
Mammography

Breast placed on a flat support plate &
compressed with a parallel plate called a
paddle

Low-dose X-rays pass through the breast to
a detector located on the opposite side

Detectors

 Photographic film plate: captures the x-ray
 image on film

 Solid-state detector: transmits electronic
 signals to a computer to form a digital
 images
Screening Mammogram

Obtained in asymptomatic
patients

Craniocaudal (CC) &
mediolateral (MLO)
projections of the breast

 CC (outer vs. inner)
 MLO (upper vs. lower)

Screening centers do not
necessitate a physician on
site
Diagnostic Mammogram

Obtained in symptomatic patients (palpable
mass or abnormal finding on screening
mammogram)

Additional views to further assess
abnormalities

 Spot compression differentiate suspicious
 masses vs. dense breast tissue

 Magnification views to evaluate calcifications

Radiologist is on site & involved in
procedure
 Example: 10 mm

R L R L spiculated mass
 in left upper-

 outer quadrant
BI-RADS Assessment

Breast Imaging Reporting & Data System

 BI-RADS 4 = consider biopsy
 BI-RADS 5 = require biopsy
 BI-RADS 6 = biopsy proven
Breast & Axillary Ultrasound

Ultrasound is NOT routinely used for breast
screening

 May have a role in screening for women with
 dense breast tissue

Typically utilized to clarify abnormalities seen
on mammography
Can be used to clinically evaluate for suspicious
axillary nodes

 Example from Radiopaedia.org � BIRADS-IV breast lesion
Breast MRI

Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE)-MRI

 Information about the morphology and function of a
 lesion

 High sensitivity but moderate specificity (72%)

 Higher rate of false positivity

As a result, often use multiparametric imaging

Features on MRI that are suspicious for malignancy

 Masslike enhancement

 Spiculated margins
 Irregular shape

 Non-masslike

 Calcifications

MRI may be used for staging & evaluation of
response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy

 Can see & stage the axilla as well

 Example from Radiopaedia.org � left breast invasive ductal
 carcinoma (IDC)
Pathology
Core-needle biopsy is preferred

Core-needle biopsy is least invasive and samples sufficient tissue to make a diagnosis
Alternatives

 Fine needle aspiration � often does not obtain sufficient tissue & need CNB regardless

 If patient is on anticoagulation and cannot hold prior to biopsy, FNA may be mandated

 Excisional biopsy � used if CNB is not feasible, or if CNB is indeterminate

Clips can be placed at the time of biopsy to mark where tissue was sampled

 These are helpful to surgeons & located intraoperatively to confirm the correct area was removed
 If benign finding, these are helpful to future radiologists to mark sites that have been biopsied

 previously
Biopsy techniques

Ultrasound-guided

 Often for palpable masses
 Preferred modality if visible on ultrasound (easiest to schedule)

Stereotactic biopsy or needle localization

 Nonpalpable lesion with suspicious calcifications

MRI-guided biopsy

 Only visible on MRI

Punch biopsy

 Paget's disease or suspicious of dermal involvement (e.g. suspected inflammatory breast cancer)
80% of invasive breast cancers are
invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC)
5-10% of invasive breast cancers are
invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC)
Carcinoma in situ: DCIS vs. LCIS

DCIS = ductal carcinoma in situ

 Considered stage 0 breast cancer (pTis)
 Less common than invasive ductal carcinoma
 If treated with breast conserving surgery, usually requires adjuvant radiation

 Up to 30% risk of recurrence � 50% of the time, recurs as an invasive carcinoma

LCIS = lobular carcinoma in situ

 NOT considered malignancy
 Exception is pleomorphic LCIS, which is thought to behave like high-grade DCIS

 Treat like high-grade DCIS
Rarer Subtypes of Breast Cancer

Subtypes Description (need >90% predominant pattern)
Tubular Small, well-differentiated variant of IDC, >75% tubules, usually ER+/PR+
Medullary a/w BRCA1, younger age (<50), large & hyperplastic LNs, mostly triple negative
Mucinous/colloid Older patients, favorable
Papillary Older patients, often multifocal/diffuse
Cribriform ER+/PR+

Most common type of breast cancer is invasive mammary carcinoma, not otherwise specified
� This is a pure invasive ductal carcinoma
Molecular Subtypes

Luminal A ER/PR Her2/neu Ki67 Prevalence
Luminal B Positive Negative <14% 70%
Her2-enriched Positive +/- 14% 10%
 Negative Overexpressed or 10%
Basal-like amplified
 Negative Negative 10%

Luminal A has best prognosis, Basal-like has worst prognosis
Basal-like & Her2-enriched cancers have higher response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy
Pathology Report � Biopsy

Additional features to note besides morphology

ER/PR status � Be sure to note level and/or percentage
 � <1% is considered negative, <10% is considered low positive

HER2/neu status � IHC detects level of protein
 � IHC 0 and 1+ is negative, 2+ is equivocal, 3+ is positive

 � FISH detects exact number of copies of the gene (located on ch17)
 � Obtained if IHC 2+ to determine if HER2 amplified or not amplified

Ki67 � Proliferation index, reported as a percentage from 0-100
 � <14% considered low (luminal A)
Pathology Report � Surgical Specimen

Gross features � Tumor size
 � Multifocal (2 lesions in same quadrant) or Multicentric (2 lesions in different quadrants)
 � Location in breast (o'clock)

Nottingham Grade � Score of 1-3 is assigned for each of 3 factors: tubular formation, nuclear pleomorphism, & mitosis
 � Sum of these scores is the Nottingham score
 � Nottingham grade 1 = score 3, 4, 5; grade 2 = score 6, 7, grade 3 = score 8, 9

LVSI & EIC � Lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI): invasion into blood vessels or lymphatics
 � Extensive intraductal component (EIC): >25% DCIS component

Resection status � Margin status
 � Invasive cancer: no tumor on ink considered sufficient
 � DCIS: >2 mm negative margin considered sufficient
Oncotype DX 21-Gene Recurrence Score

TAILORx Trial, Sparano et al., NEJM 2018

Prognostic & predictive tool

 Prognosticates risk of recurrence
 Predicts node negative, hormone positive patients

 who might benefit from chemotherapy

Validated in TAILOR Rx trial

 Node negative, hormone positive patients with
 Oncotype RS 11-25 randomized to endocrine
 therapy vs. chemotherapy + endocrine therapy

 Endocrine tx alone was non-inferior to chemo +
 endocrine therapy

Patients 50 years, score 26: add chemo + ET

Patients <50 years, score 16-25: add chemo
 (subset analysis)

 Endocrine therapy alone was noninferior to chemoendocrine therapy
 for invasive disease free recurrence at 9 years (83% vs. 84%)

Treatment Overview
Surgery

Unless metastatic, surgery is standard of care
Surgery may be upfront, or after neoadjuvant systemic therapy, depending on clinical scenario

 E.g., patients not amenable for lumpectomy upfront may receive neoadjuvant CHT for downstaging
Important point

If there is a positive margin
after a segmental mastectomy
(lumpectomy), standard of care
is to ask the surgeon to take
patient back to the OR for re-
excision of the positive margin
Axillary Nodes: ALND vs. SLNB

Axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) was a historical standard for axillary surgical staging

 Involves dissecting entirety of level I and level II axilla
 Downside is high risk of lymphedema (~25% risk)
 Now, only standard for inflammatory breast cancer or clinically positive axilla without neoadjuvant CHT

Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is now standard in most cases

 Radioactive tracer (Tc99m sulfur colloid) and/or blue dye (isosulfan) injected into the tumor
 Surgeon uses a Gamma probe to locate the sentinel nodes and only removes those which have taken up

 the tracer/dye (on average 2-3 lymph nodes removed but can vary)
 False negative rate 8-10% but results in much less lymphedema/pain & better arm mobility
 Most patients with invasive cancer undergo SLNB

 Consider omission in T1 HR+ elderly patients

 Patients with DCIS only undergo SLNB if receiving mastectomy

 In case found to have invasive cancer, there is lymphatic disruption with mastectomy
Radiation

Need for adjuvant radiation depends on extent of surgery & stage

Following lumpectomy, adjuvant radiation is standard of care

 Select patients (>65 years, T1 N0, ER+) may be considered candidates for endocrine therapy alone

 Local recurrence risk is 5-10% without radiation vs. 1-2% with radiation --> risk vs. benefit discussion

Following mastectomy, adjuvant radiation is only given if there are indications

 Absolute indications for PMRT = T3-T4 disease, 4 positive nodes, or positive surgical margins

 1-3 positive nodes: "strongly consider" per NCCN
 T3N0 disease: account for other high-risk features including LVSI, tumor grade, close/positive margins, premenopausal status

Regional nodal irradiation (RNI) typically for patients with node positive disease

 Involves treatment of axillary levels I-III, supraclavicular, and internal mammary nodes

 If patients undergo axillary dissection, sometimes there are indications for radiation to the undissected axilla (e.g., involvement of
 nodal regions which cannot be dissected such as the IMN nodes)
Systemic Therapy

Endocrine therapy

 All hormone positive patients will receive some form of endocrine therapy whether localized or metastatic
 disease, usually in adjuvant setting

Chemotherapy

 Can be used neoadjuvantly (e.g., downstaging) or adjuvantly
 No difference in survival whether given neoadjuvant or adjuvant

Targeted therapy

 Example: trastuzumab (Herceptin) -- HER2 directed antibody
 Often added to chemotherapy regimens (e.g., TCHP)

Immunotherapy

 Example: Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) -- anti-PD1 agent which showed benefit in Keynote 522 trial for triple
 negative breast cancer patients

 Often added to chemotherapy regimens (e.g., AC-TC + Pembro as in Keynote-522)
Endocrine Therapy

Given adjuvantly for 5-10 years in hormone positive breast cancer

 Usually 5 years, but 10 years if additional risk factors (e.g., developed cancer while on hormonal ppx)

Agents

 Selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERM): tamoxifen (typically used in PREmenopausal patients)

 Antagonist in breast, agonist in the bones & uterus
 Hot flashes, increased risk of clots
 Increased risk of uterine cancer is more of a concern in postmenopausal women

 LHRH agonists: leuprolide, goserelin for ovarian suppression

 May consider additional ovarian suppression in premenopausal patients
 Never given alone, always given in addition to tamoxifen or AI (usually with tamoxifen for premenopausal patients)

 Aromatase inhibitors (AI): anastrozole, letrozole (typically used in POSTmenopausal patients)

 Risk of bone loss, arthralgias & myalgias
 Not as much risk of DVTs or uterine cancer
Common Chemotherapy Regimens

Hormone positive patients: (lowest rate of pCR following neoadjuvant therapy, only ~10-20%)

 If early stage, determine need for chemo using genomic testing (e.g., Oncotype, Mammaprint)
 Usually T3-T4 disease or node positive patients (any cN+ PREmeno or 4+ nodes POSTmeno) will get neoadjuvant chemo if HR+/HER2-
 Node positive: neoadjuvant or adjuvant regimen dose-dense AC-T: adriamycin + cyclophosphamide x4 q14 days (8 weeks), then paclitaxel x12 q1 week or x4 q2-3 weeks

 Adriamycin permanent cardiotoxicity

 Node negative: neoadjuvant or adjuvant regimen TC x4 q21 days: docetaxel + cyclophosphamide q21 days (12 weeks)

 Total body alopecia seen particularly with taxanes
 Neuropathy

HER2+ patients: (~60% pCR rate following neoadjuvant TCHP)

 >0.5-1 cm get chemotherapy
 Neoadjuvant TCHP x4 q21 days (12 weeks): docetaxel, carboplatin, Herceptin (trastuzumab), Perjeta (pertuzumab)

 Herceptin is the most cardiotoxic HER2-directed agent (more likely reversible, temporary cardiotoxicity with Herceptin)

 If pCR continue Trastuzumab q3 weeks x1 year
 If incomplete response adjuvant T-DM1: Kadcyla = trastuzumab emtansine (conjugated to cytotoxic agent DM1) x14 cycles q3 weeks

Triple negative patients: (~65% pCR rate following neoadjuvant CHT/IO):

 >0.5-1 cm get chemotherapy
 Before Keynote-522, neoadjuvant dose-dense AC-T
 Nowadays Keynote-522 = neoadjuvant TC-AC + Pembro: paclitaxel, carboplatin, pembrolizumab x4 q21 days (12 weeks), then doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide,

 pembrolizumab x4 q21 days (12 weeks) - node positive or tumor >2 cm
 If incomplete response adjuvant capecitabine (Xeloda)
Summary of Everyone gets surgery, goal is negative margins
Management
 Lumpectomy/segmental mastectomy vs. total mastectomy
 Most patients will have SLNB; ALND less common in modern era

 Chemotherapy can be given neoadjuvant or adjuvantly

 If incomplete response to neoadjuvant chemo (NAC) can consider
 adjuvant treatments (e.g., capecitabine if HER2- or T-DM1 if HER2+)

 Indications for chemotherapy

 HR+/HER2-

 Oncotype score 26+ postmenopausal or 18+ premenopausal
 4+ nodes postmenopausal or 1+ nodes premenopausal
 Usually T3-T4 or N+ receive neoadjuvant CHT

 HER2+

 Tumor >0.5-1 cm

 Triple negative

 Tumor >0.5-1 cm

 Indications for hormone therapy

 If HR+, all patients receive adjuvant ET x5 years at least

 Tamoxifen (SERM) if premenoapusal
 Anastrazole (AI) if postmenopausal

 Can be given concurrently with RT or started after RT is complete
Summary of All patients receive adjuvant radiation after lumpectomy
Management
 Early-stage favorable risk & elderly may consider omission

 Still have higher LR risk with omission

 Some patients receive PMRT after mastectomy

 Generally indicated for stage III disease
 Absolute indications = T3-T4, 4 positive nodes, positive margins

 T1-2 and 1-3 nodes: "strongly consider" per NCCN
 T3N0: controversial, account for other high-risk features including LVSI, high

 grade, close/positive margins, premenopausal status

 Regional nodal irradiation (RNI)

 Includes RNI to axilla I-III, SCV, & IMN
 Consider RNI in any patient with microscopic disease (>0.2 mm),

 taking account other high-risk features (young age, grade,
 hormone status, LVSI, Ki67)

 Can consider "high tangents" --> RT to lower axilla
 Otherwise comprehensive regional nodal irradiation (RNI)

Genomic Risk Scoring

Genomic assays (DCIS Oncotype, DCISionRT) have been validated as independent prognostic &
predictive tools for estimating risk of recurrence

 DCIS Oncotype

 Based on molecular profiling of patients on ECOG E5194 study, validated in retrospective cohort
 10-year LR risk: 13%, 33%, 28% in low, intermediate, & high risk groups respectively
 Risk of recurrence in low-risk group high enough to warrant RT

 DCISionRT

 Generated based on 7 protein biomarkers at UCSF and validated in retrospective cohorts, including SweDCIS
 PREDICT Study (Shah et al., Ann Surg Onc 2021): DCISionRT changed RT decision 42% of the time

 20% net decrease in RT usage 35% of time change from no RT to yes RT, 46% of time change from yes RT to no RT

Utilization is often institution-dependent, sometimes case-dependent

 E.g., may order DCISionRT if considering omission of RT
Adjuvant RT following
mastectomy for DCIS?

TYPICALLY, RECOMME NDATION IS OBS ERVATION E VEN WITH CLOSE
OR POSITIVE MARGINS
Retrospective data after mastectomy

Childs et al., IJROBP 2012 (Harvard)

 142 patients with mastectomy without post-op RT
 15% had positive margins, 16% had close margins (2 mm)
 1 patient with positive margin & 1 patient with close margin experienced chest wall recurrence

Chan et al., IJROBP 2010 (UCSF)

 193 patients with mastectomy without post-op RT
 4 patients had positive margin, 55 patients had close margin
 Risk of chest wall recurrence 1.7% for all, 3.4% for high-grade patients

 Following mastectomy for DCIS, observation is likely adequate even with a close or positive margin.

 (Remember, if undergoing mastectomy for DCIS, should have SLNB in case of invasive component!)
Radiotherapy Same principles as invasive ductal carcinoma
for DCIS

NSABP B-04: Radical vs. Total Mastectomy

Fisher et al., NEJM 2002 (25-year results)

1971-1974: randomized based on cN0 vs. cN+ as per diagram

 Halstead radical mastectomy = en bloc resection of breast, muscles of
 chest wall, & axilla

 Total mastectomy = total mastectomy

 With axillary dissection if nodes became positive

 Total mastectomy + XRT = 50 Gy/25 Fx to whole breast and axilla, 45
 Gy to SCV & IMNs, additional 10 Gy boost if cN+

No significant difference in distant DFS or OS after total
mastectomy alone vs. total mastectomy + XRT in cN0 and cN+
patients

 There was slightly worse LRR in cN0 patients

 Note: 40% of patients with clinically negative axilla found to have pN+ disease after
 Halstead radical mastectomy --> worse imaging for clinical staging of axilla

 No need for Halstead radical mastectomy whether cN0 or cN+.
 No survival advantage from PMRT in patients with cN0 disease.

Fisher et al., NEJM 2002 (20-year results)

1976-1984: 1863 women with stage I-II breast cancer (tumor <4 cm, �LNs)
randomized

 Total mastectomy & ALND
 Lumpectomy (segmental mastectomy) & ALND (+margin got mastectomy)
 Lumpectomy & ALND + XRT (50 Gy to breast only, no boost)

 Note: cN+ patients received CHT (melphalan/fluoruracil)

XRT decreased chance of ipsilateral breast recurrence at 20 years (39%
lumpectomy alone vs. 14% with XRT, p < 0.001), independent of nodal status
No significant difference in DFS (35%) or OS (46%) between 3 arms

 30% of recurrences occurred after 10 years of follow-up in lumpectomy + RT group

 Lumpectomy + Radiation significantly reduces risk of ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence (39% --> 14%).
 Need long-term follow-up in these patients!
 EBCTCG Meta-Analysis: BCS � RT

 Lancet 2011

Meta-analysis of 10,801 patients from 17 trials looking at BCS � RT
10-year recurrence (local or distant) was 35% with BCS only vs. 19% with BCS + RT (p < 0.00001)

 >75% recurrences were locoregional without RT, but <50% were locoregional with RT

Absolute difference in LR was 15.7% (year 10), and in breast cancer specific mortality was 3.8% (year 15): 4:1 ratio

 RT after BCS substantially reduces
 risk of recurrence (local & distant).

 On average, about ONE breast
 cancer death was avoided by year

 15 for every FOUR recurrences
 avoided by year 10.

Krag et al., Lancet 2007 & 2010

1999-2004 (pre-ultrasound era): 5611 women with
invasive breast cancer (cT1-3, cN0) randomized

Technique: Tc-99m & isosulfan blue dye
82% in both groups received RT

and numbness & tingling with SLNB vs. ALND
But what do we do if SLNB is positive?

AMAROS Trial (Donker et al., Lancet 2014)

 Patients with T1-T2 and SLN+ (up to 3; ITCs did NOT count) randomized to comprehensive RNI vs. ALND
 RNI was found to be non-inferior with less toxicity (lymphedema) compared to ALND arm

 10-year LRR 3.4% ALND vs. 3.8% RT; 10-year axillary recurrence 0.9% ALND vs. 1.8% RT
 Lymphedema at 5 years was 23% with ALND vs. 11% with RNI

ACOSOG Z0011 Trial (Giuliano et al., JAMA 2017)

 Patients with cT1-2 N0 disease and 1-2 SLN+ randomized to completion ALND vs. No ALND

 Note trial did not complete accrual
 On ALND, 21% had 3 positive nodes

 Protocol specified WBI with tangents only (SCV or targeted axillary radiation prohibited)

 89% received WBI; 50% were treated with high tangents (level I-II axilla included), and ~20% received protocol prohibited RNI
 No significant difference between ALND vs. SLNB arms (Jagsi et al., JCO 2014)

 10-year OS was non-inferior (84% ALND vs. 86% SLNB, p = 0.02); No difference in DFS (78% vs 80%)

 No need for ALND if SLNB is positive. SLNB + RNI ~halves the rate of lymphedema vs. ALND.
RNI in certain high-risk patients
improves DFS

TOXICIT IES OF RNI = LYMPHEDE MA & PNEUMONIT IS
MA.20 Trial � RNI vs. WBI Alone

Whelan et al., NEJM 2015

 2000-2007: 1832 pts with node positive or high-risk node negative breast cancer

 High-risk node negative: tumor >5 cm, or >2 cm with <10 nodes removed and either grade 3, ER
 negative, or LVSI

 All had at least SLNB or ALND (with level I-II ALND if SLNB+) and all had systemic tx (chemo,
 endocrine, or both)

 Node positive: SLNB+ all had ALND --> 85% had 1-3 +nodes (10% 0 nodes, 5% 4 +nodes)

 Randomization

 WBI alone 50 Gy/25 Fx
 WBI alone + RNI (axilla level III, SCV, & IMNs; level I-II included only if <10 nodes sampled or 4

 +nodes)

 Results (10-year)

 No benefit in OS with RNI (82% both groups)
 DFS was improved in RNI group (82% vs. 77%, p = 0.01)

 Toxicity

 Acutely, worse pneumonitis with RNI (1.2% vs. 0.2%), worse dermatitis
 Long-term, worse lymphedema with RNI (8% vs. 4%)

 5% DFS benefit seen with addition of RNI, but no OS benefit.
 Note: only ~5% of patients had 4 nodes positive.
EORTC 22922: RNI to IMN & Medial SCV

Poortmans et al., NEJM 2015

 1996-2004: patients with lateral tumor & N+, or medial tumor +/- N+ randomized

 Control group: WBI/CWI
 Nodal RT group: WBI/CWI + RNI

 43% had N+, 44% had medial tumors with N0

 Results

 10-year DFS improved with RNI (72% vs. 69%, p = 0.04)

 Treat 30 patients to avoid 1 relapse
 Significance lost at 15-year follow-up

 No difference in 10-year OS (82% vs. 81%, p = 0.06)
 15-year BCM improved with RNI: 20% no RNI vs. 16% with RNI (p = 0.0055)

 Toxicity at 10 years

 Worse pulmonary fibrosis (4% vs. 2%) with RNI
 No difference in 2nd cancers or contralateral breast cancer

 3% DFS benefit seen with addition of RNI at 10-years. No OS benefit.
 At 15-years there was persistent improvement in breast-cancer specific mortality.
RNI in Early-Stage Breast Cancer

Based on AMAROS & ACOSOG Z0011, we know that for patients with SLNB+ without completion
ALND, RNI offers equivalent control with improved toxicity (less lymphedema)

MA.20 & EORTC 22922 trials add additional support for RNI in certain early-stage patients with
node positive disease or high-risk features

 Stage II: pT1-T2 pN1a (1-3 axillary lymph nodes) or pT3 pN0

 Note: anything N2+ or anything T3 N+ is considered stage III

 MA.20: tumor >5 cm, or >2 cm <10 nodes removed & ER negative, high grade, +LVSI
 EORTC 22922: lateral tumor N+ or medial tumor N+/0

Unclear if everyone benefits, or if there is a subset of patients who derive benefit from RNI

 Practice patterns may vary
 Ongoing trial Tailor RT may help add clarity for ER+ low-risk patients
Ongoing Tailor RT Trial

Phase III international, multi-center randomized non-inferiority trial

 T1-2 N1 disease (1-3 nodes allowed if ALND, 1-2 nodes allowed if SLNB)

 Oncotype Dx score 25 (these are considered "low-risk")

 WBI/PMRT
 WBI/PMRT + RNI

 TAILOR RT is an ongoing RCT designed to see if there is a particular subset of patients with low N+ or
 T3N0 breast cancer, ER+, low-risk (by oncotype) breast cancer who can avoid RNI.
RT may be omitted in certain patients
with low-risk early-stage breast cancer

 Distribution of grades was not revealed

 Randomization

 Tamoxifen alone (20 mg per day x5 years)
 Tamoxifen + WBI (TamRT)

 Results

 TamRT group had significantly longer time to locoregional recurrence
 10-year LRR was 10% for Tam alone vs. 2% TamRT (p < 0.001)
 No difference in OS, DFS, or mastectomy-free survival

 Study of omission in early stage, favorable risk breast cancer in patients age 70+.
 Risk of IBTR 10% with Tamoxifen alone, vs. 2% with Tamoxifen + RT.
PRIME II: ET +/- RT in 65+

Kunkler et al., NEJM 2004, NEJM 2023

2003-2009: 1263 patients age 65+ with stage I breast cancer

 ER+ and/or PR+
 Tumor 3 cm
 S/p BCS with negative margins (1 mm)
 Only 2-3% were grade 3

Randomization

 Tamoxifen 20 mg daily x5 years (or other ET)
 Tam + WBI

Results at 5-year follow-up

 IBTR 4.1% Tam vs. 1.3% TamRT (p = 0.0002)
 OS (94%) & DFS were same b/w groups

At 10-years: LR 10% 1% with RT (p < 0.001)

 Study of omission in early stage, favorable risk breast cancer in patients age 65+.
 Risk of 10-year IBTR 10% with Tamoxifen alone, vs. 1% with Tamoxifen + RT � still significant!
How do we interpret these studies?

CALGB 9343 & PRIME II trials � conventional RT era involving 5-6 weeks of RT

In modern day, we utilize hypofractionation

 Shortest regimen = 1 week of radiation (5 fractions over 1 week, M-F) � partial or whole breast
 Longest regimens = 3 weeks of radiation (15-16 fractions over 3 weeks, M-F) � whole breast (+ maybe 4-5 Fx boost)

These studies show no difference in OS or DFS with RT + ET, but RT still improves IBTR (5-10% to 1-2%)

 Matters to patients with good performance status
 Also, patients have to take endocrine therapy for 5 years!

Note: Lumina A study (Whelan et al., NEJM 2023): patients 55+ with luminal A breast cancer & omission of RT

 Risk of LF was 2.3%, essentially the same as risk of contralateral breast cancer which was 1.9%
 T1N0 grade 1-2 luminal A breast cancer: note that average age was actually 67

Ongoing EUROPA study 5 days or 5 years?

 Age 70+, cT1 N0 (<2 cm) � any grade if 1-10 mm or grade 2-3 if 11-19 mm
 Noninferiority trial, randomizing to partial breast irradiation 5 Fx (5 days) vs. endocrine therapy (5 years)
Major In patients 65 years with low-risk early-stage breast cancer,
Takeaways breast radiation improves IBTR

 Have to be HR+ with stage I tumors

 Note prime II allowed 3 cm maximum

 IBTR risk drops from 10% 1-2%
 Treatment takes 1-3 weeks, low toxicity profile

 If omit RT, still have to take 5 years of endocrine therapy

 Ongoing EUROPA study to see if 5 days of RT alone is non-
 inferior to 5 years of ET

 In patients who would benefit from boost (e.g., young
 age, high grade, triple negative) we wouldn't be offering RT
 omission anyway
Conclusion

Radiation improves local control after surgery

 In older studies (NSABP B-06 & EBCTCG meta-analysis), about 35-40% 15-20%
 In modern era, it's more like 15% 8% with RT 2-3% with RT + systemic tx

Tumor bed boost improves IBTR, greatest magnitude in young age

 ASTRO guideline on boost: age 50, age 51-70 with high grade, positive margin

SLNB + RNI if N+ has equivalent outcome & better toxicity vs. ALND

 Some pts with low-risk dz and low nodal burden may not need RNI after SLNB (ongoing TAILOR RT)

Elderly patients with low-risk early-stage disease still derive local control benefit from RT

 Addition of RT improves local control from 5-10% 1-2%
 If choosing RT omission, still must take ET for 5 years (ongoing EUROPA study 5 days RT instead of ET)
Thank you!

CREATE-X & KATHERINE

Adjuvant therapy after incomplete response to NAC

CREATE-X (Masuda et al., NEJM 2017)

 910 patients with HER2-negative breast cancer (68% HR+, 32% TNBC) s/p NAC & SGY without pCR
 Randomized to standard adjuvant tx +/- capecitabine 1250 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 q3 weeks x6-8 cycles
 DFS & OS were both improved in the adjuvant capecitabine group

 DFS 74% vs. 68%, p=0.01; OS 89% vs. 84%, p=0.01

 Greater magnitude of benefit in TNBC

KATHERINE (Von Minckwitz et al., NEJM 2019)

 1486 patients with HER2+ breast cancer and residual disease after NAC (usually TCHP)
 Randomized to adjuvant T-DM1 vs. trastuzumab for 14 cycles

 T-DM1 = antibody-drug conjugate of trastuzumab and emtansine (DM1), a cytotoxic agent (microtubule inhibitor)

 T-DM1 improved 3-year DFS (88% vs. 77%, p<0.001)

 If residual disease after NAC: adjuvant therapy is capecitabine for HER2(-) & T-DM1 for HER2(+)

SLNB VS. ALND
IMN IRRADIATION
SLNB vs. ALND (no NAC)

NSABP B-32: SLNB + ALND vs. SLNB & ALND only if positive

 SLN overall accuracy 97% with less AE (improved lymphedema & shoulder dysfunction)

AMAROS: RNI vs. ALND in SLN+ patients showed RNI is less toxic with non-inferior outcome

 LRR was 3.4% ALND vs. 3.8% RNI
 Lymphedema was 23% ALND vs. 11% RNI

ACOSOG Z0011: cT1-2 and 1-2 SLN+ randomized to ALND vs. no ALND

 No difference in OS (84% vs. 86%) or DFS (78% vs. 80%)
 However, despite protocol specification AGAINST any RNI, majority of patients treated with high

 tangents (low axilla treated)

 SLNB (with RNI if SLN+) is less toxic with equivalent outcomes to ALND.
IBCSG 23-01: SLNBmi+ +/- ALND

Galimberti et al., Lancet 2013

931 pts with T1-T2 (5 cm) and N1mi (2 mm)

Randomized to ALND or no ALND

No difference in 5-yr DFS or other outcomes

 5-yr DFS 84% ALND vs. 88% no ALND (p = 0.16)

ALND group higher rates of grade 3 events

 Sensory/motor neuropathy & lymphedema

Note: type of surgery & RT

 10% of patients had mastectomy, 90% BCS
 22% of patients had no axillary RT (either no RT or

 partial breast RT)

 ALND can be omitted if SLNBmi+. But then... do we treat with radiation?
 If mastectomy, can probably omit.

 If no mastectomy, consider WBI for low axillary coverage, or even high tangents (ACOSOG Z0011).
SLNB after NAC?

ACOSOG Z1071: phase II trial of cT0-4 N1-2 breast cancer s/p NAC & surgery with SLNB + ALND

 False negative rate of SLNB was 12.6% (did not accept prespecified endpoint of <10%)

SENTINA: phase II prospective study comparing SLNB vs. ALND in patients undergoing NAC

 4 arms, found 99% detection rate before NAC and higher FNR after NAC

 A: cN0 with SLNB- before NAC (no further SLNB)
 B: cN0 with SLNB+ before NAC SLNB after NAC
 C: cN+ with NAC if ycN0 SLNB + ALND
 D: cN+ with NAC if yN+ then ALND without SLNB

 Arm B FNR 52%, Arm C FNR 14% (24% if 1 node, 14% if 2 nodes, <10% if 3 nodes removed)
 FNR 8.6% with dual tracer

SN FNAC: phase II prospective study of SLNB + ALND after NAC (cT0-3, N1-2)

 Overall 8.4% FNR (18.2% with 1 node, 4.9% with 2 nodes; 16% single-tracer vs. 5.2% dual tracer)
 ITCs were counted as positive! If not counted as positive, FNR would have increased to 13.3%

 SLNB after NAC can yield low FNR (<10%) if: dual tracers are used & 2 nodes sampled
 (ITCs counted as positive in SLNB after NAC)
Alliance A011202: Ongoing

Phase III RCT of ALND vs. RNI in SLNB+ after NAC

 Inclusion:

 cT1-3 N1 M0
 4 cycles NAC

 Estimated primary
 completion 2024
Should IMNs be included in RNI?

The 3 randomized PMRT trials (DBCG 82b, DBCG 82c, & British Columbia) treated IMNs
MA.20 & EORTC 22922 both treated IMNs and showed a 10-year DFS benefit

 MA.20: high-risk node negative (tumor >5 cm, or >2 cm with <10 nodes removed and either grade 3, ER
 negative, or LVSI) randomized to WBI vs. WBI + RNI

 5% DFS benefit in RNI group (82% vs. 77%)

 EORTC 22922: lateral tumor & N+, or medial tumor �N+ randomized to WBI/CWI vs. WBI/CWI + RNI

 3% DFS benefit in RNI group (72% vs. 69%) & 15-year BCM benefit in RNI group (16% vs. 20%)

 PMRT trials (DBCG 82b/82c & BC) and RNI trials (MA.20 & EORTC 22922) both included IMNs &
 showed a benefit, but unclear if the benefit was specifically attributable to IMN inclusion.
Trials specifically looking at IMNI

French Trial (Hennequin et al., IJROBP 2013)

 Patients with +axillary nodes or central/medial tumors s/p MRM & ALND levels I-II
 Randomized to PMRT & SCV coverage +/- IMNI (first 5 intercostal spaces)
 Powered for OS; no 10-yr OS benefit seen (59% w/o vs. 62% w/ IMNI, p = 0.8)

DBCG-IMN (Thorsen et al., JCO 2015)

 Prospective population-based cohort study of pT1-3 pN1-3 patients
 Right-sided disease received IMNI, left-sided disease did not (c/f RT-induced heart disease)
 3% benefit in 8-yr OS with IMNI (OS 72% w/o vs. 75% w/ IMNI, p = 0.005)
 Also saw 3% improvement in BCM (23% w/o vs. 20% w/ IMNI, p = 0.005)

 There is likely a small but significant benefit to internal mammary nodal irradiation (IMNI) which
 French trial may not have been powered to detect.
Major ALND vs. SLNB + RNI
Takeaways
 SLNB + RNI is less toxic with equivalent outcomes

 AMAROS 23% lymphedema with ALND vs. 11% with SLNB

 If SLNBmi+ can omit ALND, if mastectomy no PMRT

 SLNB after NAC?

 Need dual tracer and 2 nodes sampled for lower FNR
 Ongoing Alliance A011202 phase III study

 RNI includes axillary levels I-III, SCV, IMN

 IMNI controversial but was included in MA.20 & EORTC 22922
 Probably provides small but significant benefit based on

 dedicated IMNI trials (French, DBCG-IMN, Korean)
Conclusions

PMRT improves OS in high-risk disease

 Landmark trials Danish 82b/c & British Columbia showed ~10% improvement in OS with PMRT
 82b/c included pts with positive axillary nodes, tumor >5 cm, or invasion to skin/pec fascia

Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NAC) allows for downstaging & directs further systemic tx

 If residual disease after NAC, capecitabine for HER2- or TDM-1 for HER2+
 If BCS, patients will always need RT after NAC � NSABP-51 abstract suggests can omit RNI if ypN0
 If mastectomy, indications for PMRT are generally clinical stage III & ypN+ disease (PMRT = CWI + RNI)

SLNB + RNI allows omission of ALND with equivalent outcomes & improved toxicity

 RNI includes axillary nodes, SCV, and IMN
 SLNB + RNI significantly decreases lymphedema (23% 11% in AMAROS)
 Tricky after NAC � need dual tracer and 2 nodes (phase III Alliance A011202 is ongoing)

Whelan et al., NEJM 2010

1993-1996: 1234 women with breast cancer s/p BCS, node negative

 Excluded positive margins, pN+, tumors >5 cm, breast separation >25 cm (d/t increased heterogeneity)
 Randomized to 50 Gy/25 Fx over 35 days vs. 42.56 Gy/16 Fx over 22 days

 No RNI or boost delivered

No significant difference in local recurrence, survival outcomes, or toxicity

 10-year LR around 6% in both groups
 10-year OS around 85% in both groups
 No significant difference with regard to toxicity or cosmetic outcomes

Note: initial subgroup analysis suggested worse local control in high-grade tumors

 Updated analysis showed no difference in outcome based on grade, just molecular subtype (Bane et al., Ann
 Oncol 2014)

 42.56 Gy/16 Fx is non-inferior to 50 Gy/25 Fx for pT1-2 N0 tumors with negative margins.

Haviland et al., Lancet 2013

1999-2002: Women with pT1�3a pN0�1 M0 s/p BCS or mastectomy were randomized

 UK Start B: no significant difference in local relapse compared to conventional fx (~4% LR)

 Moderate or marked breast shrinkage, telangiectasia, and breast edema were significantly LESS (late effects) in 40 Gy arm
 No difference in lung, heart, arm, or shoulder toxicity

 40 Gy in 15 Fx is at least as safe & effective as 50 Gy in 25 Fx, with less harm to normal tissues.
 40 Gy/15 Fx was already adopted in Canada & UK after 5-year results.

Abstract presented at ESTRO 2022

DBCG Skagen 1 Trial compared 50 Gy/25 Fx vs. 40 Gy/15 Fx for pT1-3 N0-3 pts undergoing RNI

FABREC: HypoFX in Reconstructed Breast

Wong et al., IJROBP 2023

2018-2021: 385 patients with stage 0-III breast cancer & immediate implant-based
reconstruction randomized

 o Conventional RT: 50 Gy/25 Fx
 o Moderate HypoFX: 42.56 Gy/16 Fx

+/- RNI. No boost permitted, bolus at physician discretion.

No significant difference in physical well-being or overall toxicity profile between arms

 o No significant difference in grade 3+ chest wall toxicity (p = 0.58)
 o No significant difference in physical well-being scores (p = 0.71)
 o Younger patients (<45 years) had less nausea & side effects with moderate hypoFX
 o Patients who took unpaid time-off work required fewer hours off with moderate hypoFX (p = 0.046)

 Moderate hypofractionation is safe after immediate implant-based reconstruction.
 Moderate hypofractionation a/w less side effects in younger patients, and less unpaid hours off work.

GI.Anal Cancer:
Anal Cancer

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Anal Cancer

Approximately 1 in 500 lifetime risk

~2.5% of GI malignancies

 Rectal cancer is 5x more common!

2x as likely to affect females

Average age of diagnosis is 60s

Most likely to present as local or
locoregional disease, with high rates of cure

HPV is associated with ~85-90% of cases

 HIV is also a risk factor
Objectives Definitive ChemoRT

H&P � ask about duration and nature of symptoms, complete history

 Focus on DRE with evaluation of tumor extent and sphincter function

Labs � CBC, CMP, CEA, HIV, CD4 (if HIV+)

 Excisional biopsy or FNA of suspicious lymph nodes

Procedures � sigmoidoscopy/colonoscopy

Imaging � CT CAP, MRI pelvis with contrast, PET CT (esp. T3-T4, N+)
Anatomy Above dentate line,
 lymphatic drainage
Anal canal = extends from dentate
line to anal verge (~4 cm long) to mesorectal &
 internal iliac nodes
 Dentate line separates columnar
 epithelium of rectum from squamous Below dentate line,
 epithelium of anal canal lymphatic drainage

 Canal surrounded by internal and to inguinal &
 external anal sphincters femoral nodes

Anal verge = palpable junction
between non-hair-bearing and hair-
bearing squamous epithelium

Anal margin = peri-anal skin within 5
cm of anal verge
Histology

Anal canal

 True mucosa

 Above dentate line
 Contains columns of Morgagni (longitudinal folds,

 inferiorly form the anal valves)

 Transitional zone

 Starts at dentate line, contains squamous epithelium

Anal margin

 Cutaneous zone
Pathology

Squamous cell carcinoma � ~80%

 ~85-90% of cases associated with HPV

Adenocarcinoma � ~10%, treated like rectal cancer
Other histologies � melanoma, neuroendocrine,
carcinoid, Kaposi's, leiomyosarcoma, lymphoma

 Perianal skin tumors (SCC, BCC, melanoma, Bowen's,
 Paget's) should be treated as skin cancer

 Example of anal squamous cell carcinoma

Treatment Paradigm
Summary of T1 N0 anal margin well-differentiated SCC
Management
 Wide local excision with 1 cm margin

 T1 N0 anal canal SCC, T2-T4 or N+

 Definitive chemoRT

 5-FU + mitomycin C

 N1 54 Gy/30 Fx
Surgery

Wide local excision of select, early lesions

 Tumor forms a discrete lesion arising from the perianal skin that is clearly separate from the anal canal
 Negative margin excision can be accomplished without compromise of the adjacent sphincter muscles
 No evidence of regional nodal involvement

Otherwise, most patients are treated with definitive chemoRT

SGY used in cases of local recurrence or persistence

 Typically, abdominoperineal resection (APR) as part of total mesorectal excision (TME)
 Apply same principles as rectal cancer for APR
 Often require wider perianal margins
Radiotherapy

RTOG 0529 showed improved toxicity profile with IMRT vs. 3D planning
