Chemotherapy

Standard chemotherapy is concurrent 5-FU and Mitomycin-C

 2 cycles, week 1 and week 5, given concurrently with RT

5-fluorouracil: inhibits thymidylate synthase, preventing synthesis of dTMP (needed for DNA)

 1000 mg/m2 days 1-4 x2 cycles q4 weeks
 Major toxicity = mucositis, stomatitis, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea

Mitomycin-C (MMC): alkylating agent which crosslinks DNA

 10 mg/m2 bolus x2 cycles q4 weeks
 Undergoes bioreductive activation under hypoxic conditions more potent in hypoxic conditions
 Major toxicity = neutropenia
Definitive ChemoRT

NIGRO REGIMEN SHOWED HIGH RATES OF CR WITH CHE MORT
RT + CONCURRENT 5-FU/MMC IS STANDARD
Wayne State "Nigro Regimen": 30 Gy/15 Fx

Leichman et al., Am J Med 1985

"Nigro protocol" originated at Wayne State � preoperative chemoRT with 5-FU + MMC

Retrospective review of 45 patients (stage T2 or greater) treated with preop chemoRT

 30 Gy/15 Fx using AP/PA technique to pelvis & inguinal nodes
 Concurrent 5-FU & Mitomycin C

 Infusional 5-FU 1,000 mg/m2 for 96 hours � 2 cycles days 1 to 4 and 29 to 32
 Bolus MMC (15 mg/m2) on day 1 x 1 cycle

Originally, APR was required after chemoRT, but first 5/6 patients had complete response

 Amended to biopsy at 4-6 weeks after chemoRT

84% of patients had CR at time of biopsy, with no local recurrences seen

 15% of patients that did not have CR did have recurrence, all of which were distant recurrences

Overall 5-year OS was 67% and 5-year colostomy-free survival was 59%

 "Nigro Regimen" established definitive chemoRT as an effective treatment for anal cancer.
UK ACT I: 45 Gy RT � CHT (5-FU/MMC)

Northover et al., Br J Cancer 2010

1987-1994: 585 patients with stage II-IV anal cancer (excluded T1 N0 stage UK ACT I showed improved LF & CSS
I) randomized with chemoRT for anal cancer with

 RT alone � 45 Gy/20-25 Fx with AP/PA fields no increase in late toxicity.
 RT + concurrent CHT � 5-FU infusion (4-5 days) with first and last week of RT, MMC

 bolus on day 1

 5-FU 1000 mg/m2 for 4 days or 750 mg/m2 for 5 days (x2 cycles q4-5 weeks)
 MMC 12 mg/m2 on day 1 x1 cycle

Response assessed at 6 weeks

 If good response additional RT: 25 Gy at 10 Fx per day Ir-192 HDR BT or 15 Gy/6
 Fx EBRT

 If no response salvage surgery

1o endpoint: local failure significantly decreased with CHT

 3-year LF 61% with RT vs. 39% with CRT (p<0.001)
 3-year CSS 61% with RT vs. 72% with CRT (p=0.02)

No difference in 3-year OS: 58% RT vs. 65% CRT (p=0.25)

13-year update showed 25 fewer locoregional relapses & 12.5 fewer cancer
deaths for every 100 patients treated with CRT. No difference in late
toxicity.

1988-1991: 310 patients with any T/N anal cancer treated with definitive chemoRT randomized

Perioperative CHT
MAGIC: Periop CHT for Gastric/GEJ

Cunningham et al., NEJM 2006

1994-2002: 503 patients with resectable, locally advanced (stage II+)
adenocarcinoma of stomach or lower 1/3 of esophagus randomized

 Surgery alone
 Perioperative chemotherapy (3-week cycles, 3 cycles preop 3 cycles postop)

 Epirubicin, cisplatin, 5-FU (ECF)
 Surgery 3-6 weeks after 3rd cycle of chemo, and postop chemo 6-12 weeks after surgery

42% of patients assigned to periop CHT group completed all 6 cycles of
chemotherapy

69% of patients in periop CHT vs. 66% of patients in SGY only had R0 resection

Some downstaging from preop CHT but no pCR benefit

Significant OS & PFS improvement with periop CHT

 5-year OS 36% periop CHT vs. 23% SGY only (p=0.009); HR 0.75
 HR for PFS 0.66 (p<0.001)

 MAGIC showed OS & PFS benefit to periop CHT vs. SGY alone for patients
 with adenocarcinoma of stomach or lower 1/3 esophagus.
FLOT4: Periop ECF vs. FLOT

Al-Batran et al., Lancet 2019

2010-2015: 716 patients with adenocarcinoma of stomach or GEJ
with cT2, cN+, or both randomized 1:1

 ECF/ECX x3 cycles preop, 3 cycles postop given q3 weeks

 5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, docetaxel

Surgery given 4 weeks after CHT

Significant improvement in OS & DFS with FLOT vs. ECF/ECX

 Median OS 50 mo FLOT vs. 35 mo ECF (p=0.012)

Similar rates of hospitalization (26% vs. 25%)

NeoAEGIS: Preop chemoRT vs. periop CHT

Reynolds et al., Lancet 2023

2013-2020: 362 patients with cT2-T3, N0-3, M0 adenocarcinoma of esophagus or GEJ
randomized

 CROSS regimen: preop CRT 41.4 Gy/23 Fx with carbo/taxol SGY
 MAGIC regimen: periop ECF (later allowed periop FLOT as well) with SGY

Designed to show superiority of periop CHT; closed early in 2020 after futility analysis

No significant difference in OS or DFS between arms

 3-year OS 55% periop CHT vs. 57% preop CRT (p=0.82); median OS 48 mo vs. 49 mo
 Median DFS 32 mo periop CHT vs. 24 mo preop CRT (p=0.41)
 No significant difference in patterns of recurrence

pCR rates, tumor downstaging, and R0 resection rates all favored preop chemoRT

 pCR 4% periop CHT vs. 12% preop CRT (p=0.012)
 R0 resection 82% periop CHT vs. 96% preop CRT (p=0.003)

PROs: HRQoL questionnaires

 Significantly worse emotional functioning, pain, and coughing at 1 year with preop CRT
 Diarrhea significantly worse in periop CHT

 NeoAEGIS showed similar OS and DFS for periop CHT and preop CRT approaches.
 Improved pCR and R0 resection rates after preop CRT.

Caveats � (1) did not compare to CM-577 (adj Nivo) which is new SOC; (2) FLOT regimen more toxic.

Takeaways alternative to preop CRT

Esophageal Cancer
Overview

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Esophageal SCC: ~90% of cases globally
Cancer Adeno: ~70% of cases in Western Europe & US

Incidence peaks in 6th and 7th decades

Lifetime risk of ~0.5% in men and
women

Two main histologies

 Squamous cell carcinoma
 Adenocarcinoma

Presentation

Progressive dysphagia
Early satiety
Weight loss
Heartburn
Melena
Anemia
Depending on location � hoarseness/cough
May be detected on screening endoscopy for
patients with Barrett's esophagus
Risk Factors ADENOCARCINOMA = "BOG"

SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA = "ABCDEF" B = Barrett's esophagus
 O = obesity
A = achalasia G = GERD
B = bad diet (high fat, high temperatures)
C = caustic stricture (lye ingestion) Weekly symptoms increase risk by factor of 5
C = cigarette smoking Daily symptoms increase risk by factor of 7
D = dysplsia/diverticuli
E = esophageal webs (Plummer-Vinson)
F = familial/genetic
Workup

Full H&P

Labs � CBC, CMP

Barium swallow study

Upper GI endoscopy & biopsy

Imaging

 CT CAP with oral and IV contrast
 FDG PET-CT
 Endoscopic ultrasound

 More accurate than PET CT for nodal staging, and allows biopsy of suspicious nodes

 Bronchoscopy � rule out TEF for tumors above the carina

Molecular testing

 All patients should be tested for MSI/dMMR status
 Metastatic patients should be tested for PD-L1 and HER2 status
Anatomy

Esophagus is approximately 25 cm long
Begins at cricopharyngeus muscle
Spans approximately 15-40 cm from incisors

 15-20 cm = cervical esophagus
 20-25 cm = upper thoracic
 25-30 cm = middle thoracic
 30-40 cm = lower thoracic esophagus

Anterior to vertebral levels C6-T10

No true serosa
Nonkeratinized squamous epithelium superiorly
Glandular epithelium inferiorly
Extensive submucosal lymphatic plexus ( metastases)
Anatomical
landmarks
T-stage is based on depth of invasion
Gastroesophageal Junction (GEJ)

GEJ = site of epithelial change
GEJ tumors are located within 5 cm of the true GEJ

Siewert system:

 Class I = 1-5 cm superior to GEJ
 Class II = 1 cm above to 2 cm below GEJ
 Class III = 2-5 cm below GEJ
Regional Lymph Nodes

Location Regional Nodes
Cervical esophagus
 Supraclavicular
Upper/middle/lower esophagus Scalene
GE junction Internal jugular
 Upper and lower cervical
 Periesophageal

 Upper periesophageal (above azygous vein)
 Subcarinal
 Lower periesophageal (below azygous vein)

 Lower esophageal
 Diaphragmatic
 Pericardial
 Left gastric
 Celiac
Upper Endoscopy with Biopsy

Visualize tumor on upper endoscopy and
obtain mucosal biopsy
Measurements:

 Distance from incisors to tumor
 Tumor dimensions
 Tumor circumference

Example:

 Nodular mass occupying 50-74% circumference
 of distal esophagus

 Image from Radiopaedia.org
Pathology

 Prevalence Location Response

Squamous cell 90% of cases globally Upper and middle Higher rates of pCR
carcinoma (SCC) esophagus after CRT
 Lower rates of pCR after
Adenocarcinoma 70% of cases in North Distal esophagus CRT
(ACA)
 America & Western Europe

Other, less common histologies:

 Small cell carcinoma
 Sarcoma
Molecular Testing

All patients should have MSI/MMR testing

 Microsatellite instability (MSI) is a phenotype � tested via PCR or NGS

 If instability seen in 30% loci, this is said to be MSI-H

 Mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) is the cause �tested via IHC

 If there is loss of expression of MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 proteins on IHC, this is said to be dMMR

 MSI-H and dMMR have high correlation with one another

Locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic patients should have PD-L1 and HER2 testing

 PD-L1 testing on IHC

 Combined positive score (CPS) 1% is positive � percentage of tumor associated lymphocytes and macrophages that express PD-L1
 Can also use tumor proportion score (TPS) 1% � percentage of tumor cells that express PD-L1

 HER2 testing by IHC

 HER2 3+ on IHC is positive
 HER2 2+ is equivocal determine if positive or negative via FISH
 HER2 0-1 is negative
CT & FDG PET-CT

CT CAP with oral and IV contrast + FDG PET-CT should be performed for staging

 Rule out distant metastases
 Evaluate extent of primary tumor and nodal involvement

PET CT has relatively low sensitivity and specificity and should be correlated with EUS

 ~50% sensitivity & ~80% specificity for esophageal cancer (van Westreenen et al., 2004)
Endoscopic Ultrasound for Staging

Useful for TNM staging Staging accuracy of EUS (Puli et al., 2008)

 T-staging based on invasion through esophageal wall
 N-staging based on number of nodes involved

Normally, can visualize 5 layers of esophagus T-staging sensitivity 82-92% (>T4 vs. T1)
 T-staging specificity 97-99%
 Superficial mucosa (hyperechoic)
 Mucosa (hypoechoic) N-staging sensitivity ~85% 97% with FNA
 Submucosa (hyperechoic)
 Muscularis propria (hypoechoic)
 Adventitia (hypoechoic)

On ultrasound, can see local invasion of the tumor (better at visualizing more invasive tumors)

Hypoechoic lymph nodes 1 cm with round shape, sharp borders, and absence of intranodal vessels
are suggestive of malignancy

 Can obtain FNA to confirm whether involved
Example of T3 N1 esophageal adenocarcinoma:

Irregular hypoechoic distal esophageal tumor Enlarged paraesophageal lymph node
invading adventitia/subserosa Image from Radiopaedia.org

Surgery for T1 tumors

Endoscopic resection or ablation vs. esophagectomy

Approaches to endoscopic resection or ablation

 Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) Endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD)

- Best for smaller lesions (<1.5 cm) - Can be used for larger lesions
- Piecemeal resection - En bloc resection
- Usually performed with snare - Requires ESD knife for cutting
 - Allows more precise histologic staging

Consider ablation after EMR or ESD for residual Barret's esophagus

 Radiofrequency ablation
 Cryoablation
Surgery for T2-T4 or N+ tumors
Surgery

Contraindications to surgery

 cT4b � involvement of heart, great vessels, trachea, adjacent organs
 Multi-station bulky lymphadenopathy
 Metastases/nonregional lymph nodes (stage IV) � obtain PET CT prior to SGY
 GEJ tumors with supraclavicular lymph node involvement

Lymph node assessment at time of surgery

 15 nodes should be sampled for patients undergoing esophagectomy without induction chemoRT
 After induction chemoRT, no consensus but aim for similar
 Retrospective evidence: increased # of nodes sampled improves OS (Greenstein et al., 2008)
Surgical Approaches

Approach to Esophagectomy Incisions

Ivor Lewis esophagogastrectomy Laparotomy + right thoracotomy

Minimally invasive Ivor Lewis Laparoscopy + limited right thoracotomy

McKeown esophagogastrectomy Right thoracotomy + laparotomy + cervical
 anastomosis

Minimally invasive McKeown Right thoracoscopy + limited
 laparotomy/laparoscopy + cervical anastomosis

Transhiatal esophagogastrectomy Laparotomy + cervical anastomosis

Robotic minimally invasive esophagogastrectomy

Left transthoracic or thoracoabdominal approaches with anastomosis in chest or neck
Summary

Surgery alone for T1 N0 tumors � endoscopic resection preferred, or esophagectomy

T2-T4 or N+ tumors:

 ChemoRT esophagectomy nivolumab if residual disease (assuming R0 resection)

 41.4-50.4 Gy/23-28 Fx with concurrent weekly carbo/taxol

 FLOT x4 esophagectomy FLOT x4

 Adenocarcinomas only

Future directions:

 Ongoing efforts towards organ preservation

 SANO trial is a phase III trial showing non-inferior OS with approach of upfront chemoRT active surveillance
 Further ongoing studies evaluating approach of induction chemotherapy chemoRT

Dutch TME Trial � CKVO 9504

Kapiteijn et al., NEJM 2001; Lancet Oncol 2011

1861 patients with resectable rectal cancer (<15 cm of anal verge)
who could not be treated by transanal local excision (included T1-T4
and N0/+ patients) randomized

Significant local control benefit, but no OS benefit to preop RT

RT had more beneficial effect with greater distance from anal verge

RT did improve 10-year OS for stage III patients with negative
margin: 50% vs. 40% (p=0.032)

 Dutch TME Trial: With better surgery (TME) RT has local control benefit,

Addition of CHT to LC-RT

FFCD 9203 (French � Gerard et al., JCO 2006)

 T3-T4 NX M0 rectal adenocarcinoma accessible by DRE randomized

 Preop RT 45 Gy/25 Fx
 Preop chemoRT with bolus 5-FU + LCV on weeks 1 and 5

 No improvement in OS (~67%) but significantly improved LC with chemoRT

 5-year LR 16% RT alone 8% with chemoRT (p=0.004)

EORTC 22921 (Bosset et al., NEJM 2006, JCO 2007)

 Concurrent chemotherapy given with long-course RT improves local control.

 25 Gy/5 Fx RT alone

 In TME era, RT still shows a LC benefit (not an OS benefit)

 11% local recurrence 5% with SC-RT at 10 years in Dutch TME

18% of patients in postop chemoRT group were down-staged at time of surgery

 Thought to have T3-4 or N+ on US/CT staging, but on surgery, had T1-2 N0 disease
 Probably overtreated ~20% of patients in preop chemoRT arm (pre-MRI era, now staging has improved)

Ngan et al., JCO 2012

2001-2006: 326 patients with T3 rectal adenocarcinoma
(US or MRI-staged) randomized

Powered to detect LR superiority of LC-CRT, and did not
show superiority of LC-CRT

No difference in 5-year DMFS (~30% in both arms), OS TROG 01.04 showed no significant
(~72%), or late toxicity (~7% G3+) improvement in local control with LC-CRT
Subset analysis showed cumulative local recurrence for vs. SC-RT. Again, saw trend in improved LC
distal tumors 12.5% with SC-RT vs. 0% with LC-CRT
 with LC-CRT, esp. for distal tumors.
Stockholm III: Preop Optimal Fx & Timing

Erlandsson et al., Lancet Oncol 2017

1998-2013: 840 patients with rectal adenocarcinoma planned for APR randomized

 25 Gy/5 Fx immediate TME within 1 week
 25 Gy/5 Fx delayed TME in 4-8 weeks
 50 Gy/25 Fx TME in 4-8 weeks (***no concurrent chemotherapy)

Adjuvant chemotherapy given to ~15% of patients

No significant difference in 1o endpoint of local recurrence between arms (~3-5% at 5 years)

 No significant difference in 5-year OS (~75%) or RFS (~65%) between arms either

pCR rate significantly improved in SC-RT delayed TME in 4-8 weeks

 pCR rate 0.3% vs. 10.4% vs. 2.2%

Postop complications were worse in SC-RT immediate TME (50% vs. 38% vs. 39%, p=0.075)

Stockholm III trial shows similar oncologic outcomes in all arms, but less postop complications and improved
pCR with SC-RT followed by delayed TME (longer time to respond to radiation). Potential to give preop chemo

 in the weeks between SC-RT and TME. Limited because no concurrent chemotherapy in LC-RT arm.
STELLAR: Preop SC TNT vs. LC-CRT

Jin et al., JCO 2022

2015-2018: 599 patients with distal or middle-third rectal cancer, cT3-4 and/or N+ randomized

 SC TNT: 25 Gy/5 Fx CAPEOX x4 cycles TME (6-8 weeks out) CAPEOX x2 cycles
 LC-CRT: 50.4 Gy/28 Fx + capecitabine TME (6-8 weeks out) CAPEOX x6 cycles

Required IMRT technique for radiotherapy
3-year DFS (1o endpoint) was non-inferior between arms

 3-year DFS 64.5% vs. 62.3% p<0.001, powered for noninferiority

3-year OS significantly improved in SC TNT arm � 86% vs. 75% (p=0.033)

 There was also higher compliance rate with LC-CRT vs. SC TNT (93% vs. 75% full dose)

pCR rate significantly higher with SC TNT � 22% vs. 12%, p=0.002
No significant difference in DFS (65% vs. 62%) or local control (8% vs. 11%) between arms
Worse G3-4 acute toxicity in SC TNT group (26.5% vs. 12.6%, p<0.001)

 STELLAR showed non-inferior DFS between SC TNT and LC-CRT.
 Also showed improved OS with SC TNT but interpret with caution.
RAPIDO: Preop SC TNT vs. LC-CRT

Bahadoer et al., Lancet Oncol 2021; Dijkstra et al., Ann Surg 2023

2011-2016: 920 patients with cT4a-b cN0/+ rectal adenocarcinoma randomized

 SC TNT: 25 Gy/5 Fx CAPEOX x6 cycles or FOLFOX4 x9 cycles TME
 LC-CRT: 50-50.4 Gy/25-28 Fx + capecitabine BID TME optional adj CAPEOX x8 or

 FOLFOX4 x12

1o endpoint of 3-year disease-related failure was significantly improved with SC TNT

 3-year disease related treatment failure 23.7% with SC-RT vs. 30.4% with LC-CRT (p=0.019)
 3-year LRF rate 8.3% in SC TNT arm vs. 6% in LC-CRT arm (p=0.12)
 3-year DM rate 20% in SC TNT vs. 27% in LC-CRT (p=0.0048)
 Rate of pCR 28% SC TNT vs. 14% LC-CRT (p<0.001)
 3-year OS 89% in both arms

Updated 5-year results showed worse local control with SC TNT

 LRF in 11.7% of SC TNT vs. 8.1% of LC-CRT (p=0.07)
 No significant difference in rate of R0 or R1 resection
 Among R0/R1 resection, LRR rate was 10% in SC TNT group vs. 6% in LC-CRT (p=0.027)

 RAPIDO showed worse 5-year local control with SC-RT for more locally
 advanced disease (cT4a-b N0/+)
How do we interpret these studies?

Polish, TROG, and Stockholm III showed no significant difference between SC-RT and LC-CRT.

STELLAR and RAPIDO compared [SC-RT + preop chemo TME] vs. [LC-RT TME adj chemo]

 Not a direct comparison between SC-RT and LC-CRT. These trials introduced TNT approach with SC-RT.
 Disparities in terms of how much chemotherapy the arms received and compliance rates could play into

 improved DFS outcomes in RAPIDO and improved OS outcomes in STELLAR.

In RAPIDO, 5-year local failure was worse with SC TNT vs. LC-CRT, even though pCR rate was
higher with SC TNT (pCR was also higher with SC TNT in STELLAR)

 pCR is NOT a surrogate for LRR or improved outcomes

RAPIDO also enrolled higher-risk patients � cT4 patients, more low-lying tumors
Major SC-RT leads to worse local control compared to LC-CRT
Takeaways
 Probably not true � RAPIDO enrolled higher risk patients!

 SC-RT is not always non-inferior to LC-CRT based on RAPIDO

 Consider LC-CRT for the following indications:

 T4 primary
 N+ (esp. extra-mesorectal nodes)
 Involved mesorectal fascia
 Low-lying tumors

 Lower 3rd of rectum, or sphincter/anal canal involvement

 Planned non-operative management

 Need more data on SC-RT W&W
 If any doubt as to whether patient will make it to surgery, safer to give LC-CRT

OPRA Phase II: Organ Preservation with TNT

Garcia-Aguilar et al., JCO 2022

2014-2020: 324 patients with stage II (T3-T4 N0) or stage III (any T, N+) rectal cancer
staged with MRI, colonoscopy, and CT CAP randomized

 INCT-CRT: Induction FOLFOX or CAPEOX LC-CRT (up to 54 Gy) with concurrent
 capecitabine or 5-FU

 CRT-CNCT: ChemoRT as above consolidation FOLFOX or CAPEOX

Patients restaged 8-12 weeks after TNT with DRE, flex sig, and MRI

 If cCR or near cCR watchful waiting
 TME for incomplete responder

74% of patients entered watchful waiting arm and 40% INCT and 27% in CNCT arm had
tumor regrowth requiring TME at a later timepoint (majority of cases within 1st two
years)

3-year DFS (~75%), LRFS (~95%), DMFS (~83%), and OS (~94%) very similar both arms of
TNT, NSS

 No significant difference in DFS with TNT and selective WW compared to historical controls

Organ preservation 41% in INCT arm vs. 53% in CNCT arm (p=0.01)

 Trial not powered for this analysis

 Phase II evidence that TNT in carefully selected patients, up to 50% can achieve organ preservation with high
 LRFS, DMFS, and OS rates. Those who do recur can be salvaged with TME (usually within first 2 years).
OPERA Phase III: BT vs. EBRT Boost

 Gerard et al., Lancet 2023

2015-2020: 148 patients with cT2-T3 cN0 or cN1 (<8mm nodes) adenocarcinoma of low-mid
rectum (tumor <5 cm diameter) � 45 Gy/25 Fx EBRT + concurrent capecitabine then randomized

 9 Gy/5 Fx EBRT boost
 90 Gy/3 Fx contact X-ray brachytherapy boost

After RT, partial responders received TME; complete responders could choose local excision vs.
wait & watch
1o endpoint of 3-year organ preservation rate significantly higher in X-ray BT boost arm

 3-year organ preservation 59% with EBRT boost vs. 81% with brachytherapy (p=0.0026)

 Even better for tumors <3 cm: 63% EBRT boost vs. 97% BT boost (p=0.012)
 For tumors >3 cm: 55% EBRT boost vs. 68% BT boost (p=0.11)

TME done in 38% EBRT boost vs. 18% BT boost (p=0.0042)
3-year local recurrence rate 23% EBRT boost vs. 15% BT boost (p=0.59); 3-year OS ~98% both
arms
Higher rates of acute proctitis and late bleeding related to telangiectasias in BT boost group

 OPERA trial showed excellent local control and organ preservation rates with CRT alone followed by
 brachytherapy boost. Few centers in US equipped with specific BT machine from this trial.

PROSPECT: Preop FOLFOX vs. CRT

Schrag et al., NEJM 2023

2012-2018: 1194 patients with cT2 N+ or cT3 N0/+ (MRI or CT/US staged) deemed by
surgeon to be eligible for neoadjuvant CRT sphincter-sparing TME were randomized 1:1

 FOLFOX6 x6 cycles TME if 20% decrease in size of tumor on imaging, proctoscopy, and
 exam adjuvant CHT (75% received, with 80% receiving FOLFOX 6 cycles)

 If unable to get at least 5 cycles of CHT, received CRT (94.9% received at least 5 cycles of CHT)
 If unable to get R0 resection, recommended adjuvant chemoradiotherapy (ultimately 10% received )with consideration

 for adjuvant FOLFOX x6 cycles

 Chemoradiotherapy 50.4 Gy/28 Fx with 5-FU or capecitabine TME optional 8 cycles adj
 FOLFOX (78% received)

5-year DFS was non-inferior with chemo alone; OS and LR similar between arms

 5-year DFS 80.8% FOLFOX vs. 78.6% CRT (NSS, p=0.004 for noninferiority)
 5-year OS 89.5% FOLFOX vs. 90.2% CRT (NSS)
 5-year LR 1.8% FOLFOX vs. 1.6% CRT (NSS)

Rate of R0 resection 90.4% FOLFOX vs. 91.2% CRT

G3+ toxicity � neoadj tx 41% FOLFOX vs. 23% CRT; postop tx 26% FOLFOX vs. 33% CRT

 PROSPECT demonstrated that for cT2 N+ or cT3 N0/+ rectal cancers eligible for sphincter-sparing surgery,
 preop FOLFOX direct surgery if 20% response was noninferior to preop chemoRT.
PD-1 Blockade for Stage II-III, dMMR

Cercek et al., NEJM 2022

Phase II, single-arm study of 12 patients

Stage II-III rectal cancer with dMMR (loss of expression of MLH1, MSH1, MSH6, or
PMS2 by IHC)

 No patients in the study had Lynch syndrome

Treatment plan: Dostarlimab (PD-1 inhibitor) LC-CRT with capecitabine TME

 Dostarlimab: 500 mg/m2 administered q3 weeks x9 cycles (6 months)

Tumor response evaluated on MRI, PET, endoscopy, and DRE ~q3mo

At median 12-month follow-up, 100% of patients had sustained complete clinical
response to dostarlimab, and had not required CRT or TME

 81% of patients had resolution of symptoms within 9 weeks of initiating dostarlimab

No G3+ AEs � saw G1-2 dermatitis, pruritus, and fatigue

Phase II and small sample size, but shows that dMMR rectal cancers have
 excellent response to neoadjuvant ICI. Need close monitoring, but may

 avoid local therapy altogether!

Short-course vs. long-course RT?

PROS SC-RT (25 Gy/5 Fx) LC-RT (50.4 Gy/28 Fx)

 Less immunosuppression Start some systemic tx with RT
 Fewer cytopenias Trend to improved LC for distant tumors
 More convenient Stronger data for LC-RT CHT W&W
 Less time to full-dose chemo

CONS Little to no downstaging if immediate SGY Longer treatment
 May increase LR risk (advanced tumors) Higher risk of GI and hematologic toxicity
 Less experience in US
 Less data for SC-RT W&W

Resectable Arterial Venous

Borderline No contact with CA, SMA, or CHA No contact with SMV or PV
resectable 180o contact but no contour irregularity
 Pancreatic head/uncinate process:
 - Contacts CHA but does not extend to CA or >180o contact with SMV or PV
 180o contact with contour irregularity or
 CHA bifurcation thrombosis need suitable vessel proximal and
 - 180o contact with SMA distal to site to allow for reconstruction
 - Any contact with variant arterial anatomy

 (based on degree of involvement)

Unresectable Pancreatic body/tail: Complete occlusion of SMV or PV, not amenable to
 - 180o contact with CA resection & primary reconstruction

 >180o contact with SMA or CA SMV = superior mesenteric vein
 Contact with CA & aortic involvement PV = portal vein

 CA = celiac artery
 SMA = superior mesenteric artery
 CHA = common hepatic artery

1994-2000: 289 patients s/p resection of pancreatic ACA randomized 2x2
to CRT vs. no CRT and CHT vs. no CHT for total of 4 arms:

 of particular benefit to node negative patients (abstract
Step 1 results: no OS benefit to addition of
erlotinib only)

 3-year OS 39% in both arms, median OS 28 vs. 30
 months (p=0.62)

Step 2 results (abstract): 5-year DFS improved
with chemoRT, but no OS benefit

 Improved results seen in node-negative patients
 vs. node-positive

 Multiple flaws with study design, no set backbone CHT,
 changed statistical design and endpoints
ASCO Clinical Practice Guidelines for
Potentially Curable Pancreatic ACA

Khorana et al., JCO 2019

Recommendations for adjuvant CHEMOTHERAPY
All patients with resected pancreatic cancer who have not had neoadjuvant therapy should receive
adjuvant chemotherapy:

- FOLFIRINOX preferred
- Gemcitabine/capecitabine acceptable alternative
- Gemcitabine or 5-fluorouracil monotherapy acceptable too

Recommendations for adjuvant CHEMORADIOTHERAPY
Consider adjuvant chemoradiotherapy for patients with R1 resection and/or node-positive disease
(positive node/positive margin)

- Standard dose is 45-50.4 Gy/25-28 Fx
- Concurrent 5-FU/capecitabine or gemcitabine

Borderline Resectable Disease

CHT REEVALUATE CRT REEVALUATE SURGE RY
PREOPANC-1: Upfront SGY vs. Neoadj CRT

Versteijne et al., JCO 2022

2013-2017: 246 patients with resectable (~55%) & borderline
resectable (~45%) pancreatic adenoCA randomized

 Upfront surgery gem x6c
 Neoadjuvant chemoRT: 3 cycles gemcitabine with RT 36 Gy/15 Fx

 with 2nd cycle SGY in 4-6 weeks gem x4c

Improved R0 resection rate in chemoRT group

 R0 resection 41% neoadjuvant chemoRT vs. 28% upfront SGY
 (p=0.025)

Improved OS in neoadjuvant chemoRT group

 5-year OS 21% neoadjuvant chemoRT vs. 7% upfront SGY
 (p=0.025)

 Subgroup analysis showed benefit in both resectable & borderline
 resectable patients, with greater benefit in borderline resectable

 PREOPANC-1: OS benefit to neoadjuvant chemoRT for resectable and borderline resectable patients.
Alliance A021501: Neoadj CHT vs. chemo/RT

Katz et al., JAMA 2022

2017-2019: phase II RCT of 126 patients with borderline resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma randomized

 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX q2 weeks SGY FOLFOX6 x4 cycles
 7 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX q2 weeks pancreas SBRT 33-40 Gy/5 Fx SGY FOLFOX6 x4 cycles

 Fiducial placement was required for pancreas SBRT
 88% patients received SBRT (33-40 Gy/5 Fx) & 12% received 25 Gy/5 Fx

Arms imbalanced � several factors including age, CA 19-9 levels, hepatic artery resection, and disease progression
before completion of therapy all supported CHT arm (Chuong et al., 2022)

58% of patients in chemo arm and 51% in RT arm underwent surgery

 R0 resection rate 88% with chemo vs. 74% with RT ( 2 patients had pCR, both were in RT arm)

RT arm was closed early after interim analysis

1o endpoint of 18-month OS numerically worse in RT group: 67% chemo vs. 47% RT

 Note: RT arm closed early, so not adequately powered for analysis
 RT having worse OS than chemo arm is not statistically valid

 Alliance A021501: Flawed study and underpowered for 1o endpoint of 18-month OS, but authors
 concluded worse OS with neoadjuvant chemo/RT vs. neoadjuvant chemo alone.
CONKO-007: Neoadj. CHT vs. CHT CRT

Fietkau et al., ASCO 2022 Abstract

2013-2021: Phase III RCT of 525 patients with borderline and unresectable pancreatic ACA received
induction chemo x3 months (FOLFIRINOX x6c q2 weeks or gemcitabine x3c q4 weeks)

Then randomized

 Continue same chemo for another 3 months
 ChemoRT: 50.4 Gy/28 Fx + gem 300 mg/m� weekly, followed by 1 cycle of Gem 1000 mg/m� at d1, 8, 15

ChemoRT arm improved pCR & CRM- R0 resection rates, but not total R0 resection (1o endpoint)

 Total R0 resection rate 18% chemo alone vs. 25% chemo + CRT (p=0.14)
 R0 CRM- resection (1 mm clear margin) 9% chemo alone vs. 20% chemo + CRT (p=0.0015)
 pCR rate 0% chemo alone vs. 6% chemo + CRT (p=0.0013)
 R1 resection rate 10% chemo alone vs. 3% chemo + CRT (p=0.0085)

Similar 1 and 2-year PFS and OS rates between arms

 2-year PFS ~20% and 2-year OS ~34%

 CONKO-007: manuscript pending, but shows increased pCR and R0 CRM- resection rates with chemo +
 CRT compared to chemo alone in era of FOLFIRINOX.
PREOPANC-2: FOLFIRINOX vs. Gem-CRT

Koerkamp et al., ESMO 2023 Abstract

Unresectable Disease
LAP07: Gem � Erlotinib � CRT

Hammel et al., JAMA 2016

2008-2011: 442 patients with locally advanced pancreatic cancer
randomized

 1st randomization: gemcitabine +/- erlotinib then if disease free after 4
 months next phase

 2nd randomization: continue same chemotherapy for 2 months vs. chemoRT
 54 Gy + capecitabine

No OS benefit to erlotinib

 Median OS 14 mo gem alone vs. 12 mo gem + erlotinib (p=0.09)

No OS benefit to chemoRT, but improved locoregional control

 Median OS 15 mo chemo alone vs. 17 mo chemoRT (p=0.83)
 Locoregional progression 46% chemo alone vs. 32% chemoRT (p=0.04)

 LAP07: ChemoRT has locoregional control benefit for unresectable disease.
Pancreas SBRT

25 Gy/1 Fx SBRT 29% 1-year duodenal toxicity (Chang et al., Cancer 2009)

 Retrospective review of 77 patients with unresectable pancreatic cancer treated with 25 Gy/1 Fx
 Isolated 1-year local failure 5%, 1-year PFS 9%, 1-year OS 21%
 29% risk of duodenal toxicity at 1 year, unacceptably high

5 fraction SBRT reduces GI toxicity (Pollom et al., IJROBP 2014)

 Retrospective review of 167 patients treated with single fraction vs. 5-fraction pancreas SBRT
 1-year G3+ GI toxicity 12% in single-fraction group vs. 6% in multi-fraction group

 5-fraction pancreas SBRT reduces GI toxicity compared to single-fraction.
Pancreas SBRT Dose Constraints

Simulation:

 Ideal to have fiducials placed prior to simulation, if not can use clips or stent (fiducials preferred)
 NPO x4 hours
 Motion management with breath-hold or abdominal compression with 4D-CT

Common pancreas SBRT dose fractionation:

 40 Gy/5 Fx to gross tumor + tumor-vessel interface
 25 Gy/5 Fx to elective volume including celiac, SMA, and peripancreatic nodes

Dose constraints:

 Duodenum, stomach, small bowel (contoured separately): V33 < 1cc, V20 Gy < 3 cc, V15 Gy < 9 cc, 0.03 cc < 36-38
 Gy.

 Consider 3 mm PRV constraint of V35 Gy < 1-2 cc

 Esophagus: V32.5 Gy <5 cc, V20 <3 cc, 0.03 cc < 38 Gy
 Colon: V32.5 Gy <20cc, 0.03 cc < 52.5 Gy
 Kidney: D200 cc < 17.5 Gy, V12 Gy < 75%

Note: SBRT contraindicated for tumors with duodenal perforation!
Future Directions � Dose Escalation?

Several retrospective/prospective single-arm studies have investigated dose escalation

 Possible benefit to dose escalation, although short follow-up

SMART Trial (Parikh et al., IJROBP 2023) = Stereotactic MRI-guided Adaptive RT

 Induction FOLFIRINOX vs. Gem/Abraxane MRI-guided SBRT 50 Gy/5 Fx

 acute GI toxicity possibly related to SMART)
 32% went on to get surgery (75% of these had BRPC)
 1-year OS after SMART 65%
Ongoing NRG GI-011/LAP100

Sanford et. al � ongoing NRG study

 Ongoing NRG GI-011/LAP100
 investigating dose-escalated RT
 (75 Gy/25 Fx or 50 Gy/5 Fx SBRT)

Anatomy

Pancreas head located anterior to L1-L2

Divided into head, neck, and tail

 Uncinate process: hook-shaped inferior

Venous drainage via portal system

 Posteriorly arising tumors may spread to

Pancreatic duct and accessory duct join
common bile duct and enter duodenum
via Sphincter of Oddi at Ampulla of Vater
Anatomy

Celiac trunk: T11/T12

 Common hepatic artery:

 Proper hepatic artery: supplies liver
 Gastroduodenal artery: supplies stomach and pancreas
 Right inferior phrenic artery: supplies the diaphragm

 Left gastric artery: supplies stomach & lesser omentum
 Splenic artery: supplies spleen, pancreas, stomach

Superior mesenteric artery: L1

 Inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery: supplies pancreas
 and duodenum

 Middle colic artery: Supplies transverse colon
 Right colic artery: Supplies ascending colon
 Ileocolic artery: Supplies ileum, cecum, appendix
Lymphatic Drainage

Regional nodes:

 Peripancreatic

 Superior mesenteric
 Porta hepatic

 Tumors of pancreatic head and neck drain

 Tumors of body and tail drain along common

Simultaneous dilatation of both pancreatic

GI.Rectal Cancer Overview:
Rectal Cancer Overview

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
 Rectal Cancer

Colorectal cancer is the 3rd most
common type of cancer in both men &
women (~4-5% lifetime risk)
� Also 3rd most common cause of

 cancer death
Increased incidence in younger adults
over last decade
Rectal cancer refers to cancers arising
from the rectum (rectosigmoid
junction to dentate line/anorectal ring)
� ~40% of colorectal cancer
Colon cancer & rectal cancer are
managed as two separate diseases
with different treatment paradigms
� No major role for radiotherapy in

 colon cancer
Unlike colon, rectum remains relatively
fixed in pelvis and is amenable to
radiotherapy
Objectives Treatment Paradigm

Background � Presentation & Workup Evolution of rectal cancer management
 Surgery: LAR vs. APR
 Screening guidelines Chemotherapy regimens
 Presentation Radiotherapy
 Workup Wait & watch approach
 Anatomy Metastatic rectal cancer
 Pathology
 Molecular testing
 Rectal MRI

Staging

 AJCC 8th Edition
 Patterns of Spread
Background �
Presentation & Workup
NCCN Screening Guidelines: Average Risk

For average risk asymptomatic patients, ACS & USPSTF recommend screening starting at age 45

 USPSTF recommends screening ages 45-75

Options for screening:

 Colonoscopy = gold standard

 Every 10 years if average risk, or consider sooner based on nature of polyps/lesions found

 Stool-based testing with colonoscopy within 9 months if positive

 Fecal occult blood test (FOBT) q1 year if negative
 Fecal immunochemical test (FIT) q1 year if negative
 Multitargeted stool DNA (mt-sDNA) test q3 years if negative

 Flexible sigmoidoscopy with colonoscopy if polyps
 CT colonography (CTC) q5 years with colonoscopy if 3 polyps or polyps 10 mm
NCCN Screening Guidelines: Increased Risk

Risk Factor Screening Recommendation

First-degree relative with CRC at any age, Colonoscopy at age 40 or 10 years before age of onset of

or advanced adenoma CRC/adenoma (whichever is EARLIER), repeat q5 years

Personal history of IBD (Crohn's or Colonoscopy 8 years after dx, repeat q1-5 years
ulcerative colitis)

Personal history of chemotherapy Age 35 or 10 years after CHT (whichever is later), repeat q5
 years

Personal history of abdominopelvic Age 30 or 5 years after RT (whichever is later), repeat q5 years
irradiation

Personal history of cystic fibrosis If transplant: colonoscopy age 30 or within 2 years of
 transplant, repeat q3-5 years
 If no transplant: colonoscopy age 40 q3-5 years
Hereditary Syndromes

Lynch Syndrome/HNPCC (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer)

 Mutations in mismatch repair genes (most commonly MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2
 Leads to microsatellite instability
 Increased risk of endometrial, ovarian, stomach, small bowel, hepatobiliary system, brain, renal pelvis,

 and ureteral cancers
 Screening colonoscopy q1-2 years starting at age 20-25 years

FAP (familial adenomatous polyposis)

 Autosomal dominant mutation in APC (adenomatous polyposis coli) gene on chromosome 5
 Variants include Gardener's (sarcomas, osteomas, desmoid tumors) and Turcot's (GBM,

 medulloblastoma)
 Elective colectomy or proctocolectomy at onset of polyps
Presentation of Rectal Cancer

Hematochezia = most common symptom Weight loss
 Fatigue
 Bright red blood per rectum Abdominal pain or bloating
 May range in severity Urinary symptoms if very locally advanced

Change in bowel habits

 Diarrhea or constipation

Reduced stool caliber

Tenesmus
Inadequate emptying

Pain
Workup

Complete history and physical

 Inquire about nature and duration of symptoms, personal history, family history, screening history
 DRE � evaluate tumor size, location, mobility, sphincter function; evaluate prostate
 Pelvic exam in women

Labs � CBC, CMP, CEA; PSA in men; pregnancy test in women of reproductive age

Pathology � colonoscopy with biopsies

 Histology
 MSI/MMR testing

Imaging

 CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out distant metastases and lymph node involvement
 MRI rectum with contrast to evaluate local extent of disease and lymphadenopathy

Procedures

 DRE with proctoscopy � important and most accurate method to assess distance from anal verge
 Very locally advanced tumors (e.g., bowel obstruction or fistula) should have bowel diversion with colostomy

 prior to neoadjuvant treatment
Anatomy

Rectum extends approximately 15 cm superiorly from the anal verge

 Clinical trials include tumors up to 12-16 cm superior to anal verge

MSI/MMR Testing for All Patients

All patients with rectal cancer should have MSI or MMR screening

 MSI-H (Microsatellite Instability-High) dMMR (Mismatch Repair Deficiency)

� Testing via PCR or NGS panel � Testing via IHC for LOSS of protein expression
� Instability in >30% of loci (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2)

 � Abnormal MLH1 check for BRAF V600E mutation

MSI-H is the phenotype (see mutations in the repetitive DNA sequences, or microsatellites)
dMMR is the underlying cause (loss of MMR protein expression)
MSI-H/dMMR respond to checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy more than chemotherapy

 Occurs in 15-20% of stage II-III CRC, associated with better prognosis

MSI-L/MSS and pMMR generally do not derive meaningful benefit from immunotherapy

Usually done with next-generation sequencing (NGS) panel

Surgery

Transanal local excision for small T1 tumors

 T1 tumors only! TME

 <3 cm in size, <30% circumference, >3 mm margin,
 within 8 cm of anal verge

 Moderately to well differentiated, no PNI or LVSI

 No clinical suspicion of nodal involvement

 Endoscopic procedure, full thickness rectal wall
 excision with goal of 10 mm excision margin

Total mesorectal excision for all others Affleck et al., 2022

 Excision of rectum & mesorectum (surrounding
 fatty tissue which includes mesorectal lymph

 Two techniques

 Low anterior resection (sphincter-sparing)
 Abdominoperineal resection (not sphincter-sparing)

Chemotherapy � Common Regimens

FOLFOX

 5-fluorouracil (5-FU): inhibits thymidylate synthase, preventing dTMP synthesis (inhibits DNA repair)

 Hand-foot syndrome; nausea/GI upset
 Given as IV bolus then continuous infusion over 46-48 hours

 Leucovorin: enhances therapeutic effect of 5-FU by increasing binding of drug to thymidylate synthase
 Oxaliplatin: DNA crosslinking agent

 Neuropathy

CAPEOX

 Capecitabine: oral form of 5-FU given BID days 1-14 q3 weeks
 Oxaliplatin still given IV

***For dMMR/MSI-H prefer neoadjuvant/definitive checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (Pembro, Nivo, Dostar)

Metastatic setting

 pMMR/MSS � FOLFOX/CAPEOX � bevacizumab
 dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD mutation � checkpoint inhibitor IO as first-line

 Ipi/nivo, Pembro, or Dostarlimab
Radiation

Long-course preoperative chemoradiotherapy

 RT to involved mesorectum with concurrent 5-FU (IV � bolus or infusional) or Capecitabine (PO)

 Capecitabine dose: 875 mg/m2 PO BID given M-F with radiation (only on days receiving radiation)
 5-FU dose: 225 mg/m2 daily or delivered as continuous infusion

 Usually 45 Gy to elective nodal volume with 50.4-54 Gy boost to involved mesorectum + involved nodes
 May proceed to surgery 5-12 weeks after preop chemoradiotherapy

Short-course preoperative radiotherapy

 25 Gy/5 Fx
 RAPIDO trial has now shown worse local control with SCRT vs. long-course chemoRT (10% vs. 6%, SS) for

 more advanced (e.g., T4) tumors, or low-lying tumors
 Studies looking at immediate surgery (within 1 week) or delayed (4-8 weeks) possibly more room for

 downstaging (and preop chemo) with delayed surgery
3D-CRT or IMRT Planning

3D-CRT or IMRT is acceptable for radiotherapy planning

RTOG 0822 (Hang et al., IJROBP 2015): no decrease in toxicity with IMRT vs. historical control

 Phase II trial that used IMRT with 3D boost and compared toxicity to RTOG 0247 as historical control
 Used 3D-CRT + CAPOX SGY FOLFOX (criticized for concurrent oxaliplatin increasing toxicity)

Coverage (Valenini et al., Radiother & Oncol 2016)

 Entire mesorectum + presacral, internal iliac, and mesorectal nodes 45 Gy
 If extending to anterior structures (e.g., bladder, reproductive organs) include external iliac nodes
 Involved mesorectum (and involved nodes) boost to 50.4 Gy (may consider 54 Gy)

3D considerations

 Consider simulating prone with belly board to minimize RT to small bowel (historical standard)
 3-field or 4-field box
3D Planning � Field Borders

AP/PA Fields

 Sup: L5-S1 interspace
 Lat: 2cm lateral to widest point of bony

 pelvis
 Inf: 3-5 cm from inferior extent of

 primary, or (for distal tumors) from
 anal verge marked by BB at sim

Lateral fields

 Sup/inf: same as above
 Ant: mid-symphysis (or 1 cm anterior to

 pubic symphysis if including
 external iliacs)
 Post: 2 cm posterior to presacrum (for
 T3) or 1 cm posterior to entire
 sacrum (for T4)
"Wait & Watch"

Long-course chemoradiotherapy adjuvant FOLFOX or CAPEOX "wait & watch"

H&P, CEA, DRE, protcotscopy or flex sig q3 months x2 years, then q6 months x5 years
MRI rectum q6 months x3 years
CT CAP q6 months x5 years
Colonoscopy at 1 year after completing treatment

 Repeat in 1 year if advanced adenoma, or in 3 years if no advanced adenoma, then q5 years)
Metastatic Rectal Cancer

Select patients with oligometastatic rectal cancer with lung/liver-only metastases may be
treated with curative intent
Dutch M1 Study (Phase II) � Bisschop et al., Ann Surg Oncol 2017

 2006-2010: phase II study of 50 patients with metastatic rectal cancer & lung or liver only metastases
 that were resectable or ablatable (84% had liver, 10% lung, 6% both)

 Treated with RT 25 Gy/5 Fx CAPEOX + Bevacizumab for up to 6 cycles SGY radical tx to mets
 2-year OS was 80%
 At 8 years, 32% survival and 28% disease-free

 Oligometastatic rectal cancer with liver or lung only metastases amenable to surgery or SBRT �
 treatment with curative intent:

 � CHT RT TME/surveillance SGY or SBRT to metastases
 � RT CHT TME/surveillance SGY or SBRT to metastases

 317 patients with T2-T4a bladder cancer randomized to SGY vs. neoadjuvant DD-MVAC SGY

Bladder Cancer
Overview

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Bladder Cancer

2nd most common GU malignancy

Median age of dx is 70 years

76% male

Highest rates in North
America/Western Europe

90% are urothelial carcinomas

~70% present with superficial disease
(stage I - NMIBC) managed with
TURBT +/- intravesical CHT

Muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC)
managed with cystectomy &
perioperative systemic therapy, or
bladder preservation with chemoRT
Objectives Treatment Paradigm

Background NMIBC

 Presentation Surgery for NMIBC
 Risk factors Intravesical therapy
 Anatomy
 Workup MIBC
 Pathology
 Systemic therapy
Staging Radical cystectomy
 Urinary diversion
 TNM Staging (AJCC Version 8) Selective bladder preservation with definitive chemoRT

 Locally advanced or metastatic
Background
Presentation

Hematuria � gross or microscopic

 Gross hematuria has 10-20% risk of bladder tumor

Obstructive/irritative bladder symptoms

Pain

 Pelvic or back pain if locally advanced disease
Risk Factors Schistosomiasis squamous cell carcinoma

Smoking 50% of cases Chronic inflammation

Chemical exposures Chronic UTIs/cystitis
 Stones
 Industrial aromatic amines
 Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons Radiation exposure
 Hair dyes
 Chlorinated water
 Arsenic

Drugs

 Phenacetin-containing analgesics
 Cyclophosphamide
Anatomy

Divided into 3 sections

 Body � above ureteral orifices
 Trigone � between ureteral openings & neck
 Neck � inferior most portion of bladder

Bladder is anchored to anterior abdominal wall
by the urachus

Primary lymph node drainage

 External iliac
 Internal iliac
 Obturator
 Perivesical
 Presacral nodes
Layers of Bladder Wall

Location Layer Description

Innermost Urothelium Epithelial lining made of transitional cells bounded by thin basement membrane
.
. Lamina propria Thick layer of fibroelastic connective tissue
. Detrusor muscle Smooth muscle arranged in longitudinal (inner), circular (middle), and longitudinal (outer) layers
Outermost
Workup

H&P

Labs � urine cytology, CBC, CMP, ALP

 Cytology has poor sensitivity (34%) but high specificity (>98%)

Cystoscopy � office cystoscopy (no anesthesia)

Biopsy � EUA & cystoscopy with maximal TURBT of suspicious lesions

 Include muscle to assess for muscle invasion
 Also biopsy adjacent areas (assess field defect/CIS) and biopsy prostate

Imaging

 CT urography of abdomen and pelvis with and without contrast with excretory imaging (get before TURBT)
 Consider MR urography
 CT chest for staging in patients with MIBC
 Can consider PET CT if high-risk MIBC to evaluate for distant metastases
 Neuroimaging if symptomatic or high-risk (e.g., small cell histology)
TURBT for staging Therapeutic TURBT

TURBT = transurethral resection of bladder tumor TURBT alone is therapeutic for T1 tumors (NMIBC)
 - Repeat in 6 weeks after initial TURBT if T1
 Diagnostic TURBT
 TURBT/maximal complete and safe TURBT is
 Need adequate resection with muscle in specimen preliminary component of bladder preservation
 - Can omit muscle only if low-grade Ta disease (chemoRT)
 - Visually complete TURBT is associated with
 Papillary appearing tumor (likely NMIBC) � repeat TURBT in 6 weeks if:
 - Incomplete initial resection improved outcomes in nonmetastatic settings
 - No muscle in original specimen & high-grade
 - Lesion 3 cm or multifocal
 - Any T1 lesion

 Sessile or invasive tumor (likely MMIBC) � repeat TURBT if:
 - No muscle in original specimen
 - Any T1 lesion
 - First resection did not allow adequate staging
 - Incomplete resection & considering bladder preservation
Pathology

Urothelial carcinoma (>90%)

 Aggressive variants � micropapillary, plasmacytoid,
 nested, sarcomatoid) � may warrant more aggressive tx

Squamous cell carcinoma (~3%)

 A/w schistosomiasis (Schistoma haematobium)
 Make up majority of cancers in endemic areas

Adenocarcinoma (~1%)

 Urachal tumors are often adenocarcinomas, better
 outcomes than non-urachal adenocarcinomas

Small cell carcinoma (~1%)

All others (<1%)

 Sarcomas
 Lymphomas
 Melanoma
 Metastases
Staging
Bladder Cancer Staging

Ta = noninvasive papillary carcinoma N0 N1: single N2: multiple N3: common
 pelvic node pelvic nodes iliac nodes
Tis = carcinoma in situ: "flat tumor"
 IIIB

T2 T2a Inner half of muscularis propria II

 T2b Outer half of muscularis propria

T3 T3a Microscopic perivesicular invasion IIIA

 T3b Macroscopic perivesicular invasion

T4 T4a Invades prostate, SV, uterus, vagina

 T4b Invades pelvic or abdominal wall IVA

M1 M1a LN mets beyond common iliacs

 M1b Non-LN metastases IVB

 Initial TURBT for all

 NMIBC risk-adapted adjuvant treatment

 Low - Papillary urothelial neoplasm of low Observation

Management -
NMIBC cm & solitary

 recurrence within 1 year (CHT vs. BCG)

Active Surveillance

Systematic review of surveillance for seminomatous GCT (Groll et al., CROH 2007 � Toronto)

 17% relapse after orchiectomy and active surveillance for stage I seminoma, most within 1st year

 9% of relapses were >2 years

 CSS is still ~100% due to highly effective salvage therapy

Danish retrospective cohort study (Mortenson et al., Eur Urol 2014)

 18.8% (369/1964) patients had relapse
 Median time to relapse 13.7 months

 73.4% relapsed during the first 2 years, 22.2% years 3-5, and 4.3% after year 5

 15-year DSS 99.3% and OS 91.6%

 Retrospective data shows ~15-20% relapse rates with active surveillance for stage I seminoma, with
 excellent DSS & OS due to effective salvage therapies.
Sunnybrook: Surveillance Salvage Tx

Choo et al., IJROBP 2005

1985-1996: prospective, single-arm study of 88 patients treated for stage I seminoma with
orchiectomy surveillance
10 & 15-year actuarial relapse-free rate = 80%

 17/88 patients had relapse (71 relapse-free); 15/17 relapses were below the diaphragm

Patients with relapse were treated with salvage RT or CHT

 14 received salvage RT only 1 patient had relapse and received salvage CHT
 3 received salvage CHT

Stage I seminoma can be safely treated with surveillance after orchiectomy. Reserve RT or CHT for salvage
 of relapse with excellent outcomes based on prospective data.

randomized

Pooled analysis of 3049 patients treated on TE10, TE18, and TE19

Overall case-specific survival = 99.8%

Testicular arteries arise directly from abdominal aorta
Right testicular vein joins IVC inferior to right renal vein
Left testicular vein joins left renal vein

Natural History

~85% of seminomas present with localized disease
Risk for relapse after orchiectomy:

 ~12% for stage I seminoma with size <3 cm, and ~20% if size 3 cm
 If no relapse in the first 2 years, risk of relapse in the next 5 years is lower: ~3.9% & 5.6%, respectively

90% of nodal relapses occur in the para-aortic lymph nodes ("landing zone")

 10% also have positive pelvic LN

Nodal crossover may occur from right to left (~15%), but rarely from left to right
Late distant relapses are possible
Treatment Paradigm
 Initial treatment: radical inguinal orchiectomy with high ligation
 Subsequent strategy is tailored to stage

Surgery

Radical inguinal orchiectomy = gold standard for diagnosis & initial management

If patient has testicular mass with rapidly rising -hCG or AFP, metastatic disease on imaging,
and symptoms related to disseminated disease can immediately initiate chemotherapy
without orchiectomy or tissue diagnosis

 Orchiectomy should then be performed upon completion of chemotherapy

Select patients may be candidates for testis-sparing surgery (partial orchiectomy) through
inguinal approach counsel on higher local recurrence risk & need for continued surveillance

 Synchronous bilateral GCTs
 Solitary testis or solitary functioning testis with adequate function (i.e., androgen/sperm production)

RPLND is not indicated for pure seminomas

 RPLND is indicated for select NSGCTs

 there is still a high

Stage I disease � single agent carboplatin AUC 7 x1-2 cycles is an option

Stage II-III disease � BEP x3 cycles or EP x4 cycles

Gyn.Cervical Cancer Overview:
Cervical Cancer
Overview

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Cervical Cancer

4th most common malignancy in
women GLOBALLY

 Incidence in US is decreasing due to
 screening (Pap smear) & vaccinations

Median age of dx = 50

 Most commonly diagnosed in
 women ages 35-44

Most commonly squamous cell
carcinoma

Risk factor = HPV (>95%)

 HPV 16 & 18 (~70%)
 HPV 31 & 33

Racial disparities

 Death rate in Black women & Native
 American women is 65% higher than
 that in white women

 SEER Data, 2013-2019
Objectives

Presentation, Workup, & Staging

 Presentation
 Risk factors
 Anatomy
 Lymph node drainage
 Pathology
 Workup

Management Overview

 Surgical techniques
 Postoperative RT � chemo
 Definitive chemoRT
Presentation, Workup,
& Staging
Presentation USPSTF Cervical Cancer Screening Guidelines

Often asymptomatic, detected on screening Age 21-29 Cervical cytology alone q3 years

If symptomatic Age 30-65 Cervical cytology alone q3 years, hrHPV
 testing alone q5 years, OR hrHPV testing
 Abnormal vaginal discharge + cervical cytology q5 years
 Post-coital bleeding
 Dyspareunia Age >65 If no history of moderate/severe
 Pelvic pain dysplasia, 3 negative Paps or 2 negative
 cotests in a row no further screening
Prevention:
9-valent vaccination for children 11-12 years old Note: having HPV vaccination does not alter screening recommendations
Catch-up vaccination through age 26
Adults 27-45 may be vaccinated as indicated
Risk Factors

HPV (associated with >90% of cases)

 HPV 16 & 18

 A/w ~70% of cases
 Other cancer causing strains: 31, 33, 45, 52, 58

Smoking

Immunocompromised status

 AIDS
 Transplant

Young age at first intercourse

Multiple sexual partners

Multiparity

Low SES

Diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure in utero

 A/w clear cell carcinoma of cervix/vagina
Anatomy

Cervix = lower part of uterus, cylindrical in shape

 Endocervical canal lined by columnar epithelium
 Ectocervix lined by squamous epithelium

Squamocolumnar junction = located at external os

 Most common site for carcinogenesis

Cardinal ligament attaches cervix to pelvic side wall

 Broad ligament attaches uterus to pelvic side wall
 Uterosacral ligament attaches low uterus to sacrum
Lymph Node Drainage

Drains through surrounding ligaments

 Cardinal, broad, & uterosacral

Drains to regional lymphatics

 Presacral
 Obturator
 Internal & external iliac
 Common iliac
 Paraaortic

Common sites of distant metastasis

 Lungs, liver, bones
 Supraclavicular LNs (via thoracic duct)
Pathology Less common histologies

Squamous cell carcinoma (70-75%) Clear-cell adenocarcinoma
 Small cell
Adenocarcinoma (20-25%) Neuroendocrine
 Sarcoma (rhabdomyosarcoma in young
 Higher incidence in younger patients
 Presents with larger tumors adolescents)
 Melanoma
 "barrel cervix" Adenoid cystic carcinoma

 Higher risk of local failure
 Pap-screening less sensitive

 HPV testing helps improve

Adenosquamous (5%)
Workup

H&P with focus on gynecologic history and pelvic and abdominal exam

 Examine supraclavicular & inguinal lymph nodes

Labs

 CBC, CMP, consider HIV testing
 Pregnancy test!

Procedures/pathology

 Colposcopy with cervical biopsy (LEEP)
 Cold knife cone biopsy (CKC) if cervical biopsy inadequate � can be definitive tx if early-stage disease
 EUE with cystoscopy & rectosigmoidsocopy if advanced disease, or bladder/rectal extension present

 May need ureteral stent

Imaging

 Pelvic MRI with and without contrast & vaginal gel to delineate LOCAL extent of disease
 PET/CT for NODAL/distant staging
 CXR, barium enema, skeletal X-ray, and intravenous pyelogram also allowed
2018 FIGO Staging

FIGO Stage Description

I: confined to cervix IA IA1: Stromal invasion 3 mm depth
 IA2: Stromal invasion >3 and 5 mm depth
 IB
 IB1: 5 mm depth of stromal invasion, invasive carcinoma <2 cm
II: invades beyond uterus, but not IIA IB2: Invasive carcinoma >2 and 4 cm
pelvic sidewall or lower vagina IB3: Invasive carcinoma >4 cm

 IIB IIA1: Invasive carcinoma 4 cm and extends beyond uterus
 IIA2: Invasive carcinoma >4 cm and extends beyond uterus
III: invades lower 1/3 vagina or IIIA
 Invades parametria but not up to pelvic wall
pelvic sidewall, causes IIIB
hydronephrosis, or LN involvement Invades lower third of vagina, no extension to pelvic sidewall
 IIIC
 Extends to pelvic sidewall or causes hydronephrosis/non-functioning kidney
IV: invades adjacent organs or IVA
 IIIC1: pelvic lymph node metastasis only
distant metastasis IVB IIIC2: paraaortic lymph node metastasis

 Invades adjacent organs (e.g., bladder, bowel)

 Spread to distant organs
Management Overview
Summary of Early-stage disease (IA1, IA2, IB1, IB2, IIA) surgical approach
Management
 Indications for adjuvant RT = Sedlis criteria (simplified - need 2/3)

 LVSI
 Deep stromal invasion (middle 1/3 or >)
 Tumor size 4 cm

 Indications for adjuvant CHEMO-RT = Peters criteria (3 P's)

 Positive nodes
 Positive margins
 Parametrial involvement

 Locally-advanced disease (IB3, IIA2-IVA) concurrent CRT + BT boost

 Pelvic RT 45 Gy/25 Fx with 50-54 Gy boost to parametrial involvement or
 involved nodes

 Brachytherapy boost for EQD2 of 85-90 Gy to HR CTV
 Concurrent cisplatin 40 mg/m2
Surgery

Reserved for early-stage (IA1-IB2 & IIA1): <4 cm, no parametria

Fertility-sparing procedures:

 Cold knife cone excision (CKC): remove cone-shaped tissue
 containing ectocervix & endocervical canal en bloc with scalpel;
 facilitates accurate margin status assessment

 Radical trachelectomy: removes cervix, upper vagina, & parametria;
 uterus remains in place, with cerclage ("purse-string stitch") at distal
 end of uterus

Evaluate margins & LVSI status:

 Negative margins & no LVSI observation
 Positive margins & no LVSI repeat cone biopsy or trachelectomy
 +LVSI repeat trachelectomy with pelvic SLNB or LAD, or repeat

 cone biopsy with negative margins
Surgery (ctd.)

Non-fertility sparing procedures:

 Type I hysterectomy (simple or total): uterus & cervix, parametria left intact
 Type II hysterectomy (modified radical): uterus, cervix, upper 1-2 cm vagina, WLE of parametria
 Type III hysterectomy (radical): uterus, cervix, 1/4-1/3 vagina, & parametria to pelvic sidewall or sacrum

Patients stage IB1 or above who do not desire fertility sparing should have radical hysterectomy + SLNB/LND!

� Modified radical hysterectomy is acceptable only for cervical cancers with <5 mm of invasion (IA)
� All patients should have lymph node evaluation (SLNB vs. LND)

Note: adjuvant hysterectomy is typically NOT performed; no benefit in OS or DFS
Goal is to select patients for surgery who are at low risk for needing adjuvant RT

 Bimodality & trimodality therapy increases morbidity
Postoperative Radiotherapy +/- Chemo

EBRT: 45-50.4 Gy to pelvis (1.8 Gy/Fx) Adjuvant RT alone: Sedlis criteria
 (simplified 2/3):
 Pelvic nodes � LVSI
 � Deep stromal invasion (middle 1/3 or >)
 Common iliac, external & internal iliac, obturator nodes � Size 4

 Vagina & parametria Adjuvant RT + CHT: Peters criteria (3 Ps)
 � Positive nodes
 At least 3 cm from vaginal apex, or 1 cm above obturator � Positive margins
 foramen, whichever is lower � Parametrial involvement

Vaginal cuff brachytherapy boost: 5 Gy x 2 Fx
(or 3 Fx if +margin) to upper 3 cm

 Consider if close (<5 mm) or positive vaginal
 margin

 Incidental simple hysterectomy rather than
 modified radical

Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 if indicated
Definitive CRT: Stage IIA2 or >

Pelvic RT 45 Gy/25 Fx with SIB to 55-57.5 Gy to involved nodes with weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2
Intracavitary brachytherapy boost with target HR CTV D90 85-90 Gy

RTOG 1203/TIME-C RCT (Klopp et al., JCO 2018) TIME-C Dose Constraints

 2015-2018: 278 patients with cervical (16%) or Small bowel V40 Gy 30%
 endometrial (84%) primary undergoing RT (25% CHT)
 Rectum V40 Gy 80%
 3D-conformal radiotherapy with 4 fields (45-50.4 Gy)
 Bladder V45 Gy 35%
 IMRT (45-50.4 Gy)
 Bone marrow V40 Gy 37%
 Significantly improved patient-reported GI & GU V10 Gy 90%
 toxicity

 No significant difference in clinician reported AEs �
 clinicians under-reported compared to patients

For pelvic radiation, IMRT is the preferred modality due to decreased toxicity
Brachytherapy Boost

Historical standard = point-based planning

Point A = 2 cm superior & 2 cm lateral to
external cervical os

Point B = 2 cm superior & 5 cm lateral to
external cervical os

Tandem & ovoid Tandem & ring
Brachytherapy Boost

3D volume-based planning in modern era using CT or MRI

Improves local control & DFS outcomes compared to point-based system (Hande et al., 2022)

EMBRACE studies

 EMBRACE I: prospective observational study of CRT + MRI-guided adaptive BT

 5-year local control 92%, OS 67% (stage I-IV)
 G3-5 toxicity: 7% GU, 9% GI, 6% vaginal, 3% fistula

 RetroEMBRACE: dose-volume relationship of tumor control probability (TCP)

 Target HR CTV D90 = 85 Gy

 Local control 94% for 20 cc tumor, 93% for 30 cc tumor, 86% for 70 cc tumor

 EMBRACE II: prospective observational study in IMRT/IGRT & IGABT era

 Prospective validation of EMBRACE findings with detailed DVH constraints for IMRT & IGABT
Brachytherapy Boost Doses & Constraints

Vishwanathan et al., Brachytherapy 2012

Standard brachytherapy boost dose-fractionation
regimens after 45 Gy pelvic EBRT + concurrent cisplatin-
based CRT (calculate EQD2 aim HR CTV D90 85 Gy)

� 5.5 Gy x5 (4 cm residual after CCRT) EMBRACE II Planning aims (soft Limits (hard
� 6 Gy x5 (>4 cm residual after CCRT Brachytherapy constraints � aim constraints; cannot
� 5 Gy x6 Constraints (EQD2) for while planning) exceed)
� 7 Gy x4
 Rectum D2cc < 65 Gy < 75 Gy

 Sigmoid D2cc < 70 Gy < 75 Gy

 Bladder D2cc < 80 Gy < 90

 Rectovaginal point <65 Gy <75 Gy
Total Treatment Time Matters!

Target overall treatment time should be 6-8 Retrospective data showing increased risk of
weeks maximum (no more than 56 days!) pelvic failure if total treatment time is >56 days
 (Song et al., Cancer 2012)
Each day of treatment prolongation beyond 8
weeks 0.5-1% decrement in local control

RetroEMBRACE showed worse local control with
treatment time >7 weeks EMBRACE II
recommends overall treatment time of 49 days
(7 weeks).

 Additional dose of 5 Gy required to compensate
 each 1-week increase of total treatment time

 Avoid treatment breaks whenever possible!
Major Surgery may be an option for early-stage cervical cancer
Takeaways
 Most need radical hysterectomy (stage IB or above, i.e., 5 mm
 stromal invasion)

 Indications for postop RT alone = Sedlis criteria (~need 2/3)

 Tumor size >4 cm
 Deep stromal invasion
 LVSI

 Indications for postop chemoRT = Peters criteria (3Ps)

 Positive margins
 Positive nodes

 Stage IIA2 or above need concurrent chemoRT + BT boost

 45 Gy/25 Fx to pelvis with concurrent weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2

 Target HR CTV D90 is 85 Gy (cumulative EQD2)

Cancer: Adjuvant Treatment
PORTEC-3: High-Risk EBRT � CHT

De Boer et al., Lancet 2018

2006-2013: 660 women with high-risk endometrial cancer s/p TAH/BSO (LND not required)

 >50% MMI with grade 3 and/or LVSI, stage II-III endometrioid, stage I-III serous or clear cell histology

Randomized to postoperative RT � concurrent & adjuvant CHT

 RT = 48.6 Gy/27 Fx
 Concurrent CHT = cisplatin 50 mg/m2 wks 1 & 4; Adjuvant CHT = Carboplatin AUC5 & Paclitaxel

 175mg/m2 q21d x4 cycles

5-year FFS & OS improved with CHT (76% 81%, SS) driven by stage III dz & serous histology

 Subgroup of stage I-II patients had no improvement in OS: 82% vs. 83%, NSS
 Subgroup of stage III showed OS 69% RT alone 79% with CHT (SS); FFS 58% 71% (SS)
 Subgroup of serous histology showed OS 52% 71% (SS); FFS 48% 60% (SS)

Significantly worse acute & late toxicity with addition of CHT (mainly GI & heme)

PORTEC-3: Addition of postoperative chemotherapy improves OS & FFS in stage III dz & serous histology.
GOG 258: CHT vs. CRT Stage III-IVA

Matei et al., NEJM 2019

736 patients with stage III-IVA endometrial cancer, or stage I-II
serous/clear cell with positive peritoneal washings s/p TAH/BSO (no
residual tumor >2 cm) randomized

 CHT alone: carboplatin (AUC6) + paclitaxel (175mg/m2) q21 days x6c
 CRT: 45 Gy/25 Fx with concurrent cisplatin (50 mg/m2) week 1 & 5 

 adjuvant carbo/tax x4c

 VBT boost was allowed in CRT arm

No significant difference in RFS between arms (59% vs. 58%, NSS)

 CRT arm had significantly less vaginal recurrence (7% 2%) & pelvic
 recurrence (20% 11%)

 However CRT had significantly higher distant mets (21% 27%)

SGO Abstract Update 2023: no improvement in OS with CRT

 GOG-258: Improved vaginal & pelvic control with CRT, but higher rate of
 distant metastasis, likely due to delay of systemic therapy.
Major Addition of postop CHT improves OS
Takeaways:
PORTEC-3 & Main benefit is likely control of distant disease
GOG-258
 Pelvic RT still for local & pelvic control

 Concurrent chemoRT quite toxic

 Alternate approach (era of SLNB):
 Postop carbo/paclitaxel EBRT (�BT boost)
IO for Advanced Stage or Recurrent

ENGOT-EN-6-NSGO/GOG 3031/RUBY Trial (Mirza et al., NEJM 2023) - Phase III RCT

 Stage III-IV or recurrent EC randomized to CHT with placebo vs. dostarlimab (PD-1 inhibitor)
 Carboplatin/paclitaxel + placebo vs. Carboplatin/paclitaxel + dostarlimab (concurrent & adj up to 3 yrs)
 Significant improvement in 2-yr OS & PFS with dostarlimab

 Overall PFS 36% vs. 18% (SS), 61% vs. 16% for dMMR-MSI-H, 28% vs. 18% for pMMR-MSS
 Overall OS 71% vs. 56% (NSS based on pre-established stopping rule)

 Higher g3+ toxicity (70% vs. 60%, SS) with dostarlimab

NRG GY018 Trial (Eskander et al., NEJM 2023) � phase III RCT

 Stage III-IV or recurrent EC randomized to CHT with placebo vs. pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitor)

 Concurrent q3 weeks with chemo, then q6 weeks up to 14 cycles

 1-yr PFS 74% with pembro vs. 38% with placebo (SS) in dMMR cohort
 pMMR cohort: median PFS 13 mo with pembro vs. 9 mo without (p < 0.001)

 Immunotherapy (PD-1 inhibitors) prolongs PFS in advanced stage or recurrent endometrial cancer for
 both dMMR & pMMR cohorts.
Inoperable Endometrial Cancer
Retrospective Data

 Surgically inoperable: extensive cervix or parametrial involvement

 � UPMC retrospective data (Vargo et al., Gynecol Oncol 2014)

 � EBRT (40-50.4 Gy) +/- concurrent cisplatin BT boost (5-5.5 Gy x3-4 Fx) for cumulative EQD2 60-70 Gy
 � 91% had no clinical cervical involvement, 58% had no pathologic cervical involvement
 � All had margin negative resection, pCR rate 24%
 � 3-year local control 96%, regional control 89%, distant control 84%, DFS 73%, & OS 100%

 � Upfront EBRT + BT +/- CHT can potentially allow for simple hysterectomy (less extensive surgery)

 Medically inoperable: not a surgical candidate due to comorbidities

 � Definitive BT for <50% MMI grade 1-2 with tumor size <4 cm
 � Definitive EBRT + BT if grade 3 or more advanced disease
 � ABS consensus guidelines (Schwartz et al., Brachytherapy 2014)
Summary

Surgical staging whenever possible

 SLNB can safely replace pelvic lymph node dissection in early-stage endometrial (FIRES study)

Risk-adapted adjuvant RT following TAH/BSO for early-stage

 Stage IA grade 1-2 (2023 stage IA2): observation (Sorbe low-risk study)
 Stage IA grade 3, stage IB grade 1-2 (2023 stage IB, IC): VBT (PORTEC-2)
 Stage IB grade 3, stage II, substantial LVSI (2023 stage IC, IIA-C): EBRT � VBT (GOG-99, PORTEC-1)

Locally advanced (high-risk) benefits from addition of chemotherapy

 Adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel EBRT (�BT boost)
 CHT important for distant control & very high toxicity with concurrent CRT (PORTEC-3, GOG-258)

Modern era: PFS benefit to addition of CHT + IO (PD-1 inhibitor)

 Further molecular-profile based therapies in pipeline

Inoperable endometrial cancer: surgically vs. medically inoperable
Thank you!

or positive margins

recurrence

Long-term results Kunos et al., Obstet Gynecol 2009

1977-1984: 114 pts with stage I-IV vulvar cancer undergoing radical vulvectomy & bilateral
inguinofemoral LND with positive nodes during surgery randomized intraoperatively

 o Ipsilateral PELVIC node resection (obturator, internal & external iliac, common iliac)
 o Bilateral pelvic & groin radiotherapy (45-50 Gy, no central vulvar RT)

RT improved outcomes in patients with 2 groin LNs, ECE, or fixed/ulcerated LNs

 o 2-year groin recurrence 24% SGY vs. 5% RT (p = 0.02)
 o 2-year OS 68% vs. 54% (p = 0.03) - effect in 2 nodes or ECE on subgroup analysis
 o 6-year OS 51% vs. 41% (p = 0.18) - BUT significant improvement in 2 nodes, fixed/ulcerated LNs, or ECE
 o 20% groin LN positivity a/w significant contralateral LN metastasis, relapse, & cancer-related death

No significant difference in skin toxicity or lymphedema

 o Lymphedema 22% dissection vs. 16% pelvic RT (p = 0.48)

SEER data 1988-2001: 208 patients with vulvar SCC with a single positive node
92% underwent radical vulvectomy and unilateral or bilateral inguinal LND
~50% underwent adjuvant RT
Improved 5-year disease-specific survival with adjuvant RT & especially if 12 nodes removed

 o 5-year DSS 77% with RT vs. 61% without RT (p = 0.02)
 o For those with 12 nodes removed: 5-year DSS 77% with RT vs. 55% without RT (p = 0.035)
 o Lost significance if >12 nodes removed: 77% with RT vs. 67% without RT (p = 0.23)

 SEER Data: Suggest benefit to adjuvant RT if single positive node & 12 nodes dissected

NCDB analysis 1998-2011 of 1797 patients with vulvar
SCC s/p surgery with pN+ disease

Use of adjuvant CHT in pN+ patients a/w significantly
reduced mortality risk

 o 38% reduction in risk of death (p < 0.001)

invasion (T1b, FIGO IB) need at least

IF MICROMETS, CAN SUBSTITUTE WITH INGUINAL RT
GOG 88: Groin dissection vs. RT

Stehman et al., IJROBP 1992

1986-1990: 58 pts with T1-T3 cN0 vulvar cancer amenable to
radical vulvectomy randomized

 o Radical vulvectomy + bilateral inguinofemoral groin dissection
 o Radical vulvectomy + inguinal RT (50 Gy/25 Fx to 3 cm depth)

Trial closed early due to worse outcomes in RT arm

 o PFS 90% dissection vs. 65% RT (p = 0.033)
 o OS 85% dissection vs. 60% RT (p = 0.035)
 o Groin recurrence 18% RT vs. 0% SGY

20% in dissection arm were N+ and treated with adjuvant RT

Criticized for underdosing groin with Rx to 3 cm depth

GROINSS-V: Observation if SLN-

Van der Zee et al., JCO 2008 � observational study

2000-2006: Vulvar cancer patients with T1-T2, <4 cm tumor, >1 mm DOI, cN0

SLNB using blue dye & radioactive tracer in 403 patients, of which 259 were SLN-

 o Radical excision + SLNB --> if negative, no further treatment

 Patients with multifocal disease were omitted in protocol amendment

 o Close follow-up with clinical exam q2 months x2 years

Low rates of groin recurrence when inguinal dissection omitted after negative SLNB

 o 5-year actuarial groin recurrence 2.3% in SLN- patients (median time to groin recurrence 12 months)

 Salvaged with bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy and adjuvant RT +/- chemo
 Recurrence was 3% if included multifocal disease

 o 5-year local recurrence 12%

 GROINS-V: Low groin failure when LND is omitted in patients with negative SLNB.
 GROINSS-V-I: Inguinofemoral LND if SLN+

 Grootenhuis et al., Gynecol Oncol 2016

 2000-2006: 377 patients with unifocal vulvar SCC (T1-2, <4 cm, cN0)

 o Inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy performed only if SLN+

 Postop RT to 50 Gy if 2 LN+ or ECE (10% SLN+ received RT)

 Early-stage vulvar cancer patients with SLN- have low groin recurrence, good survival, & less toxicity

Subset analysis (Oonk et al., 2010) of size of SLN metastasis showed significantly worse prognosis for
SLN >2 mm. However, no size cutoff established for omission of LND.

 GROINSS-V-I: Can safely omit LND in early-stage patients with negative SLNB. Patients with positive
 SLNB still need further management (LND standard of care).
GOG 173: SLNB-Guided Inguinal LND

Levenback et al., JCO 2012

1999-2009: 452 women with vulvar SCC, tumor size 2-6 cm, 1 mm DOI, cN0

 o Underwent SLNB using blue dye & radiocolloid followed by inguinal dissection (92% identified SLN)

Incidence of nodal metastasis = 32%
False negative rate = 8.3%
Sensitivity = 91.7%
False negative predictive value (1 � NPV) = 3.7% in all-comers

 o FNPV = 2% if tumor <4 cm vs. 7.4% if tumor 4-6 cm

 GOG 173: SLNB is a reasonable alternative to inguinal dissection.
 Women with tumors <4 cm have <3% chance of groin relapse due to false negative SLN.
GROINSS-V-II/GOG 270: RT vs. LND for SLN+

Oonk et al., JCO 2021 � Phase II prospective nonrandomized observational study

2005-2016: 1535 patients with early-stage vulvar cancer (diameter < 4 cm) & cN0

 o Treated with primary surgical treatment + SLNB
 o All patients with SLN+ underwent 3D-CRT to 50 Gy (inguinofemoral & distal external iliac nodes)

 Vulva was covered in case of close or positive margins; allowed ipsilateral nodal RT in patients with lateralized tumors

21% of patients had SLN+

 o June 2010: stopping rule activated because 10/91 patients with SLN+ & adjuvant RT had isolated groin
 recurrence. 9/10 of these had LN >2mm or ECE

 o Protocol modified to allow adjuvant RT only for SLNmi+ (micrometastases 2 mm)

Among patients with SLN micrometastases, 2-year groin recurrence rate was 1.6%

 o For SLN macrometastases, 22% with RT vs. 7% with LND (p = 0.011)
 o Lymphedema at 12 months was 10% with RT vs. 22% with LND (p < 0.001)

 GROINSS-V-II/GOG 270: Adjuvant RT for SLN+ micrometastases is safe alternative to LND.
 For SLN+ macrometastases, groin recurrence with 50 Gy RT is higher compared to LND.

Contouring guidelines in original
GROINS-V-II protocol

 inguinofemoral vessels

However, inguinal compartment is
more than that (red contour)

Protocol modified in 2015

 2015, possibly missing the nodal

Sorry, Rest In Peace, A**hole
So what do we do about SLN
macromets?

GROINSS-V-III/NRG GY024 (ongoing study � accruing)

Role of concurrent chemoradiotherapy in patients with SLN+ disease

 o Including macro & micrometastases

Radiotherapy

 o 48-50 Gy to inguinal & external iliac regions
 o 56 Gy boost involved inguinal site (prefer SIB technique)

Concurrent chemotherapy

 o Cisplatin 40 mg/m2 weekly

 Dose-reduced cisplatin or carboplatin AUC2 if renal impairment
Preoperative chemoRT for
unresectable vulvar primary or LNs
GOG 101: Split-Course ChemoRT

 Moore et al., IJROBP 1998 � Phase II Prospective Study

 73 patients with stage III-IV (cT3-4, cN0-3) vulvar SCC, not
 amenable to vulvectomy

 o Treated with RT to 47.6 Gy/28 Fx --> surgery after 4-8 weeks
 o RT = AP/PA fields

 23.3 Gy --> 1.5-2.5-week break based on skin toxicity --> 23.6 Gy
 Vulva only if cN0-1 (dotted line); added pelvis & inguinals if cN3-4 (solid line)

 o Concurrent chemo: 5-FU + cisplatin with each split course

 5-FU 1000 mg/m2 over days 1-4, cisplatin 50 mg/m2 d1

 o If no response, additional 20 Gy
 o If positive margin, additional 10-15 Gy

 High resectability rate (~95%) after preop chemoRT

 o For primary tumors, cCR in 46% (pCR ~35%); only 3% unresectable
 o For node positive disease, pCR 47%; only 5% unresectable

GOG 101: Preop chemoRT for unresectable locally advanced disease is
 feasible & reduces pelvic exenteration. Split-course is not ideal.
GOG 205: Cisplatin + RT 57.6 Gy/32 Fx

Moore et al., Gynecol Oncol 2012 � Phase II

2005-2009: 58 patients with cT3-4 vulvar SCC (unresectable) treated with chemoRT --> surgery

 o RT to 57.6 Gy/32 daily Fx over 6.4 weeks with AP/PA fields

 45 Gy to nodes if treating, then cone-down for sequential boost to primary & nodal tumor with 2 cm margin

 o Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2
 o Surgery 6-8 weeks after (note: did allow preop inguinal LND if cN0 or operable nodes)

Improved response compared to GOG 101

 o 64% cCR & 50% pCR

Well-tolerated regimen

 o ~80% completed planned RT, ~70% completed planned 5 cycles of chemo
 o Most g3+ toxicities were hematologic; majority grade 1-2 radiation dermatitis

GOG 205: Neoadjuvant cisplatin + RT (57.6 Gy/32 Fx) yielded high response rates with acceptable toxicity.
 64% cCR & 50% pCR in GOG 205
 46% cCR & 35% pCR in GOG 101
UPMC: Dose-escalated IMRT

Richman et al., Gynecol Oncol 2020

2012-2018: Retrospective review of 49 Treatment area Dose
women treated with dose-escalated (55 Gy)
IMRT for locally advanced vulvar cancer Uninvolved elective 45 Gy/25 Fx
 nodal regions
 o RT doses in table 50-50.4 Gy/25-28 Fx
 o PET & MRI (+/- contrast) for staging Involved inguinal
 o Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 recommended nodal regions 55-58.8 Gy/25-28 Fx SIB
 o Routine nodal dissection not performed If >2 cm, ~4-6 Gy seq boost
 Clinically involved 56-60 Gy seq boost
Higher response rates than GOG 205 lymph nodes If not surgical, 66-68 Gy

 o Overall cCR 76% (63% preop, 88% definitive)
 o pCR for preop 58% (70% if include definitive) UPMC Retrospective: Dose-escalated IMRT well-
 tolerated. cCR & pCR rates compare favorably to GOG.
29% g3+ acute toxicity, 6% g3+ late

 o Acute � RT dermatitis, pain, GI toxicity
 o Late � vaginal stricture, persistent perineal pain
GOG 279: Phase II Dose-Esc IMRT with Gem/Cis

Horowitz et al., JCO 2024

49 patients with locally advanced T2-T3 vulvar primary (cN0-3, M0) not amenable to upfront radical
vulvectomy

 o IMRT 64 Gy/32 Fx to vulvar primary, 50 Gy to inguinal nodes
 o 40 mg/m2 of cisplatin and 50 mg/m2 of gemcitabine weekly with IMRT

IMRT review (100% protocol compliance) � 75% acceptable, 25% minor deviations
Response rates � 72% cCR, 73% pCR
4-year PFS 74%, 2-year OS 70%
Common toxicities = radiation dermatitis, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and electrolyte
abnormalities

Major Early-stage disease
Takeaways
 � Surgery when possible
 � SLNB indicated for stage IB or greater (>1 mm depth of

 invasion)
 � Omit further tx if SLN- & adjuvant RT alone if SLNmi+
 � For SLN macromets, GROINS-V-III investigating adjuvant

 chemoRT in lieu of LND
 � Indications for adjuvant RT to vulva: close/positive margin
 � Indications for adjuvant RT to pelvis & inguinal nodes: 2

 +LN, fixed/ulcerated LN, ECE
 � Adjuvant chemo may benefit pN+ but no prospective data

 Locally-advanced/unresectable disease

 � Preoperative or definitive chemoRT
 � Consider dose-escalated IMRT
