Fractionation
Schemes for 70 Gy/35 Fx delivered as SIB
Head & Neck
 70 Gy to primary and +nodes
Note that many variations and 63 Gy to mucosal margin and involved nodal levels
alternative fractionation 56 Gy to elective neck
schedules exist.
 Exception to this rule:

 T1 N0 glottic larynx 63 Gy/28 Fx (2.25 Gy/Fx)
 T2 N0 glottic larynx 65.25 Gy/29 Fx (2.25 Gy/Fx)

 Many variations exist including sequential boost (50/60/70) & hyperfractionation (6 Fx per week), 33 Fx

 Postoperative RT

 Indicated if pT3-T4, pN2-N3, ECE, close or +SM, LVSI, PNI
 60 Gy/30 Fx delivered as simultaneous integrated boost (SIB)

 60 Gy to tumor bed
 57 Gy to operative bed
 54 Gy to elective neck

 66 Gy/33 Fx if +SM (surgical margin) or ECE (extracapsular extension)

 Treat as above, then additional 6 Gy/3 Fx as sequential boost
Definitive RT
RTOG 9003: Fractionation Trial

Fu et al., IJROBP 2000 & Beitler et al., IJROBP 2014

Phase III RCT of 1073 patients with stage III-IV SCC of OC, OPX, LNX, or HPX, or stage II-IV SCC

of BOT
 Standard fractionation (SFX): 70 Gy/35 Fx daily over 7 weeks

 Hyperfractionation (HFX): 81.6 Gy/68 Fx BID over 7 weeks

 Split-course Accelerated Fx (AFX-S): 67.2G2/42fx BID over 6 weeks (2-week break after 38.4
 Gy)

 Accelerated Fx + Concomittant Boost (AFX-C): 72Gy/30 Fx over 6 weeks + boost

 For last 12 treatment days, afternoon added boost field with 1.5Gy/Fx to reduced volumes (primary and gross nodes)

Major caveats � pre-IMRT era, no concurrent chemotherapy offered

Initial showed improved LRC with HFX & AFX-C arms, improved OS with HFX

 Long-term analysis showed best toxicity with HFX arm

 LRC & OS benefit persisted only with HFX

 SFX 2 yrs LCR 5yrs LCR RTOG 90-03: historic fractionation
 HFX 46% 45.75 trial � showed improved LRC with
 AFX-C 54.4% 53.3% HFX & AFX-C, though questionable
 AFX-S 54.5% 53.8% applicability in modern era with
 47.5% 47.8%
 IMRT & concurrent chemo.
Impact of Dose per Fx, OTT, & Total Dose

Withers et al., IJROBP 1995

Retrospective study of 676 patients with tonsillar SCC, treated across 9 institutions that used
different dose-fractionation patterns

 Key Findings (Withers Study):

 � Overall treatment time matters
 � Each day that RT was delayed 1% decrease in local control

 � Dose matters
 � ~2% per Gy increase in local control prescribe additional 0.5-0.7
 Gy for each day that RT is delayed to maintain the same local control
DAHANCA 6&7: 5 vs. 6 Fx/week

Overgaard et al., Lancet 2003

1992-1999: 1476 patients with H&N cancers (OC, pharynx, larynx) treated with primary RT (66-68 Gy
in 33-34 fractions) randomized

 5 fractions per week 7 weeks total
 6 fractions per week 6 weeks total

All patients (except glottic) also treated with hypoxic radiosensitizer nimorazole

Improved locoregional control & disease-specific survival (but not OS) with 6 Fx/week

 5-year LRC 70% with 6 Fx s. 60% with 5 Fx (p=0.0005)
 Benefit was seen for primary tumor control (76% vs. 64%, p=0.0001), but not neck node control
 5-year DFS 73% with 6 Fx vs. 66% with 5 Fx (p=0.01)
 Improved laryngeal preservation in larynx cancer patients (80% vs. 68%, p=0.007)

Worse acute toxicity with 6 Fx per week, but this resolved

 DAHANCA 6&7: Improved LRC & DFS, but not OS, with 6 fractions per week.
 6 Fx per week = Danish standard.
MARCH-HN Meta-Analysis of Fractionation

Lacas et al., Lancet Oncol 2017

Meta-analysis of RCTs [OC, OPX, HPX, LNX] from 2009-2015 with 2 comparisons

 (1) Primary or postop conventional fractionation RT vs. altered fractionation RT
 (2) Conventional fractionation RT + concurrent chemo vs. altered fractionation RT

3 types of altered fractionation: Hyperfractionated, Accelerated, Very accelerated

Comparison (1): altered fx was a/w improved OS

 3% improvement in 5-year OS, 1% improvement in 10-year OS
 Only hyperfractionated a/w OS benefit on sub-analysis

 8% improvement in 5-year OS, 4% improvement in 10-year OS

Comparison (2): conventional chemoRT a/w improved OS

 Hyperfractionated RT alone led to worse OS: -6% 5-year OS and -5% 10-year OS

Worse acute toxicity including dermatitis & mucositis with altered fractionation

 MARCH-HN Meta-Analysis: Hyperfractionated RT &
 conventionally fractionated chemoRT are both standards of

 care. (HyperFx RT � chemo yet to be tested).
Hyperfractionated RT & Chemo

GORTEC 99-02 (Bourhis et al., Lancet Oncol 2012)

 2000-2007: 3-arm RCT of stage III-IV HNSCC

 Conventional chemoRT 70 Gy/35 Fx over 7 weeks + 3 cycles carboplatin + 5-FU 3-yr PFS 38%
 Accelerated chemoRT 70 Gy/35 Fx over 6 weeks + 2 cycles carboplatin + 5-FU 3-yr PFS 34%
 Very accelerated RT alone 64.8 Gy at 1.8 Gy/Fx with BID fractionation over 3-5 weeks 3-yr PFS 32%

 No difference in PFS between conventional chemoRT & accelerated chemoRT (HR 1.02, p=0.88)
 Worse PFS with very accelerated RT compared to conventional chemoRT (HR 0.82, p=0.041)
 Worse G3-4 toxicity in very accelerated RT arm

RTOG 0129 (Nguyen-Tan et al., JCO 2014)

 2002-2005: 721 patients with stage III-IV HNSCC (OC, OPX, LNX, HPX) randomized

 SFX 70 Gy/35 Fx over 7 weeks with 3 cycles of cisplatin 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks
 AFX 72 Gy/42 Fx over 6 weeks with 2 cycles of cisplatin 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks

 No differences in 8-year OS (~48%), PFS (~42%), LRF (~38%), or DM (~14%)
 No differences in toxicities between arms

 No difference between conventional Fx and hyperfractionation in s/o concurrent chemotherapy.
 Based on GORTEC 99-02, very accelerated RT doesn't compensate for lack of chemotherapy.

 and involved nodal levels

 Mild hypofractionation: 70 Gy/33 Fx

Postop RT
RTOG 7303: preop vs. postop RT

Tupchong et al., IJROBP 1991

1973-1979: 358 patients with head and neck cancer (OC, OPX,
LNX, HPX) randomized

 Preop RT (50 Gy at 1.8-2 Gy per Fx) SGY within 4-6 weeks
 SGY Postop RT within 4 weeks (60 Gy to primary, 50 Gy to

 remaining neck)

Improved locoregional control with postop RT

 LRC 70% with postop RT vs. 58% preop RT (p=0.04)
 In both groups, locoregional failure accounted for ~60% of failures

 within first two years

No difference in OS between arms

 29% postop vs. 20% preop (p=0.15)

RTOG 7303 established postop RT as standard for patients managed
 with surgery.

Criteria from RTOG 0920 (Machtay et al., IJROBP 2023)

 Negative trial � postop RT +/- cetuximab for intermediate-risk HNSCC

General Dosing for Postop RT

One accepted standard : Note: Some only treat to two dose-

60 Gy delivered as SIB using IMRT

66 Gy delivered as 6 Gy/3 Fx sequential boost

 Areas of positive margin or ECE
IMRT

IMRT planning is standard of care for head and neck cancers

Improved xerostomia due to parotid gland sparing

 University of Michigan Study (Eisbruch et al., IJROBP 1998)

 Mean dose to parotids � 20 Gy IMRT vs. 57 Gy 3D planning
 1-year recovery of salivary flow � 63% IMRT vs. 3% 3D planning

 PARSPORT Study (Nutting et al., Lancet Oncol 2021)

 Parotid-sparing IMRT 40% rate of G2+ xerostomia at 1 year compared to 74% with 3D planning

Other theoretical benefits

 Improved dysphagia due to pharyngeal constrictor sparing
 Decreased osteoradionecrosis due to mandible sparing
 Improved sensorineural hearing preservation due to cochlea sparing
 Improved brainstem sparing

General Principles of
Concurrent ChemoRT
Chemotherapy Induction Chemotherapy

 Induction gemcitabine/cisplatin for NPX (esp. EBV+)

 Induction followed by definitive RT or chemoRT

 Consider induction for any locally advanced disease not
 amenable to upfront SGY or RT

 Concurrent Chemotherapy

 Concurrent chemotherapy = cisplatin

 Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 (Cisplatin 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks worse toxicity)
 If cisplatin contraindicated, can use concurrent cetuximab or carbo/taxol

 Postoperative setting

 Add concurrent chemo if +SM or ECE

 Definitive setting

 Add concurrent chemo if cT3-T4 or cN2+
Definitive ChemoRT
MACH-NC Meta-Analysis

Pignon et al., Radiother & Oncol 2009

Patient-level meta-analysis of over 17,000 HNSCC patients from 93 trials

 Combined different types of chemotherapy together
 Included many historical trials prior to 1970s info on p16 not available

Addition of CHT 4.5% OS benefit at 5 years

 Concurrent chemoRT showed absolute benefit of 6.5% at 5 years (SS)
 Induction 2.4% at 5 years (NS)
 Patients above 70 years of age did not benefit in terms of OS
 Both concurrent and induction CHT improved distant control (update: HR 0.73 and 0.88, p = .0001 and

 .04 but not different when compared to each other)

 MACH-NC meta-analysis showed that addition of concurrent chemotherapy provided 6.5% OS benefit.
 4.5% OS benefit to addition of any chemotherapy.
ChemoRT for Stage III-IV Disease

cT3-T4 or cN2-N3

Intergroup H&N Trial (Addelstein et al., JCO 2003)

 1992-1999: 271 pts with stage III-IV unresectable HNSCC (excluded sinus, NPX, salivary) randomized

 RT alone 70 Gy/35 Fx
 RT 70 Gy/35 Fx + cisplatin 100 mg/m2 weeks 1, 4, 7
 Split course chemoRT: Cisplatin/5-FU q4 weeks, with 30 Gy/15 Fx reevaluated & 30-40 Gy with 3rd cycle of RT if still unresectable

 Slow accrual, closed early 3-year OS for conventional RT + cisplatin

 23% arm A vs. 37% arm B vs. 27% arm C

Recall from GORTEC 99-02 & RTOG 0129 that when concurrent chemotherapy is given,
hyperfractionation of RT does not seem to add much benefit.

 Definitive chemoRT is typically indicated for cT3-T4 or cN2-N3 disease (think stage III-IV)
Postop ChemoRT
RTOG 9501: Postop CRT for +SM or ECE

Cooper et al., NEJM 2004

1995-2000: 459 patients with H&N SCC (oral cavity, OPX, LNX, or HPX) s/p resections with one or
more risk factors � 2 LNs, ECE, and +SM � randomized

 Postop RT alone, 60 Gy/30 Fx (allowed boost up to 66 Gy)
 Postop chemoRT + concurrent cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks)

Improved locoregional control with chemoRT, but no change in OS

 10-year local-regional failure rates 28.8% RT vs. 22.3% chemoRT (p=0.10)
 10-year DFS 19.1% RT vs. 20.1% chemoRT (p=0.25)
 10-year OS 27.0% RT vs. 29.1% chemoRT (p=0.31)

Unplanned subset analysis limited to patients with +SM & ECE: improved LRF & DFS

 LRF 33.1% RT vs. 21.0% chemoRT (p=0.02)

 DFS 12.3% RT vs. 18.4% chemoRT (p=0.05) RTOG 9501 showed LRF & DFS benefit to postp
 OS 19.6% vs. 27.1% chemoRT (p=0.07) chemoRT for patients with +SM and/or ECE.
EORTC 22931 & RTOG Pooled Analysis

EORTC 22931 (Bernier et al., NEJM 2004)

 PRT of 334 patients with HNSCC s/p surgery & cT3-T4 or cN2-N3 or any high-risk: +SM, ECE, PNI, or
 vascular tumor embolism

 Randomized to postop RT (66 Gy/33 Fx) or RT + concurrent cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks)
 ChemoRT significantly improved 5-year PFS (47% vs. 36%, p=0.04) & OS (53% vs. 40%, p=0.02)

Pooled analysis of RTOG 9501 & EORTC 22931 (Bernier et al., Head and Neck 2005)

 ECE and/or microscopically +margins were only risk factors which showed significant impact of
 chemo RT in both trials

 RTOG 9501 & EORTC 22931 (and pooled analysis) support adjuvant chemoRT for patients with
 extracapsular extension (ECE) and/or positive surgical margins (+SM)!
NCCN Guidelines for Adjuvant ChemoRT

Definite indications

 Positive surgical margins
 Extracapsular extension

Additional factors to consider (based on EORTC 22931 inclusion criteria)

 Multiple positive nodes (without ECE)
 PNI
 Vascular invasion
 Lymphatic invasion
 pT3-T4 disease
 Oral cavity or orpharynx primary with +level IV or V nodes
Cisplatin Dosing: Low dose (40 mg/m2 weekly)
vs. High dose (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks)

Traditionally cisplatin was given using 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks, but this was associated with significant toxicity

VA Retrospective Study (Bauml et al., JNCI 2018)

 Population-based study of 2901 patients with stage III-IVb HNSCC treated definitively with chemoRT
 2200 received HDC (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks) & and the rest received LDC (40 mg/m2 weekly)
 HDC was not associated with improved OS (HR 0.94, 95% CI 0.80-1.04)
 HDC was associated with increased risk of AKI, neutropenia, dehydration, & hearing loss

India Phase III RCT (Noronha et al., JCO 2018) cisplatin 30 mg/m2 weekly is too low

 RCT of HNSCC patients (93% treated with adjuvant chemoRT)
 Randomized to chemoRT with 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks vs. 30 mg/m2 weekly
 HDC improved 2-year LRC (73% vs. 59%, p=0.14) but no difference in OS (p=0.48)
 Higher G3+ toxicity with HDC (85% vs. 72%, p=0.006)

Ongoing NRG HN009 randomizing to concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks vs. 40 mg/m2 weekly

 Low-dose cisplatin 40 mg/m2 weekly has less toxicity and is utilized by many.
 Currently high dose cisplatin is NCCN category 1, and weekly cisplatin is acceptable alternative.
Immunotherapy in
HNSCC
Concurrent Cetuximab

Cetuximab = EGFR antibody

 Has fewer side effects compared to cisplatin

Bonner et al., Cetuximab + RT (NEJM 2006 & Lancet Oncol 2010)

 Stage III-IV HNSCC patients treated with definitive RT randomized to RT alone vs. RT + cetuximab

 Cetuximab 400 mg/m2 first dose, then 250 mg/m2 weekly with RT

 Median OS 49 mo with cetuximab vs. 29 mo with RT alone (p=0.03)
 Oropharyngeal cancer patients had most significant improvement in OS (>66 mo vs. 23 mo)

 Other disease subsites did NOT have as significant of an improvement with cetuximab (larynx, hypopharynx minimal difference)

 Follow-up study showed grade 2+ rash from cetuximab c/w improved survival vs. no rash or grade 1 rash

In HPV(+) oropharyngeal cancer, multiple clinical trials looked at definitive cetuximab + RT vs. cisplatin
+ RT and found inferior OS with concurrent cetuximab vs. cisplatin (~8-12% inferior OS)

 RTOG 1016, De-ESCALATE, ARTSCAN III trials

 Concurrent weekly cetuximab is an option for patients who won't tolerate cisplatin (NCCN category 2B).
 Concurrent carboplatin/paclitaxel is another option (category 2B).
ChemoRT + ICI

Multiple RCTs have investigated benefit of concurrent chemoRT + ICI and found no benefit

 PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibition
 Randomized to chemoRT with cisplatin + placebo vs. ICI

Trial Phase Drug 1o Endpoint
HN004 (Mell et al., IJROBP 2022) II Durvalumab
 PFS not improved
JAVELIN (Lee et al., Lancet 2021) III Avelumab LRF higher with durva
 PFS not improved

KEYNOTE-412 (Machiels et al., Lancet 2024) III Pembrolizumab EFS not improved

Currently, only roll for chemoIO is in recurrent or metastatic setting

 Pembrolizumab + cisplatin or carbo + 5-FU = first-line
Major Definitive RT
Takeaways
 70 Gy/63 Gy/56 Gy in 35 Fx as SIB is one acceptable option
 Concurrent cisplatin for cT3-T4 or cN2-N3
 Consider hyperfractionation if not getting chemo, or re-RT

 (consider BID especially if re-RT)

 Postop RT

 60 Gy/57 Gy/54 Gy in 30 Fx as SIB is one acceptable option
 6 Gy/3 Fx for 66 Gy delivered sequentially to +margin or +ECE
 Add concurrent chemo if +margin or +ECE

 Systemic Tx Considerations

 Concurrent cisplatin is standard

 High-dose 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks
 Low-dose 40 mg/m2 weekly

 If not a candidate for cisplatin, cetuximab or carbo/taxol
 No data supporting chemoIO unless recurrent or metastatic dz
Thank you!

Japanese Hypofractionation Study

Yamazaki et al., IJROBP 2006

1993-2001: 180 patients with early-stage glottic carcinoma (T1 N0 M0) randomized

 2 Gy per Fx: 60 Gy/30 Fx for tumors involving 2/3 of vocal cord, or 66 Gy/33 Fx if >2/3
 2.25 Gy/per Fx: 56.25 Gy/25 Fx for tumors involving 2/3 of vocal cord, or 63 Gy/28 Fx if >2/3

Radiotherapy delivered with parallel opposed fields

5-year local control was improved in 2.25 Gy/Fx arm

 5-year local control 77% at 2 Gy per Fx vs. 92% at 2.25 Gy per Fx (p=0.004)
 5-year CSS 97% vs. 100% (NSS)

No significant differences in acute mucosal reaction, skin reactions, or chronic adverse reactions

 Japanese Fractionation Study showed improved local control with 2.25 Gy per Fx for T1 N0 M0 glottic
 laryngeal cancers.

 Predominantly in endemic populations

 China: Sun et al., Lancet Oncol 2016

 2011-2013: 241 patients with stage III-IVB (except T3-T4 N0) nonkeratinizing NPC randomized

 ChemoRT (q3 week HD-cisplatin)

 480 patients with stage III-IV nonkeratinizing NPC randomized

 Subgroup analysis showed highest benefit to induction for patients with pre-tx EBV >4000 copies/mL

DIPPER: Adjuvant PD-1 Blockade

Liang et al., JAMA 2025

2018-2021: 450 patients in China (nonkeratinizing, endemic population) with very locally
advanced NPC (T4N1 or N2-3) s/p induction CHT & concurrent chemoRT randomized

 Observation
 Adjuvant camrelizumab (PD-1 blocker) q3 weeks x12 cycles

Significant improvement in EFS with adjuvant camrelizumab

 3-year EFS 77% obs vs. 87% IO (p=0.01)

Toxicity � 86% of patients in IO arm had reactive capillary endothelial proliferation

 Similar to cetuximab-induced rash, a/w improved survival? (Bonner et al., Lancet Oncol 2010)

DIPPER trial showed improved EFS with PD-1 blockade (carmelizumab) for very locally advanced NPC in an
endemic population. Benefit remains to be shown in nonendemic (US) population/keratinizing histology.

Treatment Paradigm
Summary of Upfront surgery whenever possible
Management
 Aim for en bloc resection of tumor
 Early stage � wide local excision (e.g., partial glossectomy)
 More advanced � hemiglossectomy, maxillectomy, mandibulectomy
 Often require reconstruction (e.g., fibular flap reconstruction after

 mandibulectomy)

 Indications for adjuvant radiotherapy

 pT3-T4
 pN2-3
 pT1-2 N0-N1 with any of the following:

 LVSI
 PNI
 Positive or close margin (5 mm)
 DOI 4-5 mm

 Indications for adjuvant chemoRT

 Positive margin
 Extranodal extension

 Inoperable (e.g., T4b tumors or unfit for surgery)

 Consider definitive chemoRT vs. induction chemo chemoRT
 Palliative systemic therapy and/or RT if poor PS
Surgery

Primary surgical excision

 Approach varies widely based on location & extent of tumor
 Early-stage OC-SCC may be resected with minimal cosmetic and functional deficits
 Hemiglossectomy, maxillectomy, mandibulotomy may be needed for locally advanced disease

Negative margin status is of utmost importance!

 If positive margin re-resection preferred whenever possible

Lymph node assessment

 Elective dissection of at least levels I-III (assuming N0)

 Levels I-IV if oral tongue

 Well-lateralized tumors may have ipsilateral elective LND
 Central tumors should have bilateral elective LND
 Only certain early-stage tumors with low risk of metastasis can be considered for omission of LND

 E.g., T1 upper lip, upper alveolar ridge, hard palate

 Sentinel lymph node biopsy is an option for T1-T2 cN0 (Senti-MERORL)
Adjuvant radiotherapy (& chemoRT)

Indications for adjuvant RT

 pT3-T4, pN2-3, pT1-2 N0-N1 with any of the following:

 LVSI, PNI, Positive or close margin (5 mm), DOI 4-5 mm

Simultaneous integrated boost

 60 Gy/30 Fx to tumor bed (includes involved nodal beds)
 57 Gy/30 Fx to operative bed (entire operative bed + dissected neck)
 54 Gy/30 Fx to elective neck

Sequential boost if needed

 66 Gy/33 Fx (additional 6 Gy/3 Fx) to areas of positive margin and/or ENE

Concurrent chemotherapy

 Indicated if positive margin or ENE
 Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 given concurrently with chemotherapy
SGY > RT for Oral Cavity
Glasgow: SGY RT vs. RT alone

Robertson et al., Clin Oncol 1998

35 patients with T2-4 N0-2 SCC of oral cavity (>90%) or
oropharynx randomized

 SGY PORT (60 Gy/30 Fx)
 RT alone (66 Gy/33 Fx)

Trial originally designed to accrue 350 patients, but closed
early due to OS benefit for SGY
With 23-month follow-up, OS & LC significantly improved in
SGY arm

 HR for OS 0.24 with surgery (p=0.001)
 Duration of LC significantly decreased with RT alone (p=0.037)

 Outdated & very small study � suggests worse outcomes with definitive RT alone compared to SGY + RT.
Singapore: SGY RT vs. CRT

Iyer et al., Cancer 2015

1996-2002: 119 patients with stage III-IV resectable H&N cancer (27% OC, 32% LX, 21% OPX,
12% HPX, 8% sinus) randomized

 Surgery with comprehensive nodal dissection RT 60 Gy/30 Fx
 ChemoRT 66 Gy/33 Fx with 2 cycles cisplatin (20 mg/m2/5-FU 1000 mg/m2 on days 1 and 28)

Improved DSS in oral cavity cancer patients with SGY

 Entire cohort

 5-year OS 45% vs. 35% (p=0.262)
 5-year DSS 56% vs. 46% (p=0.637)

 Oral cavity and sinus patients had improved DSS with SGY

 5-year DSS oral cavity 68% SGY vs. 12% CRT (p=0.038)
 5-year DSS sinus 71% SGY vs. 0% CRT (p=0.05)

 Oral cavity and sinus cancer patients drove improved DSS with SGY + postop RT
Elective LND

SLNB ACCEPTABLE FOR T1-2 N0 ORAL CAVITY
Tata Memorial: Elective vs. Therapeutic LND

D'Cruz et al., NEJM 2015

2004-2014: prospective RCT of 500 patients with well-lateralized T1-T2 cN0 oral cavity cancer

 Elective ipsilateral neck dissection at time of surgery
 Therapeutic node dissection (watchful waiting neck dissection at time of recurrence)

Positive nodes, tumor DOI 10 mm, or +SM adjuvant RT

Elective neck dissection improved OS (1o endpoint) & DFS (2o)

 3-year OS 80% elective vs. 67.5% therapeutic (p=0.01)
 3-year DFS 70% vs. 46% (p<0.001)
 Subgroup analysis showed benefit across all subgroups (even with those 3 mm invasion)

29.6% rate of nodal positivity in elective dissection arm

 Risk of nodal positivity increased with increasing DOI

Patients in therapeutic arm presented with relapse with more advanced nodal stage (p=0.005)
& higher risk of ECE (p<0.001)

Adverse events: 7% elective vs. 4% therapeutic

 Tata Memorial RCT: patients with early-stage oral cancers should have
 ipsilateral elective nodal dissection, as this improves OS & DFS.
Senti-MERORL: SLNB LND if (+) Overall survival

Garrel et al., JCO 2020

Phase III RCT of 307 patients cT1-T2 oral cavity (~88%) and oropharyngeal
cancer (~12%) randomized

 Control arm: nodal dissection
 Experimental arm: SLNB nodal dissection only if positive

Primary outcome of 2-year neck node recurrence-free survival was
equivalent

 89.6% in LND arm vs. 90.7% in SLNB arm (p<0.01 for equivalence)
 16% in SLNB arm underwent LND due to pN+

No significant difference in other endpoints either � 5-year neck node RFS,
OS, DSS, LRRFS

Functional outcomes significantly worse in LND arm until 6 months after SGY

 Senti-MERORL: For early-stage oral cavity cancer (T1-T2 N0), SLNB followed by LND only if pN+ is
equivalent to LND in terms of oncologic outcomes, and superior in terms of neck function. SLNB for early-

 stage oral cavity & oropharyngeal cancers should be standard of care.
Patterns of Failure: Retrospective Analysis

Ganly et al., Cancer 2013

Combined analysis of 164 patients treated at MSKCC & Princess Margaret

pT1-T2 N0 oral tongue cancer treated with surgery & ipsilateral elective LND � no PORT

Median f/u of 66 months

 Local control = 89%
 Regional control = 80%
 Disease-specific survival = 86%

20% of patients had regional recurrence! these patients had worse DSS (97% vs. 33%, p<0.0001)

 60% of these were ipsilateral
 40% were contralateral

On MVA, tumor thickness 4 mm was the only predictor of regional failure

 Regional control 94% for patients with thickness <4 mm vs. 72% if thickness 4 mm (p=0.02)

Ganly data: Patients with tumor thickness 4 mm more likely to have regional recurrence (~28%) & 40% of
 these recurrences are contralateral! Justifies bilateral elective nodal irradiation for these tumors.
Major Oral cavity cancers should be managed with SURGERY
Takeaways
 Elective dissection of at least levels I-III (assuming N0)

 Levels I-IV if oral tongue

 Well-lateralized tumors may have ipsilateral elective LND
 Central tumors should have bilateral elective LND
 Only certain early-stage tumors with low risk of metastasis can

 be considered for omission of LND

 E.g., T1 upper lip, upper alveolar ridge, hard palate

 Sentinel lymph node biopsy is an option for T1-T2 cN0

 May be less accurate or technically challenging in certain
 location such as floor of mouth, gingiva, hard palate

 Tumor thickness/DOI 4 mm has high risk of nodal
 involvement

 ~28% risk of nodal involvement
 ~40% of recurrences are contralateral

with RT � CHT
General Indications for Postop RT within <6
Postop RT weeks of surgery

Criteria from RTOG 0920 (Machtay et al., IJROBP 2023) improves local control

 Negative trial � postop RT +/- cetuximab for intermediate-risk HNSCC (Daly et al., IJROBP 2011).

 Ideal time = 4-6 weeks
 (allow for healing)

 Intermediate risk patients postop RT alone

T1-T2 N1-2 or T2-T4a N0-2 s/p gross total resection and one or more of the following risk factors:
 � PNI
 � LVSI

Indications for Postop ChemoRT

RTOG 9501 (Cooper et al., NEJM 2004)

 Unplanned subset analysis limited to patients with +SM and/or +ECE showed improved LRF & DFS with
 postop chemoRT vs. RT alone

EORTC 22931 (Bernier et al., NEJM 2004)

 Improved PFS & OS with postop chemoRT for cT3-T4 or cN2-N3 or any high-risk: +SM, ECE, PNI, or

Pooled Analysis (Bernier et al., Head and Neck 2005)

 ECE and/or microscopically +margins were only risk factors which showed significant impact of
 chemo RT in both trials

 RTOG 9501 & EORTC 22931 (and pooled analysis) support adjuvant chemoRT for patients with
 extracapsular extension (ECE) and/or positive surgical margins (+SM)!

HPV-associated carcinogenesis

 E6 & E7 are HPV viral oncogene products

E7 binds pRB (retinoblastoma protein) RB normally inhibits the

 progression at the G1-S checkpoint

E6 binds p53 (tumor suppressor gene) p16 is overexpressed, and
 can act as a surrogate
 Targets p53 for degradation marker for HPV

Treatment Paradigm
Summary of Surgery & Neck Dissection
Management
 � T1-T2 and select T3 lesions, N0-1
 � TORS (transoral robotic surgery) is common
 � Ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection depending on nodal status and location of primary

 tumor
 � Tailored adjuvant RT +/- CHT based on risk factors

 � Adj RT (60 Gy/30 Fx): T3-T4, N2-N3, LVSI, PNI, close margin
 � Adj CRT (66 Gy/33 Fx): margin(+), ENE(+)

 Definitive Radiotherapy

 � Option for T1-T2 N0-1, and typically recommended for T3-T4 N2-N3
 � 70 Gy/35 Fx to primary and involved nodes

 � 56/63/70 Gy as SIB or 50/60/70 Gy as sequential boost
 � Concurrent CHT if stage III-IV (T3-4, N1-3)

 � Cisplatin 40 mg/m2 weekly vs. 100 mg/m2 q3 weeks
 � Alternate options if cisplatin contraindicated = carboplatin/5-FU, cetuximab, carbo/taxol
Surgery

TORS = transoral robotic surgery

Goal is to obtain clear resection margins (~2 mm) &
maintain good functional outcomes

General Diagnosis
Approach to
Cancer � Patient presents with chief complaint
 � Exam, imaging, and ultimately biopsy lead to tissue diagnosis of

 cancer

 Staging

 � Complete necessary diagnostic procedures (imaging, surgery)
 � TNM (tumor, node, metastasis) & group (I-IV) staging systems

 Treatment

 � Identify goal of treatment: curative vs. palliative
 � Multidisciplinary consultation with Med Onc, Surg Onc, Rad Onc

 Follow-up

 � Management of toxicities from treatment
 � Periodic exam & restaging imaging
Performance
Status
Some Cancer Terms

Curative: intent is to cure
Palliative: intent is to relieve symptoms
Definitive: no further treatment is intended
Neoadjuvant: treatment is given as a first step
Adjuvant: additional treatment after primary treatment (usually to lower risk of recurrence)
Concurrent: treatment given at same time as another (e.g., concurrent chemoradiation)
Example: Glioblastoma
(GBM)

What is GBM?

 An aggressive brain tumor formed from glial cells
 Most common malignant brain tumor in adults

Treatment paradigm?

 Upfront surgery (maximal safe resection)
 Concurrent chemoradiation for up to 6 weeks

 Chemo = PO Temozolomide (TMZ) daily during RT

 Adjuvant chemotherapy for 6-12 months

 PO TMZ days 1-5 q4 weeks
Example: Stage III Non-Small
Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)

What is stage III NSCLC?

 Adenocarcinoma or squamous cell histology
 Tumor has spread to mediastinal lymph nodes

Treatment paradigm?

 Concurrent chemoradiation

 Chemo = carboplatin + paclitaxel or carbo + pemetrexed

 Adjuvant immunotherapy

 IO = durvalumab
How is radiation delivered?
Some Radiation Basics

Radiation dose is measured in units called Gray (Gy)

Radiation treatments are divided into fractions

 Generally delivered daily M-F over the course of several weeks

 Certain cancers are treated with BID fractionation (e.g., SCLC)

Modern radiation techniques allow for hypofractionation

 Higher dose per fraction & fewer total fractions

 Moderately hypofractionated regimens (e.g., breast & prostate cancer)
 Ultra-hypofractionated regimens in 1-5 fractions (e.g., SRS/SBRT)
Radiation Therapy Modalities

External beam radiation therapy Brachytherapy (BT)
(EBRT) Internal treatments

� Photons (most common) � Low-dose rate (LDR)
� Electrons (superficial tumors)
� Particle beam RT � E.g., prostate seed implants

 � Protons (pediatrics) � High-dose rate (HDR)
 � Carbon
 � Helium � E.g., intracavitary BT for cervix
 � Other ions
Linear Accelerator
(LINAC) for EBRT

Electrons accelerated to a tungsten
target in the head of the gantry results
in production of photons
Conventional RT

2D plans � simple setup with 2-4 fields
3D Conformal RT

More complex, with more fields; can contour target structures and organs at risk (OAR)
Multi-leaf collimators (MLCs)

Individual leaves
within head of
gantry that are used
to shape the
radiation beam for a
more conformal
radiation plan
Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy
(IMRT)

Non-uniform intensity of radiation
across each beam to allow for more
conformal dose distribution

� Segmental IMRT: "step & shoot"

� Dynamic IMRT: MLC leaves in
 continuous motion during
 treatment of each field
Volumetric Intensity-Modulated Arc
Radiation Therapy (VMAT)

Radiation is delivered continuously as
the beam rotates, and beam is
dynamically modulated by MLCs
throughout duration of treatment
Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) &
Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy (SBRT)

Highly targeted technique which
involves 1-5 fractions with very
high dose per fraction

 SRS: typically refers to brain tumors
 (GammaKnife, CyberKnife)

 SBRT: stereotactic radiation to other
 parts of the body
Low-Dose Rate (LDR) Brachytherapy

E.g., prostate seed
implants

Involves one
procedure for
implantation of
radioactive seeds
High-Dose Rate (HDR)
Brachytherapy

Applicator is placed inside patient (e.g., intracavitary
tandem-based BT for cervical cancer) and connected to
radioactive source on a wire for a few minutes at a time

Radioactive source contained within device called an
"afterloader"
When is radiation used?
Radiation can cure cancer!

Definitive radiation or chemoradiation

 Head & neck
 Lung
 Prostate
 Anal
 Cervix

Adjuvant radiation or chemoradiation

 Glioma/glioblastoma
 Breast
 Pancreas
 Sarcoma

... to name a few!
Palliative radiation

In the setting of metastatic or incurable
disease

 Pain (e.g., bone metastases)

 metastases)

 Airway obstruction/hemoptysis
 GI or pelvic bleeding
Oligometastatic disease

Consolidative EBRT or SRS/SBRT to locally control oligometastatic or
oligoprogressive disease
Radiation for treatment
of benign conditions

Meningioma (range benign to malignant)
Acoustic neuroma
Trigeminal neuralgia
Tremors (thalamic ablation)
Keloids
Dupuytren's contracture
Prophylaxis of heterotopic ossification
The End!

Introduction to
Radiation Biology

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Objectives

What is radiation?

 Ionizing vs. Non-ionizing radiation
 Electromagnetic waves vs. Particulate radiation

How does radiation work?

 Direct vs. Indirect ionization
 Direct vs. Indirect action of radiation
 Types of DNA-damage & relationship to cell-cycle

Why is radiation given in fractions?

 4 Rs of Radiobiology
What is radiation?
Radiation is Energy

Electromagnetic waves (x-rays, gamma-rays) or particles (electrons, protons, alpha particles,
neutrons)

When radiation hits an atom or molecule, 2 things can happen:

1. Excitation: electron to higher energy state 2. Ionization: electron ejected from orbital
Excitation
Ionization
PHOTONS = Electromagnetic Radiation
Particulate Radiation (all ionizing)
How does radiation work?
Direct vs. Indirect Ionization

Charged particles are directly ionizing

 Directly pass through tissue (provided they have enough energy)
 Directly produce chemical and biologic changes

Electromagnetic waves & neutrons are indirectly ionizing

 Do not produce chemical and biologic damage themselves
 They are absorbed as they pass through material & give up their energy to produce fast-

 moving charged particles that in turn produce damage

 X-rays and gamma rays --> fast electrons
 Neutrons --> "spallation products" (hit an atom and produce multiple alpha particles)
Photons: Different Interactions at
Different Energies

The Photoelectric Effect The Compton Effect
(a) photon absorption and electron ejection (a) photon scattering and electron
and (b) fluorescent X-ray emission. ejection leading to (b) an ionized atom
= photons
Direct vs. Indirect Action of Radiation

 Generation of
 free radicals
Radiation-induced
DNA Damage

Single-strand breaks

 Generally not lethal; can be repaired

 Base excision repair, Nucleotide excision repair, Mismatch
 repair

Double-strand breaks

 Lethal if not repaired

 Defects in non-homologous (G1 phase) and homologous (late
 S/G2 phase) recombination lead to increased radiation
 sensitivity

Chromatid aberrations

 Dicentrics, rings, and anaphase bridges are lethal
The Cell Cycle & Radiation

 Late S/G2:
 Most resistant
 to radiation

 M phase:
 Most sensitive
 to radiation
Cell Sensitivity to Radiation
Why is radiation given in
fractions?
The 4 Rs of 1. Repair
Radiobiology 2. Repopulation
 3. Redistribution
 4. Reoxygenation
Repair &
Repopulation

Cancer cells and normal tissue cells have
different repair abilities after radiation

 Fractionation takes advantage of this & gives
 normal tissue time to heal to decrease radiation
 toxicity

Normal tissues & tumor cells are capable of
increasing their cell replication rate in response
to depopulation from radiation

 E.g., healthy skin tissue that regenerates rapidly
 can allow for quick healing

 New mix of healthy and tumor tissue as cells
 within radiated region die
Redistribution

After one dose of radiation, the cells that are in
radiosensitive stage (M phase) are most likely to
undergo DNA damage and die

In between RT fractions, surviving cells can be
redistributed to various stages of cell cycle

 Allows time for more cells to reach M phase

Cells are most radioresistant in late S/G2 phase

 DNA has been replicated

 If there is DNA damage, they have a template based on which
 repair it
Reoxygenation

Cells are more sensitive to radiation in the
presence of oxygen

 Oxygen is required to create free radicals that kill
 cancer cells

Oxygen enhancement ratio

 Higher for sparsely ionizing radiation (x-rays,
 gamma rays)

 Lower for intermediate ionizing density (e.g.,
 neutrons)

 For densely ionizing radiation (e.g., alpha particles)
 -- there is no OER
WAIT � WHAT ABOUT HYPOFRACTION ATION?
That's all for now

Photon Interactions
with Matter
What is a A massless packet of energy
photon? Moves at the speed of light
Ionizing radiation
4 Main Interactions with Matter

1. Coherent scattering The probability of an interaction
2. Photoelectric effect occurring depends on:
3. Compton effect
4. Pair production (1) energy of the photon, &

 (2) properties of the absorbing
 material (Z = atomic number)
Coherent Scattering

Photon undergoes elastic scattering event with the material.
No energy is lost by the photon, as photon doesn't have enough energy to liberate electrons.
Primarily for low energy photons.

Rayleigh scattering = elastic scattering by Probability of Coherent Scattering:
ALL the electrons in the atom
- Think "R-ALL-eigh" Z
 E2
Thompson scattering = elastic scattering by
ONE electron in the atom
- Think "Thomps-ONE"
Photoelectric Effect

Photon has high enough energy that it causes ejection of an inner shell electron.
Minimum photon energy must at least equal binding energy of the inner shell electron.
After inner shell electron is ejected, outer shell electron (high energy) takes its place.
This results in release of energy as a characteristic X-ray and/or Auger electron.

Resonance phenomenon: Probability of Photoelectric Effect:

Photons with energy closest to binding Z3
energy electron are more likely to result in E3
photoelectric effect.
Incident photon Incident photon Characteristic
 X-ray
 K-shell Characteristic
 L-shell X-ray
 M-shell
 K-shell Auger
 L-shell electron
 M-shell
Resonance

Photons with energy closest to binding energy electron
are more likely to result in photoelectric effect.

This phenomenon is called resonance.

Resonance peaks are named for their electron
shells (e.g., K-edge).

These peaks are exploited for use in imaging
contrast for diagnostic imaging.
Characteristic X-rays

Characteristic X-rays are emitted as outer shell
electrons decay to a lower energy inner shell.

Energy is tied to quantized energy levels between
electron orbitals unique to a given element.
Compton Effect/Compton Scatter

Main interaction that takes place at Incident photon Scattered lower
therapeutic levels of radiation. energy photon

Most probable interaction in water from Ejected
30 KeV to 24 MeV. electron

Photon has high enough energy that Can directly
electron is ejected from the outer shell. or indirectly
 cause DNA
The incident photon retains some energy damage
and is scattered, now with lower energy.
Compton Scattering Angle

As photon energy increases, the direction of scatter is more forward-peaked.
 0-degree scatter: photon loses no energy

 90-degree scatter: photon retains up to 0.511 MeV

 180-degree backscatter: photon retains up to 0.255 MeV
Pair Production

Occurs via interaction of a photon & atomic nucleus.
Technically, needs at least 1.022 MeV (twice the rest mass of an electron, which is 0.511 MeV).
However, not actually a clinical concern in radiation therapy for beams under 10 MV.

How are Photons
Generated?
Linear
Accelerator
(LINAC)

Microwave technology used to
accelerate electrons towards a heavy
metal target (typically tungsten).

This process
generates Brehmsstrahlung X-rays
(braking radiation).
Brehmsstrahlung (Braking) Radiation

Produced as a charged particle slows & scatters during interaction with an atomic nucleus.
Highly inefficient process.
Average energy of X-ray beam is 1/3 to 1/2 of the max energy of incoming electrons.

 Photon emission

 Electron path bent by interaction
 with heavy atomic nucleus
LINAC Head

Components within LINAC head.

Flattening filter in place to help create
a more homogenous beam.

Jaws & multi-leaf collimators (MLCs)
drawn into place to help increase
conformality with a treatment target
volume.
Photon vs. Electrons in LINAC

 LINAC can also be used for
 electron therapy.

 Electrons mass is 9 x 10-31 kg
 (1/2000 that of a proton) --> good
 for treating superficial tumors.

 Flattening filter is changed for a
 scattering foil to produce
 electrons. An electron applicator
 (cone) shaped to the tumor is
 applied below the secondary
 collimators (jaws).
LINAC Gantry

The gantry can rotate around the patient,
delivering radiation from different angles.

The treatment couch can also be moved.
What happens when photon enters the
patient?

Remember, at therapeutic energies, X-rays Source
enter the patient & predominant interaction is
the Compton effect.

Ionizing radiation causes electrons to be
released from outer shell of the atoms orbit.

The incident photons are scattered and
continue to hit other atoms, causing ejection of
more electrons.

 Patient
Photon Beam Percent Depth Dose

Initially, it takes some time to reach the
maximum dose (Dmax). This is because
electrons have to be ejected as photon
interacts with matter.

This is called the build-up region.

Once Dmax is reached, the effect of
beam attenuation takes over.

As more and more electrons are ejected, the
photons keep scattering and losing energy.

Despite attenuation, photons always have an
entry dose as well as an exit dose.
Depth of
Dmax Varies
with Beam
Energy

Higher energies have
a deeper Dmax

Surgery

LOBECTOMY IS CONSIDERED GOLD STANDARD
WEDGE RESECTION OR SEGMENTECTOMY NON-INFERIOR IF <2 CM
LCSG: Lobectomy vs. Limited Resection

Ginsberg & Rubinstein, Ann Thorac Surg 1995

1982-1988: 276 patients with suspected cT1 N0 (3 cm) lung
cancer on chest X-ray randomized

 Lobectomy
 Limited resection � segmentectomy vs. wedge resection (2 cm margin)

Significantly worse local recurrence risk with limited resection

 Risk of local recurrence ~3X as high with sublobar resection
 Lung cancer related mortality ~50% higher with sublobar resection

 Ginsberg trial failed to demonstrate noninferiority of limited resection.

 Lobectomy should remain the standard of care for early-stage NSCLC.
Alliance/CALGB 140503: Sublobar resection
for tumors 2 cm

Altorki et al., NEJM 2023

2007-2017: 697 patients with 2cm tumor on CT with presumed or
confirmed NSCLC randomized

 Lobectomy
 Sublobar resection � wedge resection or segmentectomy

Sublobar resection was noninferior in terms of OS, DFS, and LRR

 5-year DFS 64% in both arms
 5-year OS ~80% both arms
 5-year rate of recurrence ~30% in both arms

 >50% of recurrences were distant

Small but significant decline in FEV1 and FVC after sublobar resection vs.
lobectomy (~2%, may be clinically meaningful in patients with
compromised lung function)

 Alliance Trial: sublobar resection (wedge resection or segmentectomy) is
 non-inferior to lobectomy for early-stage NSCLC 2 cm.
Major For patients who are surgical candidates, resection has been
Takeaways standard of care for early-stage lung cancer

 Tumors 2 cm sublobar resection (wedge resection vs.
 segmentectomy)

 Tumors >2 cm lobectomy

 VATS approach whenever possible

 Less invasive & a/w decreased pain, improved return to
 function, fewer complications

LACE: Pooled analysis of postoperative
cisplatin-based chemotherapy

Pignon et al., JCO 2008

Meta-analysis of 4584 patients across 5 RCTs
(1994-2001) comparing:

 Postop cisplatin-based chemotherapy vs. no
 chemotherapy

 Postop cisplatin-based chemoradiotherapy vs.
 radiotherapy alone

Improved OS & DFS with chemotherapy

 5.4% absolute 5-year OS benefit from chemotherapy
 Benefit varied based on stage & KPS

 Higher stage, better KPS more benefit to chemotherapy
 Saw greatest benefit in stage II & III (HR 0.83, 95% CI 0.72-0.95),

 stage IB not significant (HR 0.93, 95% CI 0.78-1.10)

LACE pooled analysis: OS benefit to adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with tumors 4 cm (T2b+, stage II)
 - Stage IB (T2a, 3 cm tumor) may consider referral to oncology
 - Stage IA typically no adjuvant chemotherapy

 cycles PD-L1 <1% (IMpower010)

CheckMate 816: Neoadj Nivolumab + CHT

Forde et al., NEJM 2022

2017-2019: phase III trial of 505 patients with stage II+ (AJCC version 8, 4 cm)
disease NSCLC randomized 1:1

 Neoadjuvant nivolumab + platinum-doublet CHT x3 cycles SGY
 Neoadjuvant platinum-doublet CHT alone SGY
 3rd arm [ipi/nivo alone SGY] closed early

1o endpoints of EFS and pCR rate significantly improved with neoadj Nivo + CHT

 2-year EFS 64% with nivo vs. 45% without (p=0.005)
 Rate of pathologic complete response 24% with nivo vs. 2% without (p<0.001)
 31% with nivo and 24% without had radiographic downstaging
 Rate of R0 resection 83% with nivo and 78% without

2o endpoint of OS improved with nivo

 2-year OS 83% with nivo vs. 71% without (p=0.008)
 ctDNA clearance was also higher with nivolumab + CHT (56% vs. 35%)

No significant differences in toxicities/adverse events between arms

CheckMate 816: First phase III study to support neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy for stage II+ patients.
 Improved both primary (EFS, pCR) & secondary (OS, ctDNA clearance) endpoints.

RTOG 0236: phase II 60 Gy/3 Fx (54 Gy/3 Fx)

Timmerman et al., JAMA 2010

First multicenter, prospective trial of lung SBRT in early-stage NSCLC 3-yr OS = 56%
Eligibility criteria (n = 59) 3-yr DFS = 48%

 Biopsy proven, INOPERABLE NSCLC RTOG 0236 treated medically
 T1-T2 (5 cm) tumors, located 2 cm from proximal bronchial tree inoperable early-stage peripheral
 No prior thoracic radiation allowed NSCLC with ~54 Gy/3 Fx 91% local

Treatment control at 3 years

 Allowed 5 mm axial margins, 10 mm craniocaudal (motion, set-up)

 No CTV margin for subclinical spread

 Prescribed 60 Gy/3 Fx given over 8-14 days

 But did not use tissue heterogeneity corrections. Later analysis with corrections showed
 actual delivered dose was closer to 54 Gy/3 Fx (Xiao et al., IJROBP 2010).

Primary outcome = tumor control

 91% 3-year primary tumor control

 Primary mode of failure = distant

 13% grade 3 & 4% grade 4 toxicities; no grade 5 toxicity (death)
 Overall ~25% toxicity counting non-protocol specified events (skin, ribs)
RTOG 0618: 54 Gy/3 Fx for operable NSCLC

Timmerman et al., JAMA 2018

 2007-2010 (n = 33)

 T1-2 tumors (5 cm), located 2 cm from PBT with
 adequate lung function

 Patients deemed OPERABLE by thoracic surgery

 Treatment identical to RTOG 0236
 Primary tumor control at 2 years was 96%
 15% experienced grade 3 toxicity; no grade 4-5
 toxicities

 RTOG 0618: 54 Gy/3 Fx for operable early-stage peripheral NSCLC 96% local control at 2 years

Videtic et al., IJROBP 2016

2009-2011 (n = 94), same eligibility criteria as RTOG 0236 RTOG 0915 established 48 Gy/4 Fx and 34 Gy/1 Fx as
 alternative fractionation schemes for peripheral tumors.
This time patients were randomized

 34 Gy/1 Fx vs. 48 Gy/4 Fx over 4 consecutive days

Treatment

 Motion management � abdominal compression, gating,
 breath hold

 PTV margins made in two ways (again, no CTV)

 Conventional CT sim: 5 mm axial, 10 mm CC like 0236
 4D sim: ITV made to account for motion, then 5 mm PTV margin

Primary endpoint was grade 3+ toxicity at 1 year

 10% with 34/1 vs. 13% with 48/4
 2 grade 5 events (one per arm)

Primary tumor control was 97% (34/1) vs. 93% (48/4)

Based on how the trial was designed, 34 Gy/1 deemed
superior, but both fractionation schedules met
prespecified endpoints

Treatment Need to be very cautious for central lung tumors.

The HILUS Trial: Nordic Phase II
ULTRACENTRAL Lung Tumors

Lindberg et al., JTO 2021

 Moderately central & ultracentral tumors were both included in
 RTOG 0813

 Ultracentral however clearly have greater risk for high-grade toxicity

 HILUS = phase II trial of risk-adapted SBRT within 1 cm of
 proximal bronchial tree

 2011-2016, n = 65
 T1-T2 N0 tumors within 1 cm of proximal bronchial tree

 Group A = within 1 cm of the trachea & mainstem bronchi
 Group B = all other tumors

 Rx = 7 Gy x 8 Fx to 67% isodose line (lower than usual 78-80%)

 Results

 83% 2-year local control
 Arm A closed early due to toxicity
 Grade 3-5 toxicity in 22 patients (8/10 deaths � bronchopulmonary

 hemorrhage)

 Need to be careful with ultracentral tumors!
(especially if overlapping trachea or mainstem bronchi)
SUNSET Trial: 8 Fractions for Ultracentral

Giulani et al., IJROBP 2024

Study designed with time-to-event continual
reassessment

 Plan was 60 Gy/8 Fx 60/10 60/15
 Stopped at 60 Gy/8 Fx which was deemed safe
 Difference vs. HILUS: moderate hotspot (120% max)

2018-2021: 30 patients enrolled with ultracentral tumors
(abutting critical structures), T1-3 N0 NSCLC

Median 3-year follow-up

 2 G3+ toxicities: 1 G3 dyspnea, 1 G5 pneumonia
 3-year local control 90%, PFS 66%, OS 73%

 SUNSET Trial showed safety and efficacy of 60 Gy/8 Fx
 fractionation scheme for central and ultracentral tumors
 (especially ultracentral, abutting mediastinal structures).

Pooled analysis of two independent randomized clinical trials that

Lung Cancer Overview

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Lung Cancer

 2nd most common cancer in
 the US (not counting skin)

 Leading cause of cancer
 death in the US

20% of lung cancers are not
related to smoking!

 NSCLC vs. SCLC

 o NSCLC more common
 o SCLC more aggressive,

 almost always a/w smoking
 history, higher propensity for
 distant failure & brain
 metastases
Objectives

Presentation & Workup

 Screening guidelines
 Workup
 Pathology
 Molecular testing

NSCLC � Anatomy, Staging, & Management

SCLC � Staging & Management
Presentation & Workup
Presentation 50-80

Varies depending on stage & location National Lung Cancer Screening Trial
 randomized patients to screening CT vs. CXR
May be detected on low-dose screening CT and found 20% relative reduction in mortality
 with low-dose CT screening (NEJM 2011).
 USPSTF screening guidelines: Annual LDCT for ages 50-80
 with 20 pack-year smoking history who actively smoke or
 have quit within the last 15 years

May be symptomatic, especially if locally advanced

 Cough, shortness of breath, hoarseness, chest pain, weight
 loss, pain, pleural effusion, post-obstructive pneumonia

Paraneoplastic syndromes

 SCC � hypercalcemia from PTH-related peptide
 SCLC � SIADH, Lambert-Eaton syndrome, cerebellar ataxia

Symptoms from metastases if present (e.g., headaches
or neuro sx from brain mets)
Risk Factors Patients with SCLC almost always have smoking history.
 This is not the case for NSCLC (15-20% may be non-smokers).
Smoking
 Non-smokers tend to develop lung adenocarcinomas rather
Radon than squamous cell carcinomas.

Asbestos Non-smokers with lung adenocarcinoma, especially females
 who are non-smokers, often carry EGFR mutation (targetable
Family history
 mutation) and have improved prognosis.
Pulmonary fibrosis

Occupational exposures

 Silica
 Cadmium
 Arsenic
 Beryllium
 Diesel exhaust
 Coal soot
Screening LD-CT: nodules vs. mass

Nodules: Low Risk High Risk
 (No risk factors) (Smoking, family history or other
 <6 mm exposure risk factors)
 6-8 mm
 >8 mm No routine follow-up CT at 12 months (optional)

 CT at 6-12 months

 Consider CT at 3 months, FDG-PET/CT, or biopsy

Also consider presence of risk factors � smoking history, radiographic appearance
Lesion 3 cm or larger is considered a mass cancer until proven otherwise
Smooth margin � more likely to be benign Spiculated margin � more concerning for malignancy
Workup

Full history & physical

 Inquire about risk factors � age, smoking history, previous cancer history, family history, occupation
 Smoking cessation counseling

Labs � CBC, CMP

PFTs � FEV1 >40%, DLCO >40% at minimum for medical operability

Imaging

 Dedicated CT chest
 FDG PET-CT Scan
 Brain MRI with contrast

 NSCLC mandatory for stage II (>4 cm) & optional for stage IB (>3 cm); mandatory for all SCLC

Pathology & Mediastinal Staging

 Stage IA lung cancer (1 cm) with high pre-test probability of malignancy may proceed to surgery
 If no preop bx, need intraoperative wedge resection or needle biopsy before proceeding with lobectomy
 Most cases, prefer preoperative biopsy � CT-guided (peripheral) or endoscopic (central) FNA

 Non-lung cancer diagnosis (e.g., granulomatous nodule)
 Suspected stage IB or higher who may be candidates for systemic therapy prior to surgery
 Intraoperative diagnosis difficult or risky
 Establish diagnosis prior to SBRT if patients are not surgical candidates or do not want surgery
Pathology

Types of Histologies Clinical Features Location

Lung Cancer

NSCLC Adenocarcinoma Most common histology, ~40% of all lung Peripheral
 cancers, ~20% may not be a/w smoking

 Squamous cell ~20% of all lung cancers Central

 Others � large cell, carcinoid Less common histologies
 (low-grade neuroendocrine)

SCLC High-grade neuroendocrine ~10-15% of all lung cancers; almost Central
 ALWAYS a/w smoking

Pathologic analysis performed by Giraud et al., IJROBP 2000:

 Recommend 6 mm CTV margin for squamous and 8 mm CTV margin for adenocarcinoma to include
 95% of microscopic extension
Mediastinal Staging ~15% of patients with negative N2/N3 nodes on
 PET CT will have +N2/N3 nodes on surgical staging
 Endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS)
Samples levels (Al Sarraf et al., 2008)
2, 4, 7, 10
 Mediastinoscopy Samples levels
 2, 4, 7

ASTER Trial (Annema et al., JAMA 2010): RCT that showed that EUS-FNA/EBUS followed by
mediastinoscopy if nothing found on EBUS is more sensitive than mediastinoscopy alone.
 Sensitivities: 79% mediastinoscopy alone, 84% EBUS alone, 94% EBUS+mediastinoscopy.
PD-L1 Testing

Programmed Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1) may be
expressed by cancer cells and prevents T-cells
from killing them

Tumor proportion score (TPS) measures the
percentage of viable tumor cells that show
partial or complete membrane staining PD-L1

 TPS 1% is considered positive

Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) that target
PD-1 or PD-L1 can be added for all patients for
NSCLC & SCLC

In advanced or metastatic setting, ICI should be
incorporated if TPS+ (TPS 1%)

 ICI monotherapy may be considered if TPS 50%
Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) for
Targetable Mutations in NSCLC

NGS should be performed on tissue samples whenever possible
If presence of targetable mutation, targeted therapy should take precedence over ICI

Gene Type of Mutation Drug
EGFR Exon 19 deletion; Osimertinib
 Exon 21 L858R
ALK Exon 20 insertion Amivantamab-vmjw + carboplatin + pemetrexed (non-squamous)
ROS1 Rearrangement Alectinib (stage I-III or metastatic), Lorlatinib (metastatic; higher CNS toxicity)
BRAF V600E Rearrangement Repotrictinib
NTRK 1/2/3 Point mutation Vemurafenib
MET Gene Fusion Larotrectinib, Entrectinib
RET Exon 14 skipping mutation Capmatinib
ERBB2 (HER2) Rearrangement Cabozantinib
KRAS Mutation Trastuzumab-emtansine
 G12C mutation 2nd line targeted therapies after initial systemic tx � Sotorasib, Adagrasib
Additional Test for Advanced
or Metastatic (Stage IV) NSCLC

Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) � "liquid biopsy"
 As tumor cells undergo normal turnover and die/get broken down
 into the bloodstream, they release cell components, including DNA
 ctDNA

 ctDNA can be sampled through a blood test and mutational
 testing/next gen sequencing can identify potentially targetable
 mutations

 Historically, mutational analysis was always done directly on tumor
 tissue, and this is still done in current era

 ctDNA analysis is faster and may help patients start therapy sooner

 Examples � Guardant360 (73 genes), InVisionFirst-Lung (37 genes)

ctDNA testing does NOT replace histological tissue diagnosis

Surgery

Lobectomy is the historical standard and always preferred
over pneumonectomy

Sublobar resection (segmentectomy or wedge resection) are
noninferior to lobectomy for tumors 2 cm (T1a-b)

 Aim for margins 2 cm or at least the size of the nodule removed

Minimally invasive surgery (VATS or robotic-assisted
approaches) preferred whenever possible
Nodal Assessment During Surgery

N1 and N2 node resection and mapping should
be routinely performed

 Minimum of one N1 and three N2 stations should
 be sampled

 Or can do complete lymph node dissection

If N2 disease is present, need formal ipsilateral
mediastinal lymph node dissection
Systemic Therapy & Surgery

Stage IB disease (>3 cm, airway or visceral pleural involvement) refer to medical oncology

 Systemic therapy is often considered for other high-risk features as well including poor differentiation, well-differentiated neuroendocrine
 tumors, vascular invasion, etc.

Always test for PD-L1 status, EGFR, and ALK rearrangements

Patients with tumors 4 cm or N+ without EGFR or ALK mutation should be evaluated for preoperative therapy

 If candidates for ICI: neoadjuvant ICI + platinum-based doublet chemotherapy

 Example: Nivolumab + carboplatin/paclitaxel q3 weeks x3 cycles

 If not candidates for ICI: neoadjuvant platinum-based doublet chemotherapy

 Example: cisplatin/pemetrexed for non-squamous histology, or cisplatin/gemcitabine or cisplatin/docetaxel for squamous

If induction therapy is not given, consider adjuvant therapy for tumors 4 cm or N+

 For all patients, consider platinum-based doublet chemotherapy x4 cycles

 Cisplatin/pemetrexed if non-squamous histology
 Cisplatin/gemcitabine or Cisplatin/docetaxel for squamous histology

 Consider targeted therapy if EGFR or ALK mutation

 EGFR mutation (exon 19 deletion, exon 21 L858R mutation) adjuvant Osimertinib
 ALK rearrangement adjuvant Alectinib

 If no targetable mutation ICI if PD-L1 1%
 If no targetable mutation & PD-L1 <1% or not candidate platinum-based doublet chemotherapy alone

Oligometastatic Disease

Concept of oligometastatic disease first
introduced by Hellman & Weichselbaum in
1995

Represents an intermediate state between
localized cancer & widely metastatic disease

 May benefit from aggressive local therapy
 SBRT ideal modality (noninvasive, effective,

 convenient)

Rationale to aim for consolidation in
oligometastatic setting

 Hopes of getting to a point where
 oligometastatic disease can be considered
 curable

 There are many different types of
 oligometastatic disease!

 Guckenberger et al., Lancet 2020
SABR-COMET: SABR vs. Palliation for 1-5 Mets

Palma et al., JCO 2020

Phase II: 2012-2016 (pre-IO), 99 patients with metastatic
cancer & 1-5 metastases randomized

 Palliative RT alone (8 Gy/1 Fx, 20 Gy/5 Fx, 30 Gy/10 Fx)
 SBRT (SABR) to all sites + palliative systemic treatments

 SBRT doses varied by site

Majority of patients (53% & 43%) had lung cancer

 Other primaries � colorectal, breast, prostate

Primary outcome was overall survival

 Median OS 28 months palliative RT vs. 50 months SBRT (p =
 0.006)

 Note, pre-specified alpha value was 0.2 (so p < 0.2
 considered significant)

Median PFS was 5.4 months palliative RT vs. 11.6 months
SBRT (p = 0.001)
"Oli-Gomez" Trial: Local MDT vs. maintenance

Gomez et al., JCO 2019

 Phase II: 2012-2016 (pre-IO era) patients with stage IV
 NSCLC with 0-3 oligometastases (not counting primary)
 randomized after initial CHT

 Local consolidative therapy (SGY, RT, SBRT - MDT) to all
 active disease + concurrent/maintenance therapy

 Maintenance therapy

 Study was terminated early due to favorable results

 Median OS 41.2 months with LCT vs. 17 months with
 standard maintenance

 Median PFS 14.2 months with LCT vs. 4.4 months with
 standard maintenance

 No grade 4-5 toxicity
SINDAS: First-line TKI � SBRT to Oligomets

Wang et al., JNCI 2022

Phase III RCT 2016-2019: 133 patients with EGFR-mutated NSCLC
and 5 metastases randomized

 First-generation TKI (erlotinib, gefitinib, icotinib) + no RT
 First-gen TKI + SBRT to all sites of metastasis (25-40 Gy/5 Fx)

Significantly improved PFS (primary endpoint) and OS (secondary
endpoint) with SBRT

 Median PFS 12.5 mo TKI alone vs. 20.2 mo SBRT (p<0.001)
 Median OS 17.4 mo TKI alone vs. 25.5 mo SBRT (o<0.001)

6% grade 3-4 pneumonitis in TKI + RT arm; no grade 5 events

Trial ended early due to efficacy in SBRT arm
NROG-002: 60 Gy Thoracic RT + TKI (EGFRm)

Sun et al., JCO 2024

2016-2022: 118 patients with EGFRm NSCLC (19del or L858R) TKI alone TKI + RT
and 1-3 oligomets (treated at discretion), � brain metastases
(must be conscious) randomized (allowed up to 2 cycles OS 1-year 88.1% 1-year 91.5%
neoadjuvant chemotherapy) 3-year 27.7% 3-year 44.5%
 5-year 12.8% 5-year 24.9%
 Oral icotinib (125 mg TID) alone � first gen TKI
 Icotinib + 60 Gy/30 Fx thoracic RT concurrently to primary and PFS 1-year 47.5% 1-year 61.0%
 3-year 7.2% 3-year 21.3%
 regional lymph nodes 5-year 2.7% 5-year 11.4%

Significant PFS and OS benefit in TKI + thoracic RT group p=0.0041 p=0.029

 Subgroup analysis showed greater benefit to thoracic RT in
 patients who were female, age 60, and no induction
 chemotherapy

 Benefit to thoracic RT regardless of brain metastases

Higher rates of esophagitis, pneumonitis, and leukopenia with
thoracic RT

2019-2021: 106 patients (59 NSCLC & 47 breast cancer) with 5 LUNG
oligoprogressive metastases after 1st line systemic therapy randomized

 SBRT to all progressive sites + SOC

 SOC alone

Median PFS 3.2 months SOC vs. 7.2 months SBRT (p=0.002)

Stratified analysis showed PFS benefit to NSCLC BREAST

Observed significant decline in 8-week ctDNA for SBRT vs. SOC in

STOP Trial: SBRT vs. SOC for oligomets

Schellenberg et al., IJROBP 2024

Phase II RCT: initially tried to enroll only NSCLC patients but had difficulty accruing

Ultimately accrued 90 patients with oligoprogressive nonhematologic malignancies
and randomized 2:1

 SBRT to all sites of oligoprogression
 SOC systemic therapy

No difference in PFS or OS between arms

 PFS Kaplan Meier curves crossed
 2-year PFS 9% SBRT vs. 24% SOC (p=0.91)
 Median OS 31.2 mo SBRT vs. 27.4 mo SOC (p=0.22)

Histologies � 44% lung, 23% GU, 13% breast

 No difference in PFS or OS based on histology
 Breast seemed to show improved PFS with SBRT but n=12

Comments

 Small study, lower accrual than desired
 Potential crossover � beyond 2 years, 86% of patients in SOC arm received RT
NRG-LU002: SBRT/SGY vs. IO/CHT

Iyengar et al., ASCO 2024 � Abstract

2017-2021: 215 patients with NSCLC s/p first-line induction therapy with no progression and 1-3 sites
of oligometastasis after induction therapy randomized

 Maintenance systemic therapy alone
 Local consolidative therapy (LCT) maintenance systemic therapy

 LCT = SBRT or SBRT & SGY to all sites of metastasis (1-3 discrete sites) + RT to primary site

Heterogenous patient population �

 Included de novo & recurrent stage IV NSCLC
 Allowed for prior RT to primary disease in case of recurrent metastatic disease
 Allowed for N+ disease and counted that as primary disease

No significant difference in PFS with addition of LCT

 2-year PFS 36% with maintenance alone vs. 40% with LCT + maintenance (p=0.66)
 90% received IO-based systemic therapy and HR for PFS with LCT added to IO-based tx was 0.90

Interim results don't show a benefit, but this was a highly heterogenous population

 Possible that patients with less locally advanced primaries would benefit from local consolidative tx

Summary SBRT to oligometastatic or oligoprogressive disease

 Some phase II evidence that this provides PFS (possibly OS?)
 benefit

 Trials like STOP and NRG-LU002 indicate that patients need to be

German HD14: "2 + 2" 30 Gy IFRT

von Tresckow et al., JCO 2012

2003-2008: 1655 patients with early-stage unfavorable cHD (HD11 inclusion criteria) randomized

Freedom from treatment failure (1� endpoint) improved with 2+2 regimen 30 Gy IFRT

 No significant difference in 5-year OS: ~97% in both arm
 Significantly higher acute toxicity with 2+2 regimen: G3-4 80% vs. 51% and G4 56% vs. 6%

 For unfavorable early-stage cHD, 2+2 30 Gy improves PFS but not OS.
 This regimen became standard for GHSG.

 Note: Intensification of therapy (with BEACOPP or RT) is more standard in Europe in general. In US,

German HD17: PET-guided RT omission

Borchmann et al., Lancet 2021

2012-2017: 1100 patients with early-stage unfavorable disease randomized

Summary of Early-Stage Favorable
Management
 � ABVD x2 Restage with PET

Chemotherapy

ABVD = Adriamycin (Doxorubicin), Bleomycin, Vinblastine, Dacarbazine

 Each cycle is one month (28 days), 2 infusions per cycle (days 1 & 15)
 Acute toxicities: nausea, vomiting, hair loss, bone marrow suppression
 Late toxicities: cardiac & pulmonary toxicity
 Can de-intensify with AVD to decrease pulmonary toxicity

BEACOPP = Bleomycin, Etoposide, Adriamycin (Doxorubicin), Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine,
Procarbazine, Prednisone

 Intensified treatment studied in setting of poor response or for unfavorable patients
 Higher incidence of bone marrow suppression & alopecia

Stanford V = Nitrogen mustard, Doxorubicin, Vinblastine, Vincristine, Bleomycin, Etoposide,
Prednisone

 Quicker treatment (takes 8-12 weeks for 4-6 cycles vs. 16-24 for ABVD)
 Includes lower cumulative doses of doxorubicin and bleomycin
 Only studied with radiotherapy, which should not be omitted!
Systemic Agents for Advanced, Relapsed,
or Refractory Disease

Brentuximab vedotin (BV) = CD30 antibody

 Used in advanced & relapsed disease
 Limitations are cost & toxicity (severe neuropathy)

PD-1 inhibitors (e.g., Nivolumab)

 Used in relapsed/refractory disease

Note: if disease relapses >12 months after CR, can repeat first-line systemic therapy; if not, need
to change agent (e.g., BV or PD-1 inhibitor)
Radiotherapy
ISRT is the modern standard

 INRT requires pre-
 chemo PET in RT
 treatment position
 � ISRT does not
Principles of ISRT

Wirth et al., ILROG Guidelines, IJROBP 2020

Post-chemo CTV should encompass pre-chemo GTV cropped out of uninvolved
structures/barriers to spread due to anatomic changes from tumor shrinkage

 Craniocaudal expansion: include pre-chemo volume craniocaudally + additional margin for uncertainty
 Axial expansion: expand to boundaries of lymph node compartment

Dosing

 20-30 Gy/10-15 Fx for early-stage favorable disease
 30-36 Gy/15-20 Fx for early-stage unfavorable disease (30 Gy + 6 Gy boost for bulky disease)
 30-36 Gy/15-20 Fx for advanced disease residual or consolidation of initially bulky disease
 36-45 Gy/20-30 Fx for partial response/refractory disease

 40-45 Gy chemorefractory disease

 Many options for palliative RT: 4-30 Gy
Dose constraints for mediastinal disease

Adapted from Dabaja et al.
"Butterfly technique" static IMRT
Rationale for Omission of RT

Chemotherapy alone is NOT non-inferior to chemoradiotherapy in terms of PFS
Despite, this many still favor chemotherapy alone

 Equivalent OS without RT
 Favorable salvage rates with autologous SCT
 Long-term toxicities of RT (especially of concern due to excellent prognosis)

 However, it's always a risk versus benefit discussion!

 Chemotherapy also confers cardiac toxicity (each additional cycle = ~4 Gy mean dose to heart)
 At some point consider RT to avoid additional cycles of CHT depending on size & location of the lesion
Summary

Hodgkin lymphoma has excellent overall prognosis

 Classic HD � Reed-Sternberg Cell � CD15+, CD30+

 4 subtypes - nodular sclerosing most common

 Non-classic HD (NLPHD) � Popcorn Cell � CD20+, CD45+, CD15-, CD30-

 Behaves similarly to early-stage low-grade NHL & can treat with RT alone (30-36 Gy)

Staging (Ann Arbor) & risk-stratification

 Early-stage divided into favorable vs. unfavorable

 Generally, based on MMR, # extranodal sites, ESR/B sx but criteria vary depending on study group (GHSG vs. EORTC vs. NCCN)

 Advanced-stage prognosis based on IPS ("SAW MEAL")

Treatment paradigm: ABVD x2 PET response

 Options for radiotherapy or chemotherapy alone depending on initial stage/risk & PET response

 20 Gy for early-stage favorable; 30-36 Gy in other cases; 36-45 Gy for residual/refractory disease

 RT improves PFS, but rationale to omit RT especially in young patients due to equivalent OS with chemo alone,
 favorable salvage with autologous SCT, & long-term toxicity of RT
Thank you!

Primary CNS WBRT 24 Gy after CR to CHT, or 30-36 Gy if PR to CHT
lymphoma (No benefit to involved site boost; CSF disease treated with intrathecal CHT, not CSI)

Orbital lymphoma Dose depends on histology: 24 Gy if indolent, 30-36 Gy if DLBCL

NK-T cell Aggressive, require high doses (50 Gy) and unique CHT regimens:
lymphoma, nasal SMILE 45-50.4 Gy, GELOX 56 Gy, DeVIC with concurrent 50 Gy, 50 Gy RT alone
type Can evaluate response after 50 Gy & boost any residual disease with 5-10 Gy

Gastric MALT Antibiotic therapy (if H. pylori+): triple therapy (clarithromycin, amoxicillin, PPI) or
lymphoma quadruple therapy (bismuth, metronidazole, tetracycline, PPI);
 ISRT with 30 Gy (typically 1.5 Gy per fraction) to entire stomach volume

Testicular Sanctuary site CNS prophylaxis (MTX) & prophylactic RT to contralateral testes
lymphoma regardless of response to chemotherapy (24-30 Gy)
Primary Cutaneous Lymphomas � ILROG

Specht et al., 2015

Cutaneous Lymphoma Treatment

1� Cutaneous Follicular Involved lesion RT 24-30 Gy
Center Lymphoma, MZL, or
Anaplastic Large Cell

1� Cutaneous DLBCL, Leg Type R-CHOP x6 36-40 Gy (40 Gy if no systemic therapy)

1� Cutaneous NK-T cell 50 Gy to initial lesion with 5-10 Gy boost to residual disease
Lymphoma, Nasal Type

Mycosis fungoides 8-12 Gy at 3-5 Gy per Fx (e.g., 8 Gy/2 Fx or 12 Gy/3 Fx) for palliation
 24-30 Gy for definitive RT in unilesional disease
 TSEBT if significant involvement (historically 30-36 Gy in BID tx over 9
 weeks 10-12 Gy in modern era with repeat tx for refractory disease)
Summary

Non-Hodgkin lymphoma is a heterogenous entity
Broadly divided into aggressive vs. indolent NHL
Aggressive NHL generally Rituximab-based chemotherapy � radiation

 30-36 Gy for consolidation after CR, or 40-50 Gy if PR

Indolent NHL early-stage RT alone 24-30 Gy

 Consider 4 Gy/2 Fx for palliation in advanced-stage

2006-2011: 548 patients (614 RT sites) with follicular
NHL or MZL randomized

4 Gy/2 Fx was worse for local control but still highly

"Boom boom" still useful, often induces response

Pinnix et al., Lancet 2024

 Single-arm prospective trial of ULD (4 Gy/2 Fx) RT for gastric

 2019-2021: 24 patients with residual lymphoma after H. pylori

 Median f/u 36 months

Palliative.Bone Metastases:
Bone Metastases

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Outline

Epidemiology

Workup & Prognostication

Classification

Management

 Surgery
 Radiation

 Dexamethasone for pain flare

 Bone-modifying agents
 Radiopharmaceuticals
Epidemiology
Epidemiology of Bone Mets

Symptomatic bone metastases are a common indication for RT

Up to 80% of cancer patients develop bone metastases

 Typically occur via hematogenous spread
 Can occasionally involve bone from direct invasion

More common in axial skeleton (skull, spine, sternum, ribs) vs.
Appendicular

 T-spine most common part of spine affected
Common Cancers that Met to Bone

Top 5 cancers that met to bone: Prostate, Breast, Renal Cell, Thyroid, Lung
Workup &
Prognostication
Workup & Diagnosis

Full H&P

 Assess pain, sensory/motor dysfunction, walking ability, impending fracture risk, weight bearing bones

Imaging

 X-ray, but CT scan is more sensitive
 MRI if concern for spinal cord compression
 Bone scintigraphy � breast cancer, prostate cancer

Tissue diagnosis necessary if solitary bone tumor, new bone tumor without existing cancer
diagnosis, first metastatic relapse

 CT-guided FNA or core biopsy preferred

Consultants

 Orthopedics � weight bearing bone, concern for fracture
 Neurosurgery � c/f cord compression, skull metastasis
Mixed lytic & sclerotic mets in patient
with breast cancer

Lytic lesions: typically
appear lucent, same
density as soft tissue

Sclerotic
lesions: appear
hyperdense on CT
Predictive Model for Survival in Advanced
Cancer Patients � Chow et al., JCO 2008

Identified 3 risk factors:

 Non-breast primary
 Non-bony metastases
 KPS 60%

Median survival

 0-1 risk factors: 60 weeks
 2 risk factors: 25 weeks
 3 risk factors: 10 weeks
Predicting Survival in Patients with Bone
Metastases � Kubota et al. Japan 2019

 Retrospective review of 616 patients treated for bone mets at
 Hyogo Cancer Center

 Primary characteristics:

 Slow = breast, prostate, thyroid, multiple myeloma, lymphoma
 Moderate = renal, endometrial, ovarian, sarcoma
 Rapid = lung, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, H&N, esophageal, melanoma, gallbladder,

 cervical, other urological cancers

 Disseminated = pleural, peritoneal, or leptomeningeal dissemination
Classification
Complicated vs. Uncomplicated Bone
Metastases (Cheon et al., 2015)

Uncomplicated bone metastases

 Presence of painful bone metastases unassociated with impending or existing pathologic fracture or
 existing spinal cord or cauda equina compression

 This definition was used in most recent meta-analysis of RCTs comparing single fraction and multi-fraction RT regimens

Complicated bone metastases

 In practice, most radiation oncologists consider bone metastases causing pathologic fractures or
 compression of the spinal cord and cauda equina to be complicated

 Some also consider those with associated soft tissue components or those within weight bearing
 bones at high risk of fracture to be complicated as well, but operational definitions vary among practice
 settings
Harrington's Criteria for Prophylactic
Fixation (Harrington 1986)

 1. Cortical bone destruction >50%
 2. Metaphyseal involvement >2.5 cm
 3. Destruction of subtrochanteric femoral lesion
 4. Persistent pain following radiation

Precedes Mirel's criteria but has not been validated and use is debated

 Harrington proposed these criteria based on results of prior studies
Fracture Risk & Prophylactic Fixation �
Mirel's Scoring System (Mirel et al. 1989)

Based on retrospective study predicting risk of pathologic fracture in long bones

 Based on site of lesion, size of lesion, nature of lesion, & pain

Comments

 Pain, size of lesion, and nature of lesion correctly increased risk with each category; site did not
 Pain is only subjective in category
 Mirel's score slightly overestimates risk of fracture
Management
Treatment Options for Bone Metastases

Surgery
Radiation therapy

 EBRT vs. SBRT

Pain management

 Tylenol, NSAIDs, Narcotics

Chemotherapy (MM, SCLC, germ cell tumors)
Bone modifying agents
Unsealed sources
Surgical Treatment of Bone Metastases

Vertebroplasty:

 Percutaneous injection of bone cement
 (PMMA � polymethylmethacrylate) into
 collapsed vertebral body
Surgical Treatment of Bone Metastases

Kyphoplasty:

 Creating cavity in fractured vertebral body using percutaneously placed balloon device, followed by
 placement of bone cement in the cavity once the balloon is removed
Surgical Treatment of Bone Metastases

Intramedullary Rod:

 Rod placed in long bone for stability followed
 by cementing
Radiation After Orthopedic Stabilization
(Townsend et al., IJROBP 1995)

Retrospective review of 64 orthopedic stabilization procedures in patients with
unirradiated metastatic disease involving humerus, acetabulum, or femur

Endpoint = functional status (normal pain free use or normal use with pain)

Evaluated many potential prognostic factors

 Univariate analysis: SGY + postop RT & pre-fracture functional status were the only significant predictors for
 patients achieving good functional status

 Multivariate analysis: postop RT was the only significant factor a/w good functional status after surgery

 PORT started within 6 weeks following surgery
 Median RT dose was 30 Gy
 21 out of 25 patients with reviewable RT plans had entire hardware treated

The estimated probability of achieving normal use of the extremity (with or without pain) any time

was 53% for postoperative radiation therapy versus 11.5% for surgery alone (p<0.01)

 PORT decreases the need for 2nd surgery at the site of first surgery: 15% vs. 3% (p=0.035)
Should postoperative RT for long-bone
mets cover ALL or part of the hardware?

Retrospective analysis of 138 patients with 145 long-
bone metastases treated with postop RT at MSKCC

 Median dose 30 Gy (IQR 20-30 Gy)

Analyses

 UVA: whole hardware & higher dose significantly a/w
 lower risk of recurrence

 MVA: not significant
 Propensity score matching: whole hardware was

 significantly associated with decreased recurrence

Coverage of whole hardware was associated with
reduced risk of local recurrence, consistent with prior
reports

 Gillepsie, et al., ARO 2021
Should postoperative RT for long-bone
mets cover ALL or part of the hardware?

Harvard (Epstein-Peterson et al., PRO, 2015): retrospective review of 82 patients with non-hematological malignancies &
postop RT (on average 71% of hardware covered in RT field)

 2 factors a/w local failure on MVA: extent of hardware coverage & increased time between surgery & RT (median 20 days in this trial)
