Radiation Dose for Bone Metastases?

Update of the systematic review of palliative radiation therapy fractionation for bone
metastases (Rich et al., 2018)

 Meta-analysis of 29 randomized trials comparing single fraction to multi fraction palliative regimens for
 uncomplicated bone metastases

 Included 3059 patients treated with single fraction and 3040 patients with multi-fraction RT regimen

Single fraction and multi-fraction RT regimens demonstrate equal pain relief & toxicities

 Overall response rate: 61% vs. 62%
 Complete response rate: 23% vs. 24%
 No significant difference in risk of pathological fracture, cord compression or acute toxicity

Retreatment is more common in patients treated with single fraction

 Rate of retreatment: 20% single fraction vs. 8% multi-fraction (p < 0.01)
RTOG 9714: 8/1 vs. 30/10

898 patients with breast or prostate cancer, 1-3 painful bone mets, and at least 3-month life
expectancy randomized: 8 Gy/1 Fx vs. 30 Gy/10 Fx

 8Gy x 1 (n=455) 3Gy x 10 (n=443)

ORR (CR+PR) 65% (15+50) 66% (18 + 48)

Narcotic Relief 33% 32%

Fracture 5% 4%

Acute Toxicity G2+ 10% 17% *

Late Toxicity G2+ 4% 4%

Retreatment 18% 9% *

Both regimens were equal in terms of pain relief and narcotic use at 3 months & well-tolerated
8 Gy x1 arm had higher rate of re-treatment and less acute toxicity compared to 3 Gy x10 arm
Indications for Radiation to Bone Mets

Symptomatic lesions (e.g., pain, cord compression)

Asymptomatic high-risk bone lesions

 Gillespie et al., IJROBP 2022: Phase II RCT of prophylactic RT vs. standard of care in high-risk, asymptomatic
 bone metastases

 High risk definition:
 - bulky disease (2cm in longest diameter)
 - Junctional spine or posterior spinal element disease
 - Disease involving hip or sacroiliac joint
 - Disease in long bone involving 1/3-2/3 cortical thickness

 Primary outcome = skeletal related event (SRE), defined as fracture, cord compression, or intervention with
 surgery or radiation
 - Occurred in 29% of SOC arm vs. 1.6% of prophylactic RT arm (p < 0.001)
 - RT ppx improved time to SRE, fewer hospitalizations, improved pain, improved OS
Reirradiation for Bone Mets: RTOG 0433
8 Gy vs. 20 Gy (Chow et al., 2013)

 Randomized, non-inferiority trial of 8 Gy/1 Fx
 vs. 20 Gy/5-8 Fx for reirradiation of painful bone
 metastases

 � Primary endpoint � pain relief @ 2 months (10%
 non-inf), sum of complete & partial response

�Results - no diff in overall response rate

�Single Fx treatment less toxic

 � less lack of appetite, vomiting, diarrhea, skin
 redness
Pain Flare after Radiation for Bone Mets

Pain flare = "a temporary worsening of bone pain in the treated metastatic site"

 Incidence: up to 44% of patients after EBRT & 64% of patients after SBRT
 Negative impact of pain flare on QoL

 Increase in analgesic use --> increased side effects including dry mouth, drowsiness, and constipation

Dexamethasone has been shown to be effective in prophylaxis against pain flare

 Hypothesis: reduces edema within periosteum of treated bone
 Half-life 36-54 hours

 Can be administered for duration of treatment and a few days posttreatment to curb pain flare

Literature review by McDonald et al. (J Bone Oncol 2014)

 7 studies in patients treated with EBRT that experienced bone mets
 3 of these included investigation of steroids for pain flare prophylaxis
 Use of prophylactic dexamethasone appeared to reduce incidence and duration of pain flare
 Still, lack of consensus on dosage, frequency, and duration of intake
Dutch DEXA Study (IJROBP 2020)

Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial
of 295 patients receiving RT for painful bone mets

 Group A: 8 mg dexamethasone before RT --> 3 daily
 doses of dex

 Group B: 8 mg dexmaethasone before RT --> 3 daily
 doses placebo

 Group C: Placebo before RT --> 3 daily doses placebo

Pain flair incidence

 38% group A, 27% group B, 39% group C
 52% in group A occurred day 2-5 vs. 73% group B/C

 Dexamethasone appeared to postpone pain flare
Role of Dexamethasone for Pain Flare
Ppx Not Clear

 Dexa study:

 Dexamethasone showed
 immediate effect on pain

 Prophylactic
 dexamethasone had no
 clear impact on reducing
 pain flare incidence
ASTRO Guideline on Palliative RT for
Bone Metastases: 2017 Update

Equivalent pain relief following 8/1, 24/6, and 30/10 for previously unirradiated bone mets

 However single fraction RT associated with higher rates of retreatment
 Single fraction may be particularly convenient for patients with limited life expectancy
 No evidence that single fraction RT is a/w higher rates of pathologic fracture or late toxicity

Can offer retreatment one month following EBRT to painful bone or spinal metastases

 For persistent or recurrent pain, while adhering to normal tissue dose constraints

Surgery, radionuclides, bisphosphonates, or kypho/vertebroplasty do not obviate need for EBRT
Bone Modifying Agents

Pamidronate/zoledronate = bisphosphonates

 Inhibit osteoclasts
 Risks: associated with osteonecrosis of jaw and atypical femur fractures

Denosumab (Xgeva, Prolia) = RANKL inhibitor

 Prevents osteoclast maturation
Unsealed Sources
Sr-89, Sm-153, P-32, Ra-223

Phophorus-32: Rarely used d/t highest hematologic toxicity level. Half-life 14.3 days.
Strontium-89: pure -emitter with an energy of 1.4 MeV and a half-life of 50.6 days
Samarium-153: primarily -emitter, has a component of gamma emission, -physical half-
life 46hrs, � excreted in urine in 8hrs. Faster time to relief of pain

Radium-223: Xofigo � -emitter. Half-life of 11.4 days

 Use if no myelosuppression, good kidneys, no cord compression/impending fracture, no soft tissue
 component, pt not receiving Zytiga (ERA 223)

 ALSYMPCA trial: 921 pts with metastatic castrate resistant prostate cancer, 3 mo OS benefit (14.9 vs.
 11.3 mos), also improvement in time to first symptomatic skeletal event (SSE: 15.6 vs. 9.8 mos)

Sr-89 and Sm-153 cause a lot of heme marrow toxicity

 Consider these if acceptable blood counts and multifocal painful mets
Conclusion

Bone metastases are an extremely common indication for palliative radiation

 May or may not be emergent

May appear lytic, sclerotic, or mixed

If patient receives hardware for stabilization, be sure to cover entire length of hardware

Multiple myeloma often requires less BED (<28): common regimens 8 Gy/1 Fx or 20 Gy/10 Fx

When treating with radiation, shorter courses provide equivalent symptom control, but longer
courses provide more durable control (less likely to need retreatment)

Consider radiation not only for symptomatic bone mets, but also high-risk asymptomatic

Per ASTRO, surgery, radionuclides, bisphosphonates, or kypho/vertebroplasty do not obviate
need for EBRT
Thank you!

Cord Compression

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Outline

Anatomy
Presentation
Workup
Prognostication

 Review of evidence

Toxicity
Technique
Anatomy
Anatomy � Spinal Cord

� Spinal cord extends from foramen magnum to L1-L2 in adults (L4-L5 in
 children)

� Cauda equina: lumbar and sacral spinal nerves from L1-L2 to S2
� Dural sac surrounds the spinal cord; nerve roots exit laterally
Anatomy � Vertebral Body
MRI Spinal Cord

T2 (left): CSF appears bright
T1 (right): CSF appears dark
MRI Spinal Cord Pathology
Cord Compression

Definition: any radiographic compression of spinal
cord or cauda equina

 Epidural: tumor or bone fragment
 Intradural

 Extramedullary: leptomeningeal disease
 Intramedullary: tumor deposit within the spinal cord
MRI � Cord Compression

 Example of metastatic
 renal cell carcinoma

 Yellow arrow: epidural
 vertebral body metastasis

 Red arrow: intramedullary
 metastasis

 Green arrow: edema
 within spinal cord
Presentation
Incidence & Significance

Affects ~5-10% of all cancer patients; significant source of morbidity and mortality

 Median survival ~3 months only ~1/3 survive beyond 1 year after cord compression

Cancers causing malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC)

 Adults: Lung > Prostate > Multiple Myeloma
 Pediatric patients: most commonly caused by Ewing's sarcoma and neuroblastoma

95% of cases are epidural, typically 2/2 external compression from metastasis to vertebral body

 Growth patterns: direct growth into epidural space, neural foramina extension by paraspinal mass, cortical
 bone destruction and displacement into epidural spine

 T spine (70%) > L spine (20%) > C spine (10%)
 5% leptomeningeal, 0.5-1% intramedullary
 Multiple levels involved in ~25% of patients
Pathophysiology of Cord Compression

Pathophysiology Spinal Cord Syndromes

� Gradual progression: Transection of cord: loss of all sensory modalities
 � Epidural venous congestion vasogenic (proprioception, vibration, touch) with weakness
 edema demyelination (potentially below level of transection and bowel/bladder
 reversible) dysfunction
 � Gradual ischemia, collaterals may develop Ventral cord syndrome: weakness and loss of
 pain/temperature sensation
� Rapid progression (more severe): Dorsal cord syndrome: loss of proprioception and
 � Arterial occlusion ischemia infarction vibration, weakness, ataxia
 (irreversible)
Symptoms of Cord Compression

Back pain = most common presenting symptom (83-95%)

 Localized, radicular pain, worsened by recumbency/Valsalva
 Most pronounced at night or early in the morning when adrenal steroid secretion is lowest
 New onset in cancer patients should be taken seriously and investigated

 Back pain typically precedes neurologic symptoms by several weeks

Other symptoms

 Weakness (60-80%), Non-ambulatory (48-77%)
 Sensory deficits (50%): band-like, ascending, or saddle anesthesia depending on location
 Autonomic dysfunction (40-64%): bowel/bladder dysfunction, urinary retention
Work-up
Work-up

H&P with focus on CNS and MSK exam

 Onset of symptoms, cancer history, extent of metastatic
 disease, prior RT, pain, ECOG/KPS

Imaging

 MRI with and without gadolinium = gold standard

 93% sensitive, 97% specific
 Image entire neuro axis, as patients can have synchronous, multifocal,

 asymptomatic sites

 CT scan for osseous structure and evaluating primary/extent of
 disease

 CT myelogram if MRI cannot be obtained (metal, pacemaker)

 Similar sensitivity and specificity for cord compression
Spinal Instability Neoplastic
(SINS) Score

Helps to assess tumor related instability of vertebral column

 Useful in guiding mobilization or operative management in patients with
 neoplastic spinal disease

Criteria

 Location?
 Mechanical pain?

 improvement with recumbency or pain with movement of spine

 Bone lesion?
 Radiographic spinal alignment?
 Vertebral body collapse?
 Posterolateral involvement?

Total score

 0-6: stable spine --> generally stable, do not require surgery
 7-12: potentially unstable --> need further assessment
 13-18: unstable spine --> reliably predicts need for surgical stabilization
 Consider NSGY intervention for TS 7
Bilsky Grading for Epidural Spinal Cord
Compression

6-point MRI-based grading system using T2-weighted axial images Bone-only disease

 Used to guide decision-making for surgery and/or radiation Epidural impingement without
 deformation of thecal sac
 0 Deformation of thecal sac without spinal
 1a cord abutment
 1b Deformation of thecal sac with spinal cord
 1c abutment
 2 Spinal cord compression, but with CSF
 3 visible around the cord
 Spinal cord compression, no CSF visible
 around the cord
Management of Cord Compression

Goals are palliative: pain control, preserve neurologic function
Always consider the patient � performance status, extent of systemic disease

 Prognosis and risk stratification scoring

Steroids

 Start ASAP in patients suspected of cord compression, effective bridge to definitive treatment
 Mechanism: reduce inflammation & decease cord edema; direct oncolytic effects (MM, lymphoma)
 Monitor for hyperglycemia, hypertension, GI ppx with PPI or H2 blocker

Neurosurgery consult
Radiation
Prognostication
Prognosis & Risk Stratification Scoring

Rades Study (2002): time to motor deficit development = significant prognostic factor of functional outcome

Methods: 98 patients with spinal cord compression
and motor deficits with no prior RT

 Tx with 30 Gy/10 Fx, 37.5 Gy/15 Fx, or 40 Gy/20 Fx
 3 subgroups formed based on time of motor deficit

 development before RT

 Group A (n = 31): 1-7 days
 Group B (n = 31): 8-14 days
 Group C (n = 36): >14 days

Results: groups A, B, C varied significantly

 Ambulatory rates: 35%, 55%, 86%
 Symptom improvement: 10%, 29%, 86%

Conclusion: acute, rapid deterioration is predictive of
irreversible cord infarction

 With gradual ischemia, collaterals develop
Prognosis & Risk
Stratification Scoring

Rades Score (2008): scoring system to predict ambulatory
status after RT for MSCC (retrospective design)

Rades score predicts for post-RT ambulatory status as well as survival
Prognosis & Risk Stratification Scoring

Rades Validation of Score Predicting Post RT Ambulation (2011): prospective study to

validate score from prior retrospective study; simplified score into 3 groups

Compared post-RT ambulation rates in 653 patients with 104 patients who got surgery + RT

 Group I: Score 28, suggest short course RT for palliation of pain.
 Group II: Score 29-37, consider adding laminectomy plus stabilization to RT
 Group III: Score 38 RT alone provides excellent results
Prognosis & Risk Stratification Scoring

Another 2010 study to validate Rades Score in prediction of overall survival in MSCC patients

 Also simplified from 5 categories down to 3 categories

2008 Study OS @ 6 months 2010 Study OS @ 6 months

First Step = Steroids!

Few, small randomized controlled trials to evaluate efficacy of steroids for MSCC

 Sorensen 1994

Sorensen Trial (1994) Steroids + RT Steroids + RT 3-month 6-month
 RT Alone ambulation ambulation

 81%* 59%*

 RT Alone 63% 33%

 Steroids: High dose 96mg dexamethasone IV bolus followed
 by 96mg PO dexamethasone for 3 days and a 10-day taper
Optimal Steroid Dose? Typical regimen:

 Vecht 1989 (Dutch): o Loading 10 mg IV dex

 Randomized 37 patients to 100 vs. 10 mg IV dexamethasone loading those followed by o IV maintenance 4-6 mg q6-
 16 mg PO daily in addition to RT 8 hours

 No difference in pain reduction, ambulation, or bladder function in high versus o Change to oral steroids after
 conventional dose group 24-48 hours

 Heimdal 1992 (Norwegian): o Following RT, gradually taper
 (completely off or lowest
 Retrospective study of 56 patients treated with RT 30 Gy/10 Fx and either 96 mg IV QD dose that maintains stability)
 or 4 mg IV QID of dexamethasone tapered over 15 days
 o Ex. Halving the total dose q4
 Severe side effects with high dose steroids � of 28 patients, 2 patients had severe GI days
 bleed (one requiring transfusion, one resulting in death) and 2 patients had GI
 perforation Don't forget GI prophylaxis

 Other less severe side effects including 1 case of pneumonia, 2 cases of wound
 infection following orchiectomy, and 1 patient experiencing persistent hyperglycemia

 Suggests lower dose <16 mg/day is adequate

 � PPI or H2 blocker
Definitive Management: RT vs. Surgery

 Radiation Surgery

+ Well-tolerated Tissue diagnosis

Minimal morbidity/mortality Allows for immediate cord decompression

Especially effective for radiosensitive tumors Stabilizes spine/corrects bony deformity

Can repeat if needed

-- Does not stabilize spine Surgical morbidity/mortality --> risks for
 Can take several days to have effect elderly, debilitated, systemically ill
Types of Spinal Surgery -
Decompression

Spinal decompression:

 Tumor is removed & immediate circumferential decompression
 achieved, usually through anterior approach

 Reconstruction of the spine intraoperatively if needed for
 immediate stabilization
Types of Spinal Surgery -
Laminectomy

Laminectomy

 Removal of posterior arch of vertebral body
 May be necessary if anterior extension of posterior tumor
 May destabilize spine
Types of Spinal Surgery -
Corpectomy

Corpectomy

 Removal of vertebral body via thoracotomy or retroperitoneal
 approach

 May delay RT for 6 weeks to allow for fusion
 Candidates: good life expectancy and performance status
Surgery + RT vs. RT alone

Young et al. Journal of Neurosurgery, 1980

 29 patients with MSCC randomized

 Decompressive laminectomy + RT 30 Gy/10 fx
 RT alone (4Gy/day x3 days, then 1.8 Gy/day x7 days
 All patients received steroids (dex 12 mg then 4mg q6h

 through end of RT)

 No significant difference between arms with regard
 to pain relief, improved ambulation, or improved
 sphincter function

 Several other studies in this time period with similar
 results --> RT alone was modality of choice
Benefit to Direct Decompressive SGY+ RT

Patchell et al., Lancet 2005

101 MSCC Surgery + RT 30 Gy/10 Fx
 w/in 14 days

101 patients 18+ with MSCC (paraplegic <48 hours) and expected survival of at least 3 months

All patients received dexamethasone 100 mg immediately then 24 mg q6h until treatment

Surgery = immediate surgical decompression tailored to patient

 Cervical tumors � anterior approach
 Thoracic or lumbar approach � transversectomy or anterior approach
 Laterally located tumors � lateral approach
 Posterior tumors -- laminectomy

Conclusions from Patchell Study

Direct decompressive surgery + RT allows higher rate of ambulation for longer period of time

 Improved rates and duration of incontinence

Palliative.SVC Syndrome:
SVC Syndrome

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Outline

Pathophysiology & Presentation
Workup
Treatment
Pathophysiology of
SVC Syndrome

Obstruction of blood flow through SVC by
extrinsic compression or intrinsic obstruction
leading to proximal venous congestion

~15,000 cases per year

 10% of SCLC and 1.7% NSCLC at diagnosis

Anatomy

 SVC carries 1/3 of total venous return, draining
 head, arms, and upper torso

 Rt/Lt Brachiocephalic vein --> SVC
 Begins @ sternal angle --> Rt atrium
 Collaterals: azygos, mammary, vertebral, lateral

 thoracic, paraspinous, esophageal veins
Presentation of SVC
Syndrome
Common Causes of SVC Syndrome

Malignancy Commonly associated cancers

 Used to be ~90% NSCLC (50%)
 Now ~60% due to implantable IV devices SCLC (25%) but more likely d/t rapid growth in

 (pacemaker leads, catheters) central airways
 Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (12%)
Catheter-induced thrombosis
 Most common cause in <50 y/o
Chronic mediastinitis DLBCL, lymphoblastic lymphoma, primary mediastinal BCL

Retrosternal goiter Mets (9%)
 Germ cell tumors (3%)
CHF Thymoma (2%)
 Mesothelioma
Aortic aneurysm
Workup of SVC Syndrome

H&P � duration & severity of symptoms, assess respiratory status, assess neurological status

 Prior cancer diagnosis? Prior intravascular procedures?
 Assess facial changes (look at Driver's license, talk to family, etc.)

Imaging

 CXR � widened mediastinum
 CT chest with contrast � brightly lit up collaterals

Labs � BMP, CBC, if unknown cancer LDH, AFP, -hCG

Tissue diagnosis of external compression � bronch/EBUS, mediastinoscopy, thoracentesis, VATS
Proposed Classification System of SVC
Syndrome � Yu et al., 2008
Treatment of SVC Syndrome

Supportive measures Emergent Treatment?
 � SVC syndrome is not always an
 Head of bed elevation decrease venous pressure
 Supplemental O2 emergency!
 Dexamethasone/diuretics if cerebral edema � Symptomatic obstruction

Endovascular Stent usually is a prolonged process
 over weeks (~1 month)
 1st line if patient is compromised --> rapid symptom relief � Collateral vessel formation is a
 sign of slowly growing, chronic
Anticoagulation: If thrombosis present process
 � Assess for respiratory,
Before choosing anti-cancer treatment, need a pathologic diagnosis neurologic and/or
 hemodynamic stability!
 RT and steroids can obscure histologic diagnosis
 Chemotherapy (Lymphoma, SCLC, GCT)
 Radiation Therapy (SCLC, NSCLC)
 Surgery (Thymoma)

Palliative vs Curative: Dose depends on intent and disease

 Median survival = 6 months
SVC Syndrome & Effectiveness of RT

Meta-Analysis Rowell & Gleeson 2002, Clin Oncol Retrospective Study Washington University IJROBP, 1987
� 2 randomized, 44 non-randomized studies � 125 patients with SVC syndrome treated with RT
� Relief of SVC syndrome � 80% good to excellent symptomatic relief
 � 2-week relief rates
 � SCLC: 77% chemo vs. 78% RT
 � NSCLC: 59% chemo vs. 63% RT � Initial high dose (3-4 Gy/Fx x3 Fx): 70%
� Did not appear related to RT fractionation schedule � Low dose (2 Gy/Fx): 56%
 or chemotherapy regimen
 � P = 0.09

 Improvements can be seen within 72 hours, especially for radiosensitive histologies
 Can consider 3-4 Gy/Fx for 2-3 days 1.8-2 Gy/Fx to complete definitive RT

 Or continue with larger fractions if palliation is the only goal

 60-70% have good-excellent response

 20% patients have complete disappearance of dyspnea, edema, distention of veins at 2 weeks
Conclusion

SVC syndrome is most often caused by NSCLC > SCLC

Usually develops over a period of weeks

Initial management

 Supportive measures � elevate head of bed, oxygen, steroids
 If truly an emergency (vitals compromised) � endovascular stent

Cancer therapies

 Ensure patient has adequate tissue diagnosis
 Chemotherapy if highly sensitive (e.g., lymphoma, SCLC, GCT)
 If treating with radiation, consider treatment goal

 If curative can still consider higher dose per fraction (3-4 Gy) initially
Thank you!

Background
Pathophysiology

Small round blue cell tumor

Originates from neural crest cells of sympathetic nervous
system that migrate to form adrenal medulla and spinal
sympathetic ganglia

Neuroblastic tumors occur on a spectrum & range in
prognosis:

 Benign ganglioneuroma (well-differentiated, favorable
 prognosis)

 Ganglioneuroblastoma (moderately differentiated,
 unfavorable prognosis)

 Neuroblastoma (poorly differentiated, favorable to poor
 prognosis) accounts for 97%
Genetics

High risk feature: Amplification of MYCN proto-oncogene on chromosome 2 (detect via FISH)

 Transcription factor involved in embryonal development

Results in N-myc overexpression � found in 20% to 25% tumors:

 0-10% early stage
 40-50% advanced stage

Imaging
 Abdominal ultrasound is often the first imaging performed (widely available, noninvasive)

Pathology
 Bilateral bone marrow biopsy (FNA not adequate)
 Bilateral bone marrow biopsy + elevated urinary HVA/MVA considered sufficient for diagnosis without biopsy

123I-MIBG Scintigraphy vs.

Iodine-123 metaiodobenzylguanidine (MIBG) = analog of
norepinephrine

 Taken up by norepinephrine transporter & accumulates in cytoplasm
 and mitochondria

Majority of neuroblastoma cells express this norepinephrine
transporter

 >90% of neuroblastomas are MIBG avid

Maximal tumoral uptake occurs 24-96 hours after
radiopharmaceutical administration cleared renally

 For normal kidneys, ~50% cleared in first 24h and ~90% cleared in 96
 hrs

 Normal distrubtion of MIBG uptake � salivary glands, liver, spleen,
 excretion in urinary bladder

Obtain prior to resection of tumor if possible

 Not required in patients <6 months with localized adrenal tumor
 3.1 cm if solid, or 5 cm if >25% cystic component

Treatment Paradigm
Summary of Low Risk (OS >95%)
Management
 � L1: surgical resection surveillance
 � Infants <6 mo with <5 cm isolated adrenal masses may be
 observed

 � MS: observation if asymptomatic & favorable biology

 Intermediate Risk (OS 95%)

 � Chemotherapy (2-8 cycles depending on risk factors) 
 consideration of surgery if <PR

 High Risk (OS 30-50%)

 � Induction: Multiagent chemotherapy
 � Resection of primary tumor/locoregional disease
 � Consolidation: high dose chemoIO stem cell rescue RT
 � Post consolidation: anti-GD2 antibody therapy & isotretinoin
Systemic Therapy for High-Risk Disease

Induction chemotherapy:

 No universal standard, multi-agent CHT dependent on protocol
 Acceptable protocols to refer to for regimen: ANBL 12P1, ANBL 1531, ANBL 0532
 Common agents: carboplatin, vincristine, vindesine, ifosfamide, dacarbazine, topotecan, melphalan

 CAPE: Cyclophosphamide, Adriamycin, cisPlatin, Etoposide

 Intensive doses of combination CHT with short intervals between courses for high-risk patients
 Can treat with ALK inhibitors for ALK mutant patients

Consolidation chemoIO:

 Dinutuximab (anti-GD2 antibody) � targets disialoganglioside GD2 on surface of neuroblastoma cells
 Acute toxicity � capillary leak syndrome & pain

Myeloablative CHT with autologous SCT improves survival over CHT alone

 Tandem transplant improves EFS over single transplant

Maintenance therapy

 Adjuvant isotretinoin (13-cis-retinoic acid) and dinituximab (anti-GD-2 monoclonal antibody)
Radiotherapy

RT to primary tumor and persistent metastatic sites in high-risk patients

 Only indicated in low or intermediate risk patients if causing urgent life/organ threatening concerns (liver mets
 with respiratory compromise, or cord compression)

Initiate RT 28-42 days post-transplant

RT dose: 21.6 Gy/12 Fx daily

 ANBL 0532: 21.6 Gy to pre-op GTV, then 14.4 Gy boost to residual no improvement in local control

Treatment volume

Post-chemo GTV: tumor prior to attempted surgical resection (cropped out of normal tissues)

 Add 1 cm margin for CTV, 0.3-0.5 cm margin for PTV

Emergent RT:

 Hepatomegaly causing respiratory distress whole liver RT 4.5 Gy/3 Fx
 Orbit or optic pathway disease leading to vision loss
 For cord compression � favor CHT followed by surgery, can use 21.6 Gy (or 9 Gy/5 Fx if <3 years)

Pediatric Renal Tumors Features

Favorable Histology (FH) Typical features of triphasic histology (blastemal, epithelial, stromal elements) without
Wilms anaplastic or sarcomatous components
Unfavorable Histology
(UH) Wilms Anaplasia = enlargement & hyperchromatism of nuclei, increased mitotic figures
 - Focal anaplasia (FA): sharply localized in primary tumor
Rhabdoid tumor of kidney - Diffuse anaplasia (DA): nonlocalized or localized with significant nuclear unrest in
(RTK)
Clear cell sarcoma of remainder of tumor of found outside tumor capsule, in metastases, or on random
kidney (CCSK) biopsy of tumor

Renal cell carcinoma Eosinophilic cytoplasm & hyaline globular inclusions (+vimentin & cytokeratin); typically
 diagnosed at <2 years and a/w primary CNS neoplasms (i.e., ATRT) & INI1 mutations

 Tumor cells with abundant intracytoplasmic vesicles; "chicken-wire" pattern with
 undifferentiated cells separated by fibrovascular septa; 4% of childhood renal tumors,
 more likely to metastasize to bone (5% p/w mets; 40-60% bone mets)

 Not included in classic studies; treatment with surgery alone, no clear role for adjuvant RT

Staging
COG Group Staging of WT

Stage Description

I Tumor completely resected with negative margins & limited to kidney, no renal sinus vessels involved

 Renal capsule intact - no biopsy or tumor rupture prior to removal

II Tumor completely resected with negative margins, but there is extension beyond the kidney (through capsule, or in vessels)

 - Penetration of tumor capsule or extensive invasion of soft tissue of renal sinus

 - Blood vessels within nephrectomy specimen outside of renal parenchyma, including those of renal sinus, contain tumor

III Residual tumor following surgery, confined to abdomen � "SLURPPIB"

 - STR/margin+

 - LN (abdominal)

 - Unresectable

 - Rupture/spillage

 - Piecemeal resection (including thrombus not removed en bloc)

 - Preoperative CHT required (i.e., unresectable)

 - Implant (peritoneal involvement, including peritoneal penetration)

 - Biopsy

IV Distant metastases or LN metastases outside of abdomen or pelvis

V Bilateral renal involvement present at diagnosis

 61% required complete nephrectomy of at least 1 kidney
 Only 39% had bilateral NSS (Aim 2 )
 After NAC, 84% underwent definitive surgery of at least one kidney @week 12 (Aim 3 )

RIA MULHERKAR KRAFT, MD
Ependymoma Intracranial ependymomas

 Uncommon tumor Peak incidence in early childhood
 Slight male predominance
 6% of CNS tumors in children Median age of diagnosis is 3-5 years
 2% of CNS tumors in adults Less common in adults, if so age <40 years

 Originates from glial stem cells Spinal ependymomas
 (ependymal cells lining the
 ventricles) Rare in children, more common in adults
 Median age 30-40 years
 Association with NF2-related schwannomas

 Classification
Background �
Presentation & Workup
Anatomy

Ependymoma originates from glial stem cells

 Most commonly located in 4th ventricle (children 
 infratentorial ependymomas)

Flow of CSF

 Lateral ventricle 3rd ventricle via foramen of Monro
 3rd ventricle 4th ventricle via cerebral aqueduct of

 Sylvius
 4th ventricle subarachnoid space via foramina of

 Magendie (medially) & Luschka (laterally)
Pathophysiology

Glial cell tumor arising from ependymal cells that line the ventricular system

 Supratentorial ependymoma thought to arise from radial glial cells in subventricular zone
 Posterior fossa cell of origin thought to be ependymal central neuroepithelial cells during

 embryogenesis or malignant degeneration of mature ependymal cells

Most common locations

 Posterior fossa (children)

 Usually in contact with 4th ventricle, with frequent extension into subarachnoid space
 2/3 posterior fossa, 1/3 supratentorial

 Intramedullary spinal cord (adults)

 Usually in cervical or thoracic spine

Unlikely to be metastatic at presentation
Presentation

Children � symptoms of increased ICP

 Headache
 Nausea
 Ataxia
 Vertigo
 Papilledema

Adults � depends on tumor location

 Back pain
Workup

H&P � evaluate for symptoms of elevated ICP
Imaging � MRI of brain & spine with and without contrast
Lumbar puncture for CSF cytology

 If ICP elevated, wait 10-14 days postop to do LP to avoid risk of herniation

Maximal safe resection!!

Postoperative MRI to assess extent of resection
LP if not done preoperatively
Management of Elevated ICP

ENDOSCOPIC THIRD VENTRICULOSTOMY (ETV) VENTRICULOPERITONEAL SHUNT (VP SHUNT)

Endoscope is passed into third ventricle, and fenestrations Catheter implanted to drain excess fluid from the brain
are created in the floor of third ventricle, allowing CSF to into the peritoneum.
drain directly to prepontine subarachnoid space

ETV is generally preferred over VP shunt due to lower rates of infection and complications.
VP shunt may be preferred in very young children due to lower rate of failure (Rosyidi et al., 2024)

Merchant et al., JCO 2019

Prospective study observing patient outcomes after resection for intracranial ependymoma

 Most important prognostic factor is extent of surgical resection
 In this study, immediate postop RT to children <3 years outcomes similar to older children

 Don't need to delay RT until age 3 years

Background
Anatomy

Most commonly presents in posterior fossa

 ~75% in midline vermis

Hemispheric location associated with older
age & desmoplastic histology

Posterior Fossa Boundaries
Ant Clivus & posterior clinoid
Post Calvarium
Sup Tentorium cerebellum
Inf Occipital bone
Lat Temporal, occipital, parietal bones
Pathophysiology

Small, round blue cell tumor

 Used to be classified as PNET
 2016 WHO update changed classification of embryonal tumors
 Now known as embryonal tumors with multilayered rosettes

Classic histologic finding = Homer-Wright Rosettes

 Tumor cells concentrically arranged around a lumen containing
 neuropil (unmyelinated axons, dendrites and glial cell processes)

Thought to originate from granule cell precursors in external
germinal layer (EGL) of the developing cerebellum

Typically occurs in midline posterior fossa

 75% occur in midline vermis
 Can compress 4th ventricle --> hydrocephalus p/w ICP

Highly malignant tumor with predisposition to spread via
subarachnoid system

 "Drop mets" along the spinal cord
Symptoms of increased ICP! Other symptoms:

Lateral ventricle Cerebellar symptoms
Third ventricle Truncal ataxia
Fourth ventricle (destruction of vermis)
 Dysmetria
 Laterally: Foramen of Luschka Dysdiadochokinesia
 Medially: Foramen of Magendie Spasticity

Subarachnoid spaces Other possible symptoms
 Parinaud syndrome
Medulloblastoma tends to obstruct CSF efflux raised ICP
Symptoms include headaches, morning emesis, papilledema, diplopia (CN VI palsy) Upward gaze palsy
Infants may present with bulging anterior fontanelle, splitting of cranial structures
 "Setting sun sign"

 Conjugate down gaze
Workup of posterior fossa mass

If suspect brain tumor based on imaging Cytology
upfront maximal safe resection
 Lumbar puncture
 Not biopsy!
 Obtain preop, but usually not feasible if increased ICP

Principles of Surgery

MAXIMAL SAFE RESECTION (aim for GTR/NTR)

 GTR (gross total resection) is the goal
 NTR (near total resection) if 1.5 cm2 residual diameter, or >90% resection
 STR (subtotal resection) if 51-90% resection
 Partial resection if 11-50% removal
 Biopsy <10% removal
 GTR/NTR typically equivocal outcomes with best prognosis compared to STR, etc.

Posterior Fossa Syndrome

 Complication of surgery in up to 25%
 "Cerebellar Mutism" � swallowing dysfunction, truncal ataxia, mutism, emotional liability (same)
 Usually occurs 12-24 hours postoperatively & self-resolves over weeks to months
 Should not delay adjuvant treatment!

Patterns of Failure 
ACNS 0331 � Conclusions

Michalski et al., JCO 2021

 Can we reduce volume of boost in average-risk medulloblastoma?
 � Yes � no difference in EFS or OS with IFRT vs. PFRT

 Can we reduce CSI dose in average-risk medulloblastoma in patients ages 3-7?
 � No � significantly worse EFS & OS (~7-10%) with 18 Gy CSI vs. 23.4 Gy CSI

 What are the differences in toxicities?
 � No difference in IQ between IFRT & PFRT
 � Greater decline in IQ for ages 3-7 with SDCSI vs. LDCSI
 � Modern RT techniques may be associated with less global cognitive morbidity for older children 8+

All patients received maintenance
vincristine/cisplatin/cyclophosphamide q28 days x6 cycles

Background �
Presentation & Workup
Epidemiology

Primary CNS tumors constitute ~15-20% of all pediatric malignancies

 2nd most common childhood malignancy

 After hematologic malignancies

 THE most common pediatric solid organ tumor
 Leading cause of cancer death in children 0-14 years old

Incidence: approximately 6.3 per 100,000 cases from 2015-2019 in the US

 58% cases malignant, 42% non-malignant

Pediatric tumors more likely to be infratentorial (~60-70%)

 Also more likely to be low-grade
Presentation

 Cerebellar tumors:
 � Midline tumors--truncal or gait ataxia (broad-based gait, difficulty with heel-to-toe walking), head titubation (head bobbing) & nystagmus
 � Lateral tumors--dysmetria on finger to nose testing, intention tremor, difficulty with heel to shin test
Tumor
Predisposition
Syndromes

Pehlivan et al., Pediatrics in Review 2022
Mnemonics
General Workup

Full history and physical

 Full neuro exam--mental status, cranial nerves, motor function, sensation, reflexes, gait & coordination
 Skin examination--dermatologic manifestation of tumor predisposition syndromes
 Fundoscopic examination--evaluate for papilledema (increased ICP)
 Unstable vital signs, altered mental status, concern for increased ICP (HA/N/V, papilledema) ER

Imaging

 CT head without contrast initially (acute hydrocephalus, impending herniation, acute hemorrhage)
 MRI with and without contrast is gold standard for visualizing brain tumors

 Brain preop & postop within 48-72 hours (before maturation of blood products)
 Spine preop or postop after 10-14 days (avoid confounding artifactual change)

Labs

 CBC, CMP
 AFP, -hCG, LDH
 Lumbar puncture with CSF cytology (10-14 days postop to decrease risk of herniation)

From there, neurosurgery & neuro-oncology consult

 Patients will require multidisciplinary care

 Neurology, neuro-radiology, radiation oncology, genetics, endocrinology, ophthalmology, audiology, neuropsychology (baseline IQ), physical medicine and
 rehabilitation, palliative care, social work, etc.

CSI Technique

Simulation

 Supine, shoulders down, neck extended with attempt to minimize curvature of spine
 5-point mask to secure head & shoulders
 Large vac-loc for body (check for straightness) +/- knee sponge to reduce lordosis

Contours

 Whole brain (ensure covering cribriform plate/crista galli)
 Entire thecal sac as identified by MRI (usually bottom of S1-S2)
 When contouring spine, include extensions along nerve roots laterally

Fields

 2-3 isocenters (one for each field)
 Two lateral beams for whole brain field
 1 or more PA fields to cover spine (feather junction to avoid hot spots)
Divergence:

Beam's diameter increases
with distance from the source
Hot spots are created Rotate patient to match brain
where fields overlap 1 & spine field to minimize
(& cold spots behind)
 their divergence into one
 another

 Minimize hot spots in the
 2 spine by "feathering the

 junction" this also
 spreads out the cold spot
Fractions 1-5 Fractions 6-10 Fractions 11-15 ...etc.
CSI Fields

Typically need ~3 total fields

Plan upper spinal field first � single PA field

 Upper border as low as possible

 E.g., C4-C5/C5-C6 to L1-L2, but span depends on child's height

 Avoid exit through oral cavity (neck extended)

Whole brain field � 2 opposed laterals

 Ensure covering cribriform plate/crista galli & BOS
 Avoid entering through shoulders
 Can have 0.5 cm gap between brain and spine fields

Lower spinal field(s) � additional PA field(s)

 Match point of divergence ANTERIOR to spinal cord

Each field has a divergence = arctan( 12 = arctan( 21

way that avoids overlap into the
neighboring field

Achieve this by doing 2 things:

 Whole brain field: Rotate the collimator for
 opposed laterals to match upward
 divergence of the PA spine field

 About 12o

 Spine field: Kick the couch TOWARD the
 head of the gantry to match downward
 divergence of the opposed laterals

 About 8o
Matching Multiple Spine Fields

Match spine field ANTERIOR to the spinal cord to
avoid hot spot in the cord
Therapists can place marker on skin to check gap
Calculate skin separation using similar triangles

Feather the junction = 1 � ()
 2
 There is still cold spot in the spine posterior to match
 point of the two fields

 Match line should be moved superiorly by 0.5-1 cm
 periodically to spread out the cold spot "feathering
 the junction"

 Typically, every 5 fractions, or every 9 Gy once a week
Protons vs. Photons for CSI

Kahalley et al., JCO 2020

Retrospective study comparing intelligence
tests scores of 79 pediatric patients treated
between 2007-2018 with CSI on the same
medulloblastoma protocols

 Treatment differed only in modality (protons
 vs. photons)

Photon group exhibited significant decline
in global IQ, working memory, & processing
speed

Proton group exhibited stable scores in all
domains with exception of processing speed

Surgery (if performed) is done at week 13

 Ewing sarcoma is chemosensitive induction chemo gives tumor time to

 Upfront SGY risks tumor spread to surrounding tissues
 Consider surgery if lesion can be resected with NEGATIVE margins and

 Prefer surgery for large, bulky tumors that can be resected

 "Dispensable bones" have good postop outcomes without requiring
 reconstruction � rib, clavicle, proximal fibula, distal scapula, metatarsals,
 metacarpals, and small iliac wing or pubic bone lesions

 Large lesions may require allograft or endoprosthetic reconstructions

Debulking surgery (intralesional or R2) never indicated

 Delays systemic therapy
 RT is still required to full dose, with larger, hypoxic target

COG AEWS0031: Interval Compressed CHT

Womer et al., JCO 2012; Cash et al., JCO 2021

2001-2005: 568 patients with extradural Ewing sarcoma randomized

 Standard CHT: VDC/IE q3 weeks (21 days) x14 cycles
 Intensified CHT: VDC/IE q2 weeks (14 days) x14 cycles

Primary treatment (SGY and/or RT) at week 13

 S/p 4 cycles CHT in standard arm or 6 cycles in intensified arm

No significant differences in toxicities between regimens

Significantly improved EFS in intensified arm (not OS)

 5-year EFS 65% standard vs. 73% intensified (p=0.048)
 Interval-compressed CHT had no impact on LR, but rather related to improved distant relapse
 5-year OS 77% standard vs. 83% intensified (p=0.056)

Long-term outcomes showed improved EFS & OS!

 10-year EFS 61% standard vs. 70% intensified (p=0.03)
 10-year OS 69% standard vs. 76% intensified (p=0.04)

 compressed CHT, i.e., alternating VAC/IE q2 weeks

Best reported 5-year EFS & OS outcomes to date!

Treatment details

D9803 (IRS-V) (Arndt et al., JCO 2009) � negative study for VAC vs. VAC/VTC

 Intermediate risk RMS � embryonal stage II-III group III, all non-metastatic alveolar or undifferentiated
 Randomized to 42-week regimen of VAC vs. alternating VAC/VTC (VC same as VAC but T = topotecan)

 No significant difference in 4-year FFS (73% VAC vs. 68% VTC, p=0.3) no significant improvement
 Toxicities similar between arms

ARST 0531 (IRS-VI) (Casey et al., Cancer 2020) � alternating VAC vs. VAC/VI

 Intermediate risk RMS � embryonal stage II-III group III, or nonmetastatic alveolar
 Randomized to 42-week regimen of VAC vs. VAC/VI (alternating VAC with vincristine irinotecan, low dose CPM in this arm)
 RT started at week 4 (to see if there was a benefit to earlier initiation)
 No difference in 4-year EFS or OS based on CHT regimen (4-year EFS ~60%, 4-year OS ~73%)

 Higher local failure on ARST 0531 compared to D9803! 5-year LF 28% on 0531vs. 19% on D9803

 Why? Unclear... possibly due to confounders, allowed omission of RT in patients 24 months (although subset analysis excluding these patients still had worse LC...)

Even though ARST 0531 has worse LF compared to D9803, alternating VAC/VI adapted as standard due to

CSI Technique

Simulation

 Supine, shoulders down, neck extended with attempt to minimize curvature of spine

Contours

 Whole brain (ensure covering cribriform plate/crista galli)

 When contouring spine, include extensions along nerve roots laterally

Fields

 2-3 isocenters (one for each field)
 Two lateral beams for whole brain field
 1 or more PA fields to cover spine (feather junction to avoid hot spots)

Beam's diameter increases
with distance from the source

where fields overlap 1 & spine field to minimize
(& cold spots behind)
 their divergence into one
 another

 2 spine by "feathering the

Typically need ~3 total fields

Plan upper spinal field first � single PA field

 Upper border as low as possible

 E.g., C4-C5/C5-C6 to L1-L2, but span depends on child's height

 Avoid exit through oral cavity (neck extended)

Whole brain field � 2 opposed laterals

 Ensure covering cribriform plate/crista galli & BOS
 Avoid entering through shoulders
 Can have 0.5 cm gap between brain and spine fields

Lower spinal field(s) � additional PA field(s)

way that avoids overlap into the
neighboring field

Achieve this by doing 2 things:

 About 12o

 About 8o
Matching Multiple Spine Fields

avoid hot spot in the cord

 2

Protons vs. Photons for CSI

Kahalley et al., JCO 2020

Retrospective study comparing intelligence
tests scores of 79 pediatric patients treated
between 2007-2018 with CSI on the same
medulloblastoma protocols

in global IQ, working memory, & processing
speed

Proton group exhibited stable scores in all

Fundamentals

Electrons (beta particles) are charged

Resting mass energy = 0.511 MeV

 Compare to photons, which are massless, and

Used to treat superficial tumors

Electrons vs. Photons PDD

Photons:

 Surface dose is LOWER at higher energies
 Dmax is DEEPER at higher energies

Electrons:

 Surface dose is HIGHER at higher energies
 Dmax is DEEPER at higher energies

 Higher energy electron beams range straggling

 Dose falls off less steeply (broader range of high dose)

LET = linear energy transfer

Physics.Properties of Proton Beams:
Properties of Proton
Beams

RIA MULHERKAR KRAFT, MD
Proton Basics

Proton mass is ~2000x that of electron

 Electron: 511 keV (0.511 MeV)
 Proton: 938 MeV

Generated in cyclotrons or synchrotrons

 Harvested by a deflector which generates an
 electrostatic field to redirect protons out of
 centripetal motion

 Cyclotrons � accelerate particles in a spiral path
 Synchotrons � accelerate particles in a closed loop

Very different depth dose curve compared to
photons

 "Bragg peak"
Proton RBE

Proton RBE = 1.1
Multiply photon dose x1.1 to account for proton RBE

 Can report dose as cobalt Gray equivalent (CGE) or Gray equivalent NOT SI units
 DRBE is what you should report per ICRU

RBE for Carbon is ~3 (or higher)
Bragg Peak

As proton traverses tissue, it loses energy
At some point, it loses all its energies, and then it stops
The point where it stops is also the point where it deposits most of its energy

Higher proton energy deeper penetration
Pristine vs. Spread Out Bragg Peak

A monoenergetic proton beam will have a "pristine" Bragg peak
We can "spread out" the Bragg peak by adding proton beams of many different energies (SOBP)
Changing Energy of the Proton Beam

 Passive scattering

 Range shifter: determines highest E of beam; can allow treatment of more superficial tumor if needed
 Range modulators (e.g., range modulator wheel, ridge filter): introduce devices of various thickness to alter

 energy of the proton beam for SOBP
 Scattering foils placed directly within the line of the field to widen the beam (dual scatterers for larger fields)
 Compensators: make shape conformal to the target
 Downside = leads to higher surface dose
Changing Energy of the Proton Beam

Active scanning/Spot scanning

 "Pencil beam" technique: beam as thin as a pencil, and allows proton beam to be precisely directed to
 the spot where it can release its maximum energy (painting the target)

 Improved proximal dose conformity
 Can do ~1000 spots in 60 seconds
 Tumor motion is more of a concern
Protons are Sensitive, Many
Uncertainties

Tissue inhomogeneity

 E.g., having bone in the path can change the distal edge of the
 proton beam (example: draws distal edge in by 2 cm in figure b vs.
 effect on photon shown in figure a)

 Applies to metal artifacts after surgery, chemo ports, etc.

More sensitive to patient anatomical changes (e.g., weight loss),
motion during treatment, air/soft tissue interfaces

Uncertainty in CT Hounsfield units/density calculation, stopping
power, range uncertainty

Add distal margin (more margin at distal end of the beam)

 Distal margin = 0.035 x CTVdistal + 3 mm
 Exact formula may vary between centers
Major Protons generated in cyclotrons/synchrotrons
Takeaways
 Proton RBE = 1.1

 Multiply photon dose x1.1 to account for proton RBE
 Dose can be reported as cobalt gray equivalents (CGE)
 Report to ICRU as DRBE

 Protons have a Bragg peak

 Deposit most of their energy at the depth at which they stop
 Monoenergetic beams have a pristine Bragg peak
 Polyenergetic beams have a spread-out Bragg peak (SOBP)

 Techniques for changing proton energy to treat full tumor

 Passive scattering
 Active scattering/spot scanning/pencil beam technique

 Protons are very sensitive with many uncertainties

 Tissue inhomogeneities, tumor motion, anatomic change
 Uncertainties in CT #, stopping power, range uncertainty

Castrate-Sensitive vs. Castrate-Resistant

Castrate-Sensitive Prostate Cancer

 Responding to androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)

Castrate-Resistant Prostate Cancer (CRPC)

 State of clinical or biochemical progression in an environment with very low serum testosterone
 Low testosterone (castrate environment) = serum testosterone concentration <50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/dL

 Diagnosis of castrate-resistant prostate cancer is based on rising PSA in the setting of adequately
 suppressed testosterone (<50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/dL)
mCSPC: Low-Volume vs. High-Volume

 Low-volume disease High-volume disease

3 bone metastases total Presence of visceral metastases
Bone mets only involving vertebral bodies or pelvis 4 bone lesions, with at least one outside vertebral
Any number of pelvic nodal metastases* bodies or pelvis

*pelvic nodal metastases not included in CHAARTED definition, but was considered low-volume met in revised version of STAMPEDE
Summary of Management of Metastatic
Prostate Cancer

mCSPC

 Low-volume disease

 Doublet therapy: ADT + Abiraterone, Apalutamide, or Enzalutamide
 Prostate-directed RT (OS benefit)
 Metastasis-directed therapy (SBRT or SGY) for up to 5 metastases (PFS benefit)

 High-volume disease

 Triplet therapy: ADT + Docetaxel + Abiraterone or Daralutamide
 PEACE-1 abstract data only: prostate-directed RT may prolong time to GU events and improve castration resistance -free survival

mCRPC

 Many options for adenocarcinoma depending on extent of prior therapy

 No prior tx � abiraterone, docetaxel, apalutamide
 Olaparib for BRCA mutant, Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR
 Cabazitaxel or docetaxel re-challenge if progresses on docetaxel

 Radiopharmaceuticals for prostate adencoarcinoma

 Ra-223 (Xofigo) for bone only metastases
 Lu-177-PSMA-617 (Pluvicto)

 If neuroendocrine differentiation cis/etoposide, carbo/etoposide, etc.
RT to prostate improves OS in
patients with low metastatic burden

OV ERALL SURVIVAL BENEFIT IN PAT IEN TS WITH LOW BURDE N
METASTATIC DISEASE ( 3 BON E METS, OR VERT & PELVIS ONLY)
STAMPEDE: RT to Primary Tumor in M1 Dz

Parker et al., PloS Medicine 2022

 2013-16: phase 3 RCT of 2061 men w/ de novo metastatic prostate cancer

 Stratified per definitions used in CHAARTED Trial using CT or MRI (not PSMA PET!)

 High metastatic burden (58%): 4 bone metastases with 1 outside vert bodies or pelvis, or visceral metastases, or both
 Low metastatic burden (42%): all others (3 bone mets, or bone mets only involving vert bodies/pelvis)

 Randomization

 Standard: lifelong ADT (18% received docetaxel once approved, 75 mg/m2 q3 wks x6 cycles, +/- prednisolone 10 mg daily)
 Experimental: EBRT either 55 Gy/20 Fx daily over 4 weeks (53%), or 36 Gy/6 Fx weekly over 6 weeks (47%) + systemic

 therapy as above

 5-year results: improved OS in patients with low metastatic burden

 5-year OS 53% without RT vs. 65% with RT (p = 0.00004)
 For high burden, 5-year OS 35% without RT vs. 30% with RT (p=0.164)
 FFS improved across all patients (3-year 32% vs. 23%, p<0.05)

 No significant differences in QoL between groups (73% vs. 72% average global QoL score at 2 year)

 Overall G3+ toxicity over 5 years: 44% SOC vs. 45% SOC + RT
 2% G3+ urinary toxicity at 1 year, 3% G3+ bowel toxicity at 1 year, no significant difference between arms
STAMPEDE: RT to Primary Tumor in M1 Dz

Parker et al., PloS Medicine 2022

All patients Low metastatic burden High metastatic burden

 OS 61 months 85 months OS 41 months 39 months
 p=0.0004 p=0.164

� STAMPEDE: improved overall survival with prostate RT in patients with metastatic prostate cancer
 with low-burden metastatic disease

� Doses used: 55 Gy/20 Fx daily & 36 Gy/6 Fx weekly
STOPCAP Meta-Analysis

Burdett et al., Eur Urol 2019

Prospective meta-analysis of STAMPEDE & HORRAD trials which both asked similar questions

 STAMPEDE: M1 prostate cancer treated with ADT +/- RT (55 Gy/20 Fx or 36 Gy/6 Fx)

 Improved OS in low-burden metastatic disease

 HORRAD: M1 prostate cancer treated with ADT +/- RT (70 Gy/35 Fx or 57.76 Gy/19 Fx)

 No significant OS or FFS benefit (note included PSA >20 and bone only mets)
 Low metastatic burden defined in HORRAD trial as <5 bone lesions and PSA 142

Prostate-directed RT improved OS in patients with <5 bone lesions

 3-year OS 70% 77% with addition of RT (SS)
 In unselected men, no OS benefit, but there was ~10% FFS and bPFS benefit with size effect that varied

 based on metastatic burden

� STOPCAP meta-analysis shows 3-year OS benefit of 7% in patients with <5 bone metastases
PEACE-1 Trial: Abstract

ASCO 2023 Annual Meeting

2013-2018: 1172 men with de novo mCSPC (1 distant met on bone scan or CT scan)were randomized 1:1:1:1 (2x2
factorial design)

 SOC (ADT +/- Docetaxel 75 mg/m2 q3 weeks x6 cycles)
 SOC + Abiraterone 1000 mg/day + prednisone 5 mg BID
 SOC + Prostate RT 74 Gy/37 Fx
 SOC + Abiraterone + Prostate RT

Addition of abiraterone to SOC improved radiographic PFS and OS (SS)

In low-volume population, SOC + Abiraterone + Prostate RT improved rPFS

 rPFS was 7.5 years with RT vs. 4.4 years without (p=0.02)
 No significant OS benefit was seen

In overall population, SOC + Abiraterone + Prostate RT improved time to serious GU events & castration-resistance-
free survival

 Time to serious GU events hard to interpret, because receiving RT to prostate was defined as a GU event
 CRFS was 2.5 years with RT vs. 1.9 years without (p=0.007)

� PEACE-1 provides a rationale for treating metastatic prostate cancer patients with prostate RT
 � rPFS benefit for low-volume metastatic disease
 � Time to GU event & castration-resistance free survival benefit for all patients
Major RT to primary tumor in setting of mCSPC is beneficial

PSA Metrics

PSA value >2.5-4 ng/mL indicates higher risk of cancer
 Normal PSA value depends on age & ethnicity
 False positives: manipulation/biopsy, BPH, prostatitis, urinary retention, trauma,
 ejaculation
 One isolated value is difficult to interpret

Alternative metrics
 PSA density: serum PSA / volume of prostate gland
 >0.15 ng/mL2 indicates higher risk of prostate cancer
 PSA velocity: change in PSA over time
 >0.75 ng/mL/year indicates higher risk of prostate cancer
 PSA doubling time:
 <12 months indicates higher risk of prostate cancer
 PSA ratio: free PSA/total PSA
 Ratio <25% indicates higher risk of prostate cancer

 (ADC) images Cancer cells (d/t high cellularity) restrict diffusion
Dynamic contrast enhancement Focal findings with restricted diffusion on high b-value and ADC
 imaging are suspicious

 More useful in post-prostatectomy setting � enhancing focus
 suggests recurrent cancer

 Genetic biomarker score based on analysis of 22 genes in prostate tumors

1999-2009: 1643 men with prostate cancer randomized

Prostate Cancer (Simplified)

Risk Group Treatment
Low risk Active surveillance or Monotherapy (SGY or RT)
Favorable intermediate risk Monotherapy
Unfavorable intermediate risk SGY or Prostate RT + Short-term ADT (4-6 months)
High risk Consider pelvic RT + Long-term ADT (18-36 months) or SGY
Very high risk Pelvic RT + Long-term ADT + Abiraterone/prednisone or SGY

Summary of Management:
Post-prostatectomy

Adjuvant prostate radiation used to be standard of care if patients had certain risk factors:

 pT3-T4 disease
 Positive surgical margin

A series of studies (RAVES, RADICALS, & GETUG-AFU 17) compared salvage vs. adjuvant RT:

 Salvage RT = postop RT after biochemical recurrence
 Salvage approach proved non-inferior in terms of bPFS & spared ~50% of patients from postop RT

Adjuvant RT only indicated in select cases of pN+ disease

Generally, in postop setting, utilize salvage RT if PSA becomes detectable and continues to rise.
Adjuvant RT reserved for select cases of pN+ disease.

Postop definition of biochemical recurrence = PSA >0.2 ng/mL or becomes detectable & continues to rise
Summary of Management:
Metastatic Prostate Cancer

Prostate-directed RT in mCSPC

 Provides OS benefit in patients with low-volume disease (STAMPEDE, STOPCAP)
 Possibly provides local control/toxicity benefit even in high-volume disease (PEACE-1 data, manuscript

 pending)

Metastasis-directed therapy

 Phase II evidence for improved PFS with SBRT to oligometastases for patients with up to 5 metastases
 with mCSPC

 Ongoing PCS-IX trial for SBRT to oligomets in mCRPC

Radiopharmaceuticals in mCRPC (adenocarcinoma)

 Ra-223 (Xofigo) provides OS benefit for bone-only mets
 Lu-177 (Pluvicto) provides OS benefit for PSMA+ bone and/or visceral mets
Major Localized prostate cancer
Takeaways
 Treatment options based on risk-stratification

Radiobiology of Prostate
Cancer
The / ratio

Dose (Gy) where linear component of cell killing equals quadratic
component of cell killing

 Linear component = cell kill due to a single hit to DNA
 Quadratic component = cell kill due to a double hit to DNA

Higher / ratio = less sensitive to dose per fraction
 Rapidly proliferating

 Tumors thought to have / ratio ~10

Lower / ratio = more sensitive to dose per fraction
 Slower proliferating

 Most normal tissues thought to have / ratio ~3
 Spinal cord / ratio ~1-2
Prostate cancer has a low / ratio

Estimated / ratio of prostate cancer is ~1.5-3

 So prostate cancer might be harder to kill at conventional dose fractionation

How can we overcome this?

 Dose escalation (conventional fractionation)
 Hypofractionation (exploit the therapeutic ratio)

 Moderate hypofractionation
 Ultra-hypofractionation/SBRT

brachy boost

Phase III RCT � Morris et al., IJROBP 2017

2002-2011: 398 men with intermediate & high-risk prostate cancer

 69% high risk, 31% intermediate risk (most unfavorable intermediate risk)

Randomization:

 DE-EBRT: 46 Gy/23 Fx to prostate, SV, & pelvis --> 32 Gy/16 Fx EBRT to prostate (78 Gy/39 Fx total)

 24 Gy/12 Fx to prostate + 1 mm PTV margin --> 8 Gy/4 Fx to prostate + 5 mm PTV margin

 LDR boost: 46 Gy/23 Fx to prostate, SV, & pelvis --> 115 Gy I-125 LDR prostate brachytherapy boost

 115 Gy = minimum peripheral dose (MPD)

Primary endpoint = bPFS (Phoenix definition: PSA nadir + 2 ng/mL)

brachy boost

Phase III RCT � Morris et al., IJROBP 2017

Results

Castrate-Sensitive vs. Castrate-Resistant

Castrate-Sensitive Prostate Cancer

 Responding to androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)

Castrate-Resistant Prostate Cancer (CRPC)

 State of clinical or biochemical progression in an environment with very low serum testosterone
 Low testosterone (castrate environment) = serum testosterone concentration <50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/dL

 Diagnosis of castrate-resistant prostate cancer is based on rising PSA in the setting of adequately
 suppressed testosterone (<50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/dL)

Rad Bio.Mechanisms of DNA Damage & Repair:
Mechanisms of DNA
Damage & Repair

RIA MULHERKAR, MD
Objectives

DNA damage

 RT induced DNA damage
 Measuring DNA damage
 Proteins involved in detecting damage
 Chromosome & chromatid aberrations

DNA repair

 Mechanisms of DNA repair

 Base excision repair
 Nucleotide excision repair
 Mismatch repair
 Nonhomologous end joining
 Homologous recombination
 Crosslink repair
DNA Damage
Damage induced per 1 Gy of radiation

 1000 single-stranded breaks easily repaired and generally not lethal
 40 double-stranded breaks cell kill via chromosome and chromatid aberrations
Radiation damages DNA along tracks

Radiation damages DNA along the tracks of charged particles

 Electrons in the case of x-rays & -rays
 Protons & -particles in the case of neutrons

Low LET radiation (x-rays, -rays) "spurs" High LET radiation (neutrons) "blobs"

� Contain ~3 ion pairs � Contain ~12 ion pairs
� 100 eV of energy � 100-500 eV of energy
� ~4 nm diameter � ~7 nm diameter

Multiple spurs and/or blobs can occur to create clustered lesions
Measuring DNA Damage

3 Techniques to Know

1. Pulsed-field gel electrophoresis (PFGE)

 Separates DNA fragments based on size
 Can be used to detect DNA damage & repair
 The smaller the fragment, the further they travel on agarose gel

2. Comet assay: single-cell gel electrophoresis

 Can detect DNA damage & repair at single-cell level
 Cells are lysed, and if no DSBs, DNA stays in a clump; if there are

 DSBs, it spreads out like a comet's tail
 To detect DSBs, lysis of cell is performed in an alkaline buffer
 To detect SSBs, lysis of cell is performed in a neutral buffer
Measuring DNA Damage SNoW DRoP

3 Techniques to Know Southern blot = DNA
 Northern blot = RNA
3. DNA damage/radiation-induced foci assay Western blot = Protein

 This assay detects specific proteins that are recruited to
 sites of DNA DSBs

 -H2AX & 53BP1 are common markers

 H2AX is a histone protein rapidly phosphorylated
 after DNA damage to form -H2AX, which is induced
 rapidly on a Western blot

 -H2AX can be detected within 15 minutes of DNA damage

 At 24 hours, any residual -H2AX represents unrepaired DNA damage

 53BP1 is also phosphorylated in response to stress and
 can be used as a marker
What happens when DSB occurs?

 DNA DSB occurs

MRN complex (MRE11/RAD50/NBS1) is recruited to the site

ATM (ataxia-telangiectasia mutated) is recruited & autophosphorylates itself

ATM phosphorylates H2AX -H2AX -H2AX ATM also phosphorylates proteins like CHK2
 serves as platform to recruit repair proteins to halt cell cycle and allow time for repair

53BP1 protein binds & promotes BRCA1 binds & promotes

non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) homologous recombination (HR)
Clinical relevance

Mutations that affect these proteins can lead to various genetic diseases

Gene Disease Symptoms
ATM Ataxia telangiectasia
ATR Seckel syndrome Telangiectasias, neurologic symptoms, RT sensitivity, risk
MRE11 Ataxia telangiectasia-like disorder of cancer (lymphoma, leukemia, breast cancer)
NBS1 Nijmegen break syndrome
 Growth retardation, microcephaly, facial dysmorphism,
 skeletal abnormalities, immune deficiency

 No telangiectasias, but neurologic symptoms & RT
 sensitivity

 Growth retardation, microcephaly, high risk of cancer
 and RT sensitivity
Chromosome & Chromatid Aberrations

Consequence of DNA DSBs
Certain chromosome & chromatid aberrations are lethal result in cell death

Chromosome aberrations � early in Chromatid aberrations � late in
interphase, before cell has replicated interphase, after cell has replicated

Dicentric Ring Anaphase Bridge
DNA Repair
Mechanisms of DNA Repair

Single-stranded Errors in DNA Double-stranded
 break replication break

Base excision Nucleotide excision Mismatch Nonhomologous end Homologous
 repair (MMR)
repair (BER) repair (NER) joining (NHEJ) recombination (HR)

 DNA crosslinks
SSB: Base Excision Repair (BER)

 Removes incorrect bases
 Short patch repair:

 Glycosylase APE1 DNA pol beta DNA
 ligase 3/XRCC1

 Long patch repair:

 PARP/XRCC1 PNK DNA pol delta/epsilon +
 PCNA FEN1 DNA ligase 1

Typically, mutations in BER don't increase radiosensitivity
Exception � XRCC1 mutations lead to slight increase in radiosensitivity

 Thought to be due to role of XRCC1 in other repair processes
SSB: Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER)

 Removes bulky adducts such as pyrimidine dimers
 (caused by UV light)

 Two mechanisms to detect DNA damage

 Global genome repair (GGR)

 Uses XP endocnucleases (XPC, XPE)

 Transcription coupled repair (TCR)

 RNA pol, CSB (ERCC6), CSA (ERCC8)

 Repair processes are identical

 TFIIH XPA, XPG/ERCC5 RPA DNA polymerase
 delta/epsilon

Xeroderma pigmentosum: mutations in xeroderma pigmentosum genes, mainly affects GGR

 Increased photosensitivity to UV light (increased risk of skin cancers)

Cockayne syndrome: mutations in CSB & CSA genes, mainly affects TCR

 Neurologic symptoms, increased photosensitivity but without risk of skin cancers

Trichothiodystrophy: mutations in parts of TFIIH complex, affects both pathways of NER

 Brittle hair and nails, increased photosensitivity but without risk of skin cancers
Mismatch Repair (MMR)

Corrects errors (mismatched bases) that occur during DNA replication

5 steps:

 (1) Mismatch is recognized by Msh2- Msh6 or Msh2-Msh3 complexes (aka. MutS, MutL)
 (2) Recognition complexes recruit MLH1- PMS2, MLH1-PMS1, and MLH1-MLH3
 (3) EXO1 catalyzes excision of incorrect nucleotides
 (4) DNA polymerases fill in the gaps
 (5) Ligation

Lynch syndrome: mutations in MMR genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2)

 Increased risk of many cancers including colorectal, endometrial, gastric, and ovarian cancers
DSB: NHEJ & HR

 DNA DSB occurs

MRN complex (MRE11/RAD50/NBS1) is recruited to the site

ATM (ataxia-telangiectasia mutated) is recruited & autophosphorylates itself

ATM phosphorylates H2AX -H2AX -H2AX ATM also phosphorylates proteins like CHK2
 serves as platform to recruit repair proteins to halt cell cycle and allow time for repair

53BP1 protein binds & promotes BRCA1 binds & promotes

non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) homologous recombination (HR)
DSB: Nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ)

Does not require sequence homology (can occur during G1 before DNA is replicated)

Repairs DSB more efficiently (faster), but less accurate

53BP1 promotes NHEJ over HR

5 steps:

 (1) end recognition by Ku binding � Ku70/80 (XRCC5/6)
 (2) recruitment of DNA-dependent protein kinase catalytic subunit (DNA-PKcs) � DNA PKCs/XRCC7
 (3) end processing � Ku/DNA PKC complex deal with 5' and 3' DNA ends
 (4) fill-in synthesis or end bridging � Artemis endonuclease
 (5) ligation � Ku/DNA/XRCC4/DNA ligase IV complex

SCID (severe combined immunodeficiency): mutations in DNA PKCs

 Radiosensitivity & immunodeficiency (NHEJ involved in VDJ recombination, needed for B and T cell receptors)

LIG4 deficiency: mutations in DNA ligase IV

 Also results in immunodeficiency & radiosensitivity
DSB: Homologous recombination (HR)

Requires sequence homology, occurs in late S and G2 phase after DNA has been replicated

 Requires physical contact with an undamaged chromatid or chromosome to serve as template

Slower than NHEJ but much more accurate Clinical application:
BRCA1 promotes HR
Mediated by proteins including RAD51, RAD52, and BRCA 1/2 PARP inhibitors (e.g., Olaparib) used in
Process includes formation of Holliday junctions which are later resolved BRCA mutant breast cancer. PARP is
 involved in BER, so patients with
 Crossover between damaged strand and template strand
 defects in DSB repair are made further
 susceptible to SSBs by impairing BER.

BRCA1/2 mutations: increased risk of many cancers, including breast, ovarian, prostate, pancreas
Fanconi anemia: errors in crosslink repair, which involves a combination of NER & HR
Crosslink Repair

 DNA crosslinks may be caused by agents like cisplatin or mitomycin C
 Refers to an inappropriate covalent bond between two strands of DNA
 Repair involves both nucleotide excision repair & homologous recombination
 Also involves FANC proteins (e.g., FANCD2)

Fanconi anemia: errors in crosslink repair, which involves a combination of NER & BER

 Pancytopenia, growth delays, cognitive impairment, immunodeficiency, risk of cancers such as leukemia
Cell Cycle & NHEJ vs. HR

NHEJ predominates HR predominates after
before DNA replication
(error-prone) DNA replication

 G1 (greater accuracy)

 radiation in late S/G2

 M
Cell is most sensitive to radiation in M phase

Repair Mechanism Damage Repaired Proteins Involved Resulting Disease Key symptoms

joining DSBs � more accurate MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 Immunodeficiency
Homologous SCID Cancer risk
recombination DNA crosslinks Ku70/80, DNA PKCs, LIG4 syndrome

 Electrons

 Radiation mechanism of action is DNA damage

 Direct DNA damage by ions
 Indirect DNA damage by free radicals (formed when ions interact with molecules such as

 Radiation causes cell kill by causing DNA double-stranded breaks (DSBs)

 DSBs are the hardest for our cells to repair accurately
 Cell is better at repairing DSBs in late S/G2 after DNA replication

 Homologous recombination can occur using replicated DNA as template
 Cell is most radioresistant in late S/G2 phase

 What happens when we fractionate radiation? "4 Rs of Radiobiology"

 Repair
 Repopulation
 Redistribution
 Reoxygenation
OER: Oxygen
Enhancement Ratio
OER: Oxygen Enhancement Ratio

OER = ratio of dose administered in hypoxic conditions to dose administered in aerobic
 conditions to achieve the same biologic effect

The higher the OER, the more oxygen helps with cell kill Oxygen "fixes" or makes
 permanent the damage
OER differs based on type of radiation 2.5-3 caused by free radicals.
 1.6

For oxygen effect to occur, oxygen has to be present
 either during or within microseconds of radiation

 The free radicals that damage DNA have a lifespan

 Increasing oxygen levels further to even 100%

 Maximum OER is seen is at 5% oxygen (30 mm Hg).
 50% OER is seen is 0.5% oxygen (3 mm Hg).
Acute vs. Chronic Hypoxia

Oxygen can only travel ~70-200 �m reaches tumor cells within this radius of blood vessel

Areas of acute hypoxia develop from temporary closing or blockage of a blood vessel

Areas of chronic hypoxia are far away from blood vessels, or due to permanent closing/blockage

 These areas are often necrotic
 Less sensitive to radiation due to hypoxia
 May limit success of radiotherapy

Possible for these cells to undergo reoxygenation

 Acutely hypoxic rapidly reoxygenate
 Chronically hypoxic slowly reoxygenate

Radiosenstizers

Hypoxic radiosensitizers

LET: Linear Energy
Transfer
LET: Linear Energy Transfer

LET = average energy transferred per unit length of a track (kEV/�m)

 
 = 

At microscopic level, energy per unit track length can vary, so 2 ways of calculating the average

 Track average: divide track into equal lengths, and calculate average number of energies deposited in each segment
 Energy average: for every x number of energies deposited, calculate track length, and average the track lengths
LET for different types of radiation

decreases

As LET increases, OER decreases
Approximate OERs

 X-rays & protons: 2.5

RBE: Relative Biological
Effectiveness
RBE: Relative Biological Effectiveness

 = 250
 

RBE = ratio of dose of 250 kV x-rays to dose of X type of radiation needed to cause the same level of damage

RBE depends on a few factors

 Radiation quality (LET)
 Radiation dose
 Number of dose fractions
 Dose rate
 Biologic system or endpoint

Compare x-rays, neutrons, & -particles

 RBE is highest for -particles, and lowest for x-rays
 Dose rate matters the most for x-rays (sparsely ionizing) and matters the least for -particles (densely ionizing)
RBE & Tissue Type

RBE is higher for tissues that accumulate
sublethal damage (dose-response curves with
initial broad shoulder)

 Late-responding tissues have higher RBE � e.g.,
 spinal cord, kidneys, lungs, etc.

 Early-responding tissues like tumor, skin, GI tract
 have lower RBE

Think � what is the dose of 250 kV x-rays
needed to cause the same level of damage?

 More x-ray dose needed in a single dose to cause
 greater damage to late-responding tissues

 Therefore, higher RBE for late-responding tissues
RBE & Fraction Size

RBE is higher with greater accumulation of sublethal damage
RBE decreases with increasing fraction size

Why?

 With smaller dose per fraction, there is more sublethal damage
 Therefore RBE is higher at smaller fraction sizes
RBE & LET

RBE increases with increasing LET up to LET of
100 kEV/�M

 RBE of x-rays < neutrons < -particles (of lower
 energy)

 RBE of protons is 1.1

Beyond 100 kEV/�M, RBE decreases with
increasing LET.

 DNA diameter is 2 �M
 At 100 kEV/�M, distance between ionizations is

 optimized to cause DSB with single track
 Beyond 100 kEV/�M, there are too many extra

 ionizations which are wasted, thus lowering the RBE
RBE increases with LET up to LET of 100 kEV/�M.
 Beyond that, RBE decreases with increasing LET.
RBE & Oxygen

For high LET radiation, RBE can change with tissue oxygenation

 This is because the reference dose for RBE is 250 kV x-rays, which is low LET radiation
 Low LET is subject to OER, whereas high LET is not

In hypoxic cells, RBE would be HIGHER

 Think: more 250 kV X-rays to cause the same effect for hypoxic cells

In aerated cells, RBE would be LOWER

 Think less 250 kV X-rays to cause the same effect when there is oxygen to help

Radiation dose depends on many things

 Total dose

Paraganglioma

Neuroendocrine tumor that develops from nerve cells along blood vessels

 May be linked to genetic conditions (NF1, VHL, MEN2)
 Commonly seen in head & neck region but may occur anywhere

Sympathetic paragangliomas may be associated with elevated catecholamines

 Often located in chest or abdomen (e.g., pheochromocytoma � forms within adrenal
 gland)

 Check urine catecholamines & control prior to treatment (alpha blockers, e.g.,
 doxazosin)

Parasympathetic paragangliomas are often asymptomatic

 Often occur in head & neck adjacent to carotid artery

Imaging features

 Enhancing on CT (highly vascular)
 "Salt & pepper" appearance on MRI (dark vascular flow voids & bright areas of

 hemorrhage)

Manage if enlarging or symptomatic

 RT dose is 45-54 Gy (1.8 Gy/Fx), 12-18 Gy x1, or 25 Gy/5 Fx

If myasthenia gravis anticholinesterase inhibitors to control sx before surgery

Manage with resection first

WHO Type Histology Masaoka-Koga Staging

A Medullary thymoma I Grossly and microscopically completely encapsulated tumor
AB Mixed thymoma
B1 Predominantly cortical thymoma II a Microscopic transcapsular invasion
B2 Cortical thymoma
B3 Well-differentiated thymic carcinoma b Macroscopic invasion into surrounding fatty tissue or grossly adherent to but not breaking through
C Thymic carcinoma mediastinal pleura or pericardium

 III Macroscopic invasion into neighboring organ (e.g., pericardium, great vessels, or lung)

 IV A Pleural or pericardial dissemination

 B Distant metastasis

Indications for PORT: positive surgical margins, stage III disease (macroscopic invasion into
surrounding organs), consider for any thymic carcinoma

RT dosing based on extent of resection

 45-50 Gy for negative margins, 54-60 Gy for positive margins, 60-70 Gy for gross residual
Mesothelioma

Management of mesothelioma is highly controversial

Mainstays of systemic therapy (neoadjuvant and/or adjuvant)

 Cisplatin + pemetrexed � bevacizumab
 Ipilimumab + nivolumab

Select patients may be candidates for surgery

 Historically, epithelioid histology, stage I-IIIA (hemithoracic involvement) if deemed resectable, only
 considered curative for stage IA (T1)

 Pleurectomy/decortication (P/D) preferred over extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP)
 May precede with neoadjuvant chemotherapy
 MARS & recent MARS-2 study suggest worse OS with SGY (EPP or P/D)

Role of radiotherapy is highly controversial

 RT thought to decrease LR after SGY, though no strong data to support
 Adjuvant RT at 1.8 Gy/Fx (dose is 50-54 Gy �SM, 54-60 Gy +SM) � IMRT
 Many are wary of any benefit to adjuvant RT, esp. if gross residual � RT may be best reserved for palliation
Sarcoma � Workup

H&P: thoroughly examine mass and respective draining lymphatic region

Labs: CBC, CMB

Imaging:

 MRI with contrast: usually hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2
 CT chest to rule out metastatic disease
 Role of PET CT is evolving (may be helpful to differentiate malignant vs. benign)

Biopsy:

 Ideally should be done the same surgical/orthopedic oncologist who will operate
 Core needle biopsy; Biopsy tract or open biopsy incision should be longitudinally along the same plane

 as tumor (so scar can be resected at time of surgery)
 For retroperitoneal sarcoma (RPS), CT-guided retroperitoneal biopsy can avoid seeding of peritoneum
Sarcoma � Management

Goal is functional limb preservation via en bloc resection with negative margins (ideally >1 cm)

 If unplanned positive margin postop adjuvant RT (unless given preop)
 Planned close/positive margin may be appropriate in certain circumstances (e.g., close proximity to

 critical structure such as blood vessel, nerve, joint, bone, etc.)

Preop or postop RT to improve local control indicated for stage II-III (grade 2-3)

 Preop dose: 50 Gy/25 Fx

 Alternative: 42.75 Gy/15 Fx as per HYPORT-STS
 Myxoid liposarcoma: 38 Gy/18 Fx as per DOREMY

 Postop dose: 60 Gy/30 Fx

 66 Gy/33 Fx if +margin
 70-76 Gy if gross residual disease

Preop RT generally preferred (higher acute wound complications, but decreased fibrosis/edema,
improved long-term functional outcomes)

Dual Isocenter Technique for RT to SCV

Normal mono-isocentric technique, half-beam block tangents superiorly so no divergence into SCV field
But, if half-beam blocked tangents would not cover the whole breast due to long torso (typically if region of interest is
>20 cm long), or if ipsilateral lung dose exceeds tolerance need dual isocenter
But this technique would cause divergence of beams into the SCV field
-Avoid this by kicking the couch AWAY from the gantry to eliminate divergence of superior tangent field into SCV field for
each of the tangents
-Also have to rotate the collimator for the tangents to match the angle of the chest wall
-Can still use half beam block for SCV

Extremity STS � Local Tx
NCI: Amputation vs. LSS + PORT

Rosenberg et al., Ann Surg 1982

1975-1981: 43 adult patients with high-grade STS of extremities randomized 2:1:

 Limb-sparing surgery (LSS) + post-op RT (PORT) [n=27]

 50 Gy/25 Fx to at risk area, with 60-70 Gy to tumor bed

 Amputation [n=16]

ALL patients received post-op CHT: doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate

4 local recurrences in LSS group, vs. 0 in amputation group

No difference in 5-year DFS or OS between arms:

 5-year DFS: 71% LSS + PORT vs. 78% amputation (p=0.75)
 5-year OS: 83% LSS + PORT vs. 88% amputation (p=0.99)

MVA showed patients with positive margin after resection had significantly higher chance of local
recurrence (p=0.0001)

 NCI Rosenberg Study: Limb-sparing surgery + post-op RT (60-70 Gy) is standard of care.
 (Recurrences can be managed with salvage amputation.)

1994-1997: 190 patients with extremity STS randomized (stratified by size
10 cm vs. >10 cm)

Primary endpoint of wound complications after surgery was WORSE with
preop RT
