Soft Tissue Sarcoma
Overview

RIA M. KRA FT, MD
Sarcoma Benign soft-tissue
 tumors:
Rare, ~1% of malignancies
Heterogenous group of tumors of - Lipoma
mesenchymal origin - Ganglion cyst
� >100 histologic subtypes - Synovial cyst
� ~80% arise from soft tissue - Hemangioma
� ~20% arise from bone - Desmoid tumor
Median age of dx is 45-55 years - Glomus tumor
� ~20% <40 years - Hamartoma
� ~30% 40-60 years - Myoma
� ~50% >60 years - Fibroma
 - Neurofibroma

 Most soft-tissue lesions
 are benign!

 Most common STS:
 - Liposarcoma
 - Leiomyosarcoma
 - Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS)
 - Synovial sarcoma
 - Gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST)
 - Angiosarcoma

 Most common bone sarcoma:
 - Osteosarcoma
 - Chondrosarcoma
 - Ewing sarcoma

Background
Risk Factors

Male gender

Genetic predisposition

 E.g., FAP (Gardner syndrome) � risk factor for desmoid tumors

Prior RT or CHT

 MSKCC reported series of RT induced sarcomas:

 21% osteosarcoma, 16% undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS), 15% angiosarcoma
 Seen after treatment of breast cancer (26%), lymphoma (25%), cervical cancer (14%)
 Median latency 10.3 years

Chemical carcinogens

Chronic irritation or lymphedema
Presentation

Site-dependent presentation
Enlarging, painless mass
New onset edema or paresthesia
LE swelling/DVT

 E.g., retroperitoneal sarcoma

Metastatic disease in 6-10% (higher
risk if deep invasion or high-grade)

Very heterogenous presentation!
Most soft tissue tumors are benign
Workup

H&P: thoroughly examine mass and respective draining lymphatic region

Labs: CBC, CMP

Imaging:

 MRI with contrast: usually hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2
 CT chest to rule out metastatic disease
 Role of PET CT is evolving (may be helpful to differentiate malignant vs. benign)

Biopsy:

 Ideally should be done the same surgical/orthopedic oncologist who will operate
 Core needle biopsy; Biopsy tract or open biopsy incision should be longitudinally along the same plane

 as tumor (so scar can be resected at time of surgery)
 For retroperitoneal sarcoma (RPS), CT-guided retroperitoneal biopsy can avoid seeding of peritoneum
Anatomy

STS arise from mesenchymal cells, can be anywhere in the body

~2/3 occur in extremities

 90% of extremity STS are localized
 Most common histologies in extremities: liposarcoma, UPS, synovial sarcoma, fibrosarcoma

~1/3 occur in retroperitoneum, trunk, or H&N

 Most common histologies: well-differentiated and dedifferentiated LS, leiomyosarcoma, visceral GIST
Appearance on MRI

Example of undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) of right upper medial thigh within
adductor longus muscle (from Radiopaedia)

Axial T1 Axial T1c Axial T2
Pathology Image: classic leiomyosarcoma with area of
 necrosis and inflammatory infiltrate

 Liposarcoma

Treatment Paradigm
Summary of Surgery
Management
 � Goal is functional limb preservation via en bloc resection with negative margins
 (ideally >1 cm)

 �If unplanned positive margin postop adjuvant RT (if preop RT, +/- postop boost)
 � Planned close/positive margin may be appropriate in certain circumstances (e.g.,

 close proximity to critical structure such as blood vessel, nerve, joint, bone, etc.)

 Radiotherapy

 � Preop or postop RT to improve local control indicated for stage II-III (grade 2-3)
 �Preop dose: 50 Gy/25 Fx

 �Alternative: 42.75 Gy/15 Fx as per HYPORT-STS
 �Myxoid liposarcoma: 38 Gy/18 Fx as per DOREMY
 �Postop dose: 60 Gy/30 Fx
 �66 Gy/33 Fx if +margin
 � 70-76 Gy if gross residual disease

 Systemic therapy

 �For locally advanced/unresectable try to convert to resectable with
 neoadjuvant systemic therapy + RT

 � Anthracycline based regimen, e.g., AIM (Adriamycin, ifosfamide, mesna)
 � Emerging evidence for pembrolizumab (esp. UPS or dedifferentiated LS)
Surgery

Core needle biopsy (ideally done by surgical/orthopedic oncologist)

 Biopsy tract should avoid potential contamination of uninvolved compartments
 Biopsy tract should ideally be in line with future surgical resection

Goal of surgery = functional limb preservation

 Goal is to resect tumor en bloc with clear margins (at least 1 cm), dissecting along normal tissue planes
 that are uncontaminated by tumor

What if margins are not clear?

 If unplanned excision with positive margin reexcise positive margin, and if cannot, do adjuvant RT
 If planned positive margin (e.g., adjacent to blood vessel or nerve) adjuvant RT (unless done preop)
 Exception: retroperitoneal/intra-abdominal sarcoma with positive margin--re-resection and/or

 adjuvant RT NOT considered (for RP sarcoma, strongly favor preop RT)
Preop vs. Postop RT

 Pre-op RT Post-op RT

Pros - Lower dose (50 Gy) - Lower post-op wound
 - Smaller treatment volume complication rate (~17%)
 - Lower late complications

 (fibrosis, edema, stiffness)

Cons - Higher post-op wound - Higher dose (60-70 Gy)
 - Larger treatment volume
 complication rate (~35%) - Worse long-term functional

 outcomes

 - Equivalent local control for pre-op vs. post-op RT (85-95%)

ASTRO 2021 Guidelines (Salerno et al., 2021)

 Pre-op RT strongly favored over post-op RT for STS of trunk, extremities, & retroperitoneum
Radiotherapy for STS

Preop RT Dosing Volumes (Trunk & Exremities) RPS volumes:
 - GTV iGTV
 50 Gy/25 Fx or 50.4 Gy/28 Fx (RP) - GTV = gross tumor - 1.5 cm CTV
 Alternatives: - CTV = 1.5 cm radial, 3-4 cm sup/inf
 - 42.75 Gy/15 Fx margin
 - 36 Gy/18 Fx (myxoid liposarcoma) - 0.5-1 cm into uninvolved muscle
 - Include peritumoral edema, biopsy tract
Postop RT 60 Gy/30 Fx - PTV = ~0.5 cm 50 Gy
 66 Gy/33 Fx (positive margin) - Can trim PTVeval 0.3-0.5 cm from skin
 70-80 Gy (gross residual)
 - GTV = tumor bed (surgical clips, preop MRI)
 - CTV1 = 1.5 cm radial, 3-4 cm sup/inf

 - 0.5-1 cm into uninvolved muscle
 - CTV2 = 1.5 cm radial, 1.5-2 cm sup/inf

 - 0.5-1 cm into uninvolved muscle
 - PTV = ~0.5 cm: PTV1 50 Gy; PTV2 60-66 Gy

 - Can trim PTVeval 0.3-0.5 cm from skin
Common Dose Constraints for Extremity
STS

Long bone

 Mean < 37 Gy
 0.03 cc < 59 Gy
 V40 Gy < 64%
 Avoid circumferential coverage of bone with 50 Gy IDL

RTOG 0630 Constraints

 V20 Gy <50% for a 2 cm skin strip
 V50 Gy <50% for a weight-bearing bone
Systemic Therapy

Conflicting data regarding benefit of chemotherapy, especially in adjuvant setting

Can consider RT + systemic therapy for locally advanced/unresectable or metastatic disease

 Not amenable upfront to surgery may be able to convert to resectable with RT + systemic therapy
 Disease that is high-risk for metastasis
 Metastatic disease

Systemic therapy regimens:

 Highly variable; NCCN guidelines have comprehensive histology-specific recommendations
 Common regimens:

 AIM (doxorubicin/ifosfomide/mesna)
 Epirubicin/ifosfomide/mesna

 Emerging paradigm: RT + pembrolizumab adjuvant pembrolizumab

 UPS, myxofibrosarcoma, dedifferentiated liposarcoma, pleomorphic liposarcoma
Management of STS

STS Trunk & Extremities General Treatment Paradigm

 Grade 1: surgery alone
 Grade 2-3: RT + SGY

STS Retroperitoneum SGY (consider preop RT for liposarcoma histology)

Malignant Phyllodes SGY (lumpectomy with 1 cm margin, axillary staging not required) adjuvant RT 50-60
Tumor of Breast Gy regardless of margin status
 (Note: if mastectomy, then consider adjuvant RT for high grade, large size, recurrent dz)

Desmoid Tumors Active surveillance until progression or symptoms systemic therapy favored
(Aggressive Fibromatosis) (Nirogacestat or Sorafenib) due to high rates of recurrence postop; exception -
 abdominal wall lesions have lower recurrence after SGY
 If progress on systemic therapy SGY (favored) or definitive RT (56 Gy/28 Fx)

 � Can consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy

 � BCC: SHH inhibitor (vismodegib, sonidegib)

 5-FU, carboplatin/paclitaxel, EGFR inhibitors like cetuximab)

Outcomes For low-risk, early-stage non-melanoma
 skin cancers, EBT has equivalent local
 Local control at mean 3.5 years: 99.5% EBT vs. 100% control & cosmetic outcomes to Mohs.

Adjuvant RT Evidence
Regional recurrence risk factors

Brantsch et al., Lancet Oncol 2008

Prospective, multivariable analysis of risk factors
was performed for regional recurrence after
margin-negative excision of cSCC in 615 patients

Tumor thickness was most strongly associated
with recurrence, especially thickness >6mm

Elective RT for patients at high-risk of N+ disease,
defined as thickness >6mm, is conditionally
recommended only for those who undergo
primary RT with overlap of adjacent nodal basins

 Can also consider SLNB to guide need for RT
Retrospective Study: Resection +/- RT

Harris et al., JAMA H&N 2018

2008-2016: retrospective study of 349 patients
treated with surgery +/- adjuvant RT

Larger tumors, LN+, poorly differentiated,
+PNI, and younger immunosuppressed
patients more likely to receive adjuvant RT

Adjuvant RT improved OS in entire cohort (HR,
0.59; 95% CI, 0.38-0.90)

Subset analysis showed RT improved DFS & OS
for the following:

 LN+
 PNI

Retrospective study shows improved OS & DFS
with adjuvant RT if high-risk disease (esp. LN+
and PNI)
Retrospective Matched-Pair Study

Ruiz et al., J Am Acad Derm 2022

20-year retrospective review of 508 cSCC tumors (BWH T2b-T3) treated with surgery (negative
margin resection) +/- adjuvant RT

Adjuvant RT decreased 5-year cumulative incidence of LRR

 5-year LR 4% with adjuvant RT vs. 5% without
 5-year LRR 8% with adjuvant RT vs. 15% without

Adjuvant RT especially decreased 5-year cumulative incidence of LRR in high-risk subgroup
(defined as T3, recurrent, or diameter 6 cm)

 5-year LRR 17% (95% CI, 11.9%-26.4%) with adjuvant RT vs 31% (95% CI, 26.1%-40.8%) without

Adjuvant RT after margin-negative resection for high-risk cSCC ~halves the risk of LR & LRR.
Adjuvant Radiotherapy

ASTRO Clinical Practice Guidelines: Likhacheva et al., PRO 2020

 STRONG recommendation for cSCC with:

 Gross PNI that is clinically or radiographically apparent
 Close or positive margins that cannot be corrected with further surgery
 Recurrence after a prior margin negative resection
 T3-T4 tumors
 Desmoplastic or infiltrative tumors in setting of chronic immunosuppression

 CONDITIONAL recommendation BCC with:

 Close or positive margins that cannot be corrected with further surgery
 Recurrence after prior margin negative resection
 Locally advanced or neglected tumors involving bone or infiltrating into muscle
Elective Nodal Radiation

ASTRO Clinical Practice Guidelines: Likhacheva et al., PRO 2020

 STRONG recommendations:

 For cSCC or BCC that metastasized to clinically apparent regional lymph nodes, therapeutic lymphadenectomy
 followed by adjuvant RT is recommended, with EXCEPTION of single <3 cm cervical LN involvement without
 ECE

 For cSCC or BCC that metastasized to clinically apparent regional LNs, definitive RT only recommended for
 patients who are medically inoperable or surgically unresectable

 For cSCC or BCC undergoing adjuvant RT after therapeutic lymphadenectomy, dose of 60-66 Gy (1.8-2 Gy/Fx)
 is recommended

 For patients undergoing elective RT in absence of lymphadenectomy, a dose of 50-54 Gy (1.8-2 Gy/Fx) is
 recommended

 CONDITIONAL recommendations:

 For patients with cSCC at high risk of nodal metastasis, imaging & sentinel lymph node biopsy to guide the
 need for and target lymph node basin RT

 For patients with cSCC at high risk of regional nodal metastasis (thickness >6 mm), elective lymph node basin
 RT for those undergoing RT to primary site with overlap of adjacent nodal basin

Evidence for PD-1 Inhibitors in cSCC

Gross et al., NEJM 2022 � Phase 2 trial of neoadjuvant cemiplimab up to 4 doses prior to curative
intent surgery for resectable stage II-IV cSCC

 79 patients enrolled, primary endpoint: CR in 40 patients, for a CR rate of 51% (95% CI 39-62)
 Pathological major response (10% or less viable tumor) in 13% (95% CI 6-22), objective response on imaging in

 68% (95% CI 57-68)
 G3+ adverse events in 18%, with 4 Grade 5 events of which 3 believed to be unrelated to cemiplimab

 87% experienced any grade event with most common being fatigue, diarrhea, nausea, rash

Migden et al., NEJM 2018 � Phase 1/2 trial of cemiplimab in setting of locally advanced or metastatic
cSCC

 Overall response rate 46% and disease control rate 69% with median f/u of 7 months
 First systemic tx approved by FDA for metastatic or locally advanced cutaneous SCC not amenable to surgery

KEYNOTE-629: Grobb et al., JCO 2020 � Phase 2 trial of pembrolizumab for recurrent/metastatic cSCC

 Objective response rate 34% & disease control 52%
 Second PD-1 inhibitor to be approved by FDA for advanced cutaneous SCC
Evidence for SHH Inhibitors in BCC

ERIVANCE Study Sekulic et al., BMC Cancer 2017 � Phase 2 trial of 104 pts with
metastatic or locally advanced BCC

 ORR 48.5% in metastatic patients and 60.3% in locally advanced patients
 No pCR in metastatic pts while 30% pCR rate in locally advanced patients
 Median response duration of about 13 months
 Primary resistance where 50% of patients have no response
 About 30% can develop secondary resistance and regrowth
 G3+ events in 56% of patients � weight loss, muscle spasms

Workup

Complete history & physical

 Prior history of skin cancer, prior treatments, history of RT, etc.
 Complete skin exam � investigate for skip lesions and examine regional nodes
 For tumors of H&N � neurological exam to rule out perineural invasion (PNI)

Pathology

 Punch, shave, excisional, or incisional biopsy
 SLNB for at risk melanoma (Breslow depth >0.8 mm, ulceration, or risk factors such as age 42 yrs)

 Molecular testing � check for BRAF V600 mutation!

 SLNB always for Merkel cell carcinoma

Imaging

 If suspect PNI or tumor of skull base, periorbital structures, lesions fixed to underlying muscle/bone/fascia 
 MRI with and without contrast

 CT with contrast if locally advanced tumor or suspect regional lymphadenopathy
 FDG-PET/CT for high risk or node-positive patients to rule out metastatic disease

Paradigms
Summary of Management

Cancer of Skin General Management RT Doses

Basal Cell Low-risk: excision or alternate focal therapy 60-70 Gy/30-35 Fx } prefer protracted courses near critical
carcinoma High-risk: resection adjuvant therapy 50-55 Gy/17-20 Fx OARs or in cosmetically sensitive areas
 Advanced/metastatic: SHH Inhibitor (vismodegib, sonidegib) 40-44 Gy/10 Fx
Cutaneous 40 Gy/5 Fx (twice weekly)
Squamous Cell Low-risk: excision or alternate focal therapy 30 Gy/3 Fx (once weekly)
carcinoma High-risk: resection adjuvant therapy 20-25 Gy/1 Fx (small volumes only!)
Cutaneous Advanced/metastatic: Immunotherapy (cemiplimab,
Melanoma pembrolizumab) BED10 60-79 Gy (conventional) or 56-70 Gy (hypofx)

Merkel Cell Neoadjuvant IO (immune checkpoint inhibitors) If used � 48 Gy/20 Fx (consider 51 Gy/21 Fx for positive margins)
Carcinoma WLE + SLNB & completion dissection if (+) � need >10 LNs in 30 Gy/5 Fx over 2.5 weeks
 groin and >15 LNs in axilla/H&N 50 Gy/20 Fx commonly used for lentigo maligna

 Surgical resection (WLE) + SLNB (controversial in H&N locations 50-56 Gy/25-28 Fx if negative margins

 d/t c/f accuracy) 56-60 Gy/28-30 Fx if microscopic positive margins

 No systemic therapy 60-66 Gy/30-33 Fx if gross residual or definitve RT

 No RT to LN if negative SLNB or full dissection with 1 +LN
 50-56 Gy if microscopic node positive; 56-60 Gy if ECE

Radiation Therapy

Definitive RT: recommended for BCC & cSCC that cannot undergo surgical resection

 RT contraindicated for patients with certain genetic conditions, e.g., basal cell nevus syndrome
 RT relatively contraindicated for patients with connective tissue disease, e.g., scleroderma
 May be used for melanoma in situ or lentigo maligna if unresectable
 Definitive or palliative RT for mycosis fungoides

Postoperative RT: recommended for high-risk cases

 BCC: gross PNI (clinically or radiographically evident)
 cSCC: gross PNI, close/positive margins (cannot be corrected with SGY), T3-4 tumors, desmoplastic or

 infiltrative tumors in setting of chronic immunosuppression
 Melanoma: considered for select cases of high-risk desmoplastic melanoma
 Merkel cell: always recommended, start <7-8 weeks post-surgery (as soon as healed)
